Later side extension and alterations to original extension and outbuildings on road front. Original windows, particularly on road front, replaced and flat roofed dormer windows added. Two storey canted window bay overlooking garden with hipped plain tile roof. Brick band course at first floor level with dentillation detail plus corbelled brick course at attic level. Inset brickwork panels on garden front. Large areas of facing brickwork to ground floor storey now rendered and painted. Stone lintels and sills to original openings. A number of original window openings altered including removal of stone lintels and sills. Surviving stone details now painted. Plain tile roof with overhanging eaves. Bargeboards to side gable with wood tie member. Brick side wall chimney stack at central ridge position on gable. Single gable with bargeboards overlooking garden above canted window bay. Originally, red brick chimney stacks with string courses and corbelled caps. Side wall stacks now altered or partly removed. Original pots removed. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. House adversely affected by removal of original windows and alterations to original openings, particularly on road front. Brick boundary wall adjacent to Cavendish Crescent South with saddleback copings. Original outbuildings in former yard area adjacent to road front. Former conservatory/porch adjacent to entrance on side of house, now demolished.
history: One of a pair of houses (with No.5) c1875. Now converted into flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic. Red brick with ashlar dressings and gabled plain tile roof. Original yard, stable and carriage buildings at lower end of garden adjacent to Lenton Road now altered and extended as separate residential development c1970-80. Flat roofed garage building added, early 20c, on road front replacing earlier outbuildings. Tudor arched doorway to detached entrance porch on road front with gabled and hipped plain tile roof. Later adjacent doorway. Other alterations and additions on road front facade include enlarged window openings, later window openings plus glazed porch with lean-to roof over central doorway. Single storey canted and rectangular window bays on garden front with lean-to canopy across facade at first floor level. Glazed link between entrance porch on road front and main house. Decorative brick band at first floor level with combination of plinth and string courses plus dentils. Also, stepped brickwork in shallow relief at gable verges behind timber bargeboards. Ashlar surrounds to a number of door and window openings, also separate lintels and sills plus copings and finials. Much of the stonework remains unpainted. Plain tile roof with gables and timber bargeboards to all facades. Red brick chimney stacks with string courses, dentils and corbelled caps. Some stacks altered or partly removed. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Cross windows with glazing bar casements to a number of original openings plus replacements, alterations or additions. Panelled porch entrance door on road front with Tudor arched head. Surviving section of original red brick boundary wall adjacent to Cavendish Crescent South with plinth and later copings. Later central gate opening. Part of original wall altered or replaced with later addition of garage building.
history: Detached house c1875, possibly by builder James Wright. Part of house converted into separate flat.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Red brick with ashlar dressings and hipped and gabled plain tile roof. Later alteration and enlargement of main entrance porch adjacent to South Road. Part of original lower garden area including outbuilding adjacent to Lenton Road developed for separate residential use c1977-80.Original single storey, pitched roof outbuilding on road front adjacent to Cavendish Crescent South now altered and converted for garage use. Single storey pitched roof porch on same front. Single and two storey canted window bays with flat roofs on garden front. Stepped brick relief patterning at gables with brick banding and dentillation detail at first and second floor levels. Splay brick reveals to a number of openings. Stonework features include full surrounds with mullions and transoms to a number of openings particularly main entrance, garden front and staircase window on east facade. Elsewhere, separate stone lintels and sills. Plain tile roof with hips plus prominent pair of gables on garden front. Pair of gabled dormers facing Cavendish Crescent South and single gabled dormer above main entrance. Overhanging bargeboards to gables and gabled dormers with carved trefoil end detail. Flat roofed dormer windows added. Clay ridge and hip tiles with lead valleys. Red brick side wall and ridge chimney stacks with decorative brick banding at caps. One original stack, other stacks partially rebuilt or rendered. Original pots replaced. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Casement windows generally with a number of alterations and additions. Original red brick boundary wall with saddleback copings and coursed rubble stone plinth. Part of wall now rendered and painted. New openings formed in boundary wall adjacent to Cavendish Crescent South.
history: Large detached house c1875, by architect Robert Evans for himself. Now converted into flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic. Red brick with ashlar dressings and gabled slate roof. Coach house/stable building late 19c, added in part of lower garden area with access from Lenton Road, now converted for separate residential use. Single storey, flat roofed bow window bay added to dining room by J.E. Walton for Arthur Brownsword c1934. Also, new entrance gate opening on main road front c1934. Alterations, additions and conversion of outbuildings into garage and flat by Evans, Cartwright & Woollatt architects for Wilford Dunn 1960. Also, early 21c alterations. Half glazed and panelled main entrance door with moulded stone surround, round arched head and side shafts. Main entrance doorway flanked by single fixed lights with round arched heads and moulded stone surrounds. Double gabled road front with plain bargeboards. Three single window openings at ground and first floor levels, including later window bay, plus single round arched window opening to each gable. Double gabled garden front with plain bargeboards. Central doorway flanked by single storey, flat roofed canted window bays. Three single window openings at first floor level with single round arched window to each gable. Later extensions and alterations obscure original ground floor openings on east facing facade. Round arched single original window at first floor level. Decorative buff brick banding with recessed square panels to facades at eaves and first floor level and to canted bay parapets. Brick plinths to canted bays now rendered and brick skin added to base of main entrance facade and road front. Stonework features include door and window surrounds, some with moulded hood and drip moulds, corniced head and plain sill bands to canted window bays plus plinths and copings. Slate roof with twin gables and bargeboards on road and garden fronts. Roof windows added. Red brick chimney stacks with altered copings. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Mainly original glazing bar sash windows generally. Stepped garden with paved terrace adjacent to house, now extended, and stone side steps leading down to lawn area enclosed by trees and shrubs. Altered brick boundary wall with decorative banding below copings. Small original outbuildings now replaced by garage and other additions.
history: Early detached house c1860, possibly by architect T.C. Hine. In single household use.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic. Red brick with ashlar dressings and hipped slate roof. Coach house and stable buildings adjacent to Lenton Road, late 19c/early 20c, now altered and converted for separate residential use. Large part of original garden at side of house lost to separate residential developments c1970-80. Garage building added in entrance forecourt area. Prominent doorway on main entrance front with moulded brick pilasters, shafts and stone dressings plus decorated stone pointed arch head. Above at first floor level, large stone framed hall window with leaded lights and stained glass. Two storey pointed window bay on garden front plus central gabled dormer window. To left, square corner door/ window bay with slate pyramidal roof. Late 20c conservatory added to garden front. Two storey canted window bay on west facing facade. Prominent pair of brick dormer windows on road front with stone pediments, flanked by stone pedestals with urns. Below at ground level, pair of single storey brick outbuildings, now partly rendered. Various brickwork details include shafts with moulded plinths and capitals at door and window reveals, ornamentation at main entrance doorway plus blue brick decorative banding. Stonework features include lintels and sills generally, mullioned windows, dormer window dressings, steps etc. Hipped slate roof, with bay roofs and gabled dormers. Prominent curved wooden bracket detail at projecting eaves. Red brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning, decorative stone panels and corbelled brick caps. A number of original chimney pots are missing or replaced. Some original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Plain sash windows to original openings generally. Panelled, double leaf main entrance door. A number of alterations and additions to door and window openings. Part of original brick boundary wall with gate opening between outbuildings on road front removed and replaced with railings. Other sections of wall have been rendered. Brick piers with moulded stone caps to forecourt entrance.
history: Detached house c1870, now converted into two flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic. Red brick with ashlar dressings and gabled slate roof. Lower part of original terraced garden area adjacent to Fiennes Crescent replaced by development of four terraced townhouses c1970-80. Originally, side entrance on east facing facade through later conservatory. Single storey canted bay with hipped lead roof on west facing facade. Canted and rectangular window bays on garden front. Symmetrical triple gabled road front with combination of single, double and triple window openings with stone surrounds. Also doorway in central bay. Twin gabled garden front plus single gable to each side. Corbelled and splay brick detail at eaves and gable verges. Full ashlar surrounds to door and window openings with cusp detailing at a number of round and pointed arch heads. Also, splay detail to reveals. Stone details to parts of eaves and gable kneelers. Much of the stonework remains unpainted. Slate roof with prominent gables on all fronts plus gable dormers. Imposing red brick ridge and side wall chimney stacks with plinths, brick relief patterning, string courses and corbelled caps. Buff crown chimney pots. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Plain sash windows to original openings generally. Panelled doorway on road front with pointed arch glazed apertures. Small original outbuildings in forecourt area on road front now removed and red brick boundary wall with gated openings altered. Original brick and stone boundary wall with central gated opening adjacent to Fiennes Crescent replaced by later residential development.
history: Detached house c1858, possibly by architects Hine & Evans. One of first houses in The Park to be built away from periphery of estate. Also known as Amelia House. Early occupant of house was A.J.Mundella (1825-97), Liberal MP and prominent educationalist who served as cabinet minister in successive governments under W.E. Gladstone. House now converted into flats.

desc: One of first houses in The Park to be built away from periphery of estate. Two storeys plus basement and attic storey. Red brick with ashlar dressings and hipped and gabled slate roof. Lower part of original terraced garden area, including coach house/stable building adjacent to Fiennes Crescent, replaced by development of four terraced townhouses c1970-80. Flat roofed garage building and hipped roof glazed porch added to road front, 20c. Garage replaced part of earlier outbuilding. Originally, glazed passageway with round arched brick entrance at side of house, now altered and partly removed. Twin gabled road front with hipped roof side extension. Canted window bay on garden front with gable above. Decorative brick banding at eaves with square inset panels and buff brick. Bull nosed detail at eaves in hipped roof side extension. Stone surrounds to door and window openings including a number with round arched heads and hoodmoulds. Stonework now painted. Slate roof with overhanging eaves and projecting verges at gables supported on ornamental brackets. Red brick chimney stacks with buff decorative banding, red brick corbelled caps and moulded stone copings. A number of stacks altered or partially rebuilt. Buff crown chimney pots. Flat roofed dormer window added. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Mainly glazing bar sash windows to original openings with some alterations. Also, later window openings formed. Original brick boundary wall and panelled gate piers with stone copings on road front, now roughcast rendered and painted. Part of wall replaced by later garage building. Coach house and original boundary wall with opening on to Fiennes Crescent now replaced by later residential development.
history: Detached house c1858, possibly by architects Hine & Evans. Also known as Albert Villa. Now converted into flats.

desc: Flat roofed single storey side extension possibly by architects Heazell & Sons c1930 for F. Arthur Wadsworth. Coach house at bottom of garden adjacent to Fiennes Crescent boundary converted into separate dwelling c1992. Detached garage building added in entrance forecourt area c1994. Two storey house plus basement and attic. Red brick with stone dressings and hipped slate roof. Pair of two storey canted stone window bays on garden front with 3 segment arched window openings to each bay at ground floor level and 3 flat headed openings at first floor level. Moulded stone surrounds to doors and windows generally plus moulded stone cornice band and string course at first floor level on all facades except road front. Also, moulded stone string course at eaves level on all facades. Flat headed moulded surround with pilasters to main entrance porch with monogrammed datestone. Stonework now painted. Glazing bar sash windows on road front with mainly plain sashes elsewhere. Remnant of earlier glazed roof to passageway between house and smaller gated opening on road front. Hipped slate roof with projecting eaves. Clay ridge and hip tiles with lead roll flashings to bay hips. Dormer window and roof windows added. Red brick side wall and ridge chimney stacks with chamfered stone plinth course, separated flues and corbelled caps. A number of original buff ‘crown’ pots are missing. Red brick boundary wall adjacent to Cavendish Crescent South with chamfered blue brick plinth course and stone copings. Original brick gate piers to main entrance forecourt with inscribed stone band course plus moulded stone string course and cap. To right of original smaller gated opening, later vehicular opening with brick and stone gate piers similar to original entrance. Garden area terraced to suit sloping site.
history: Detached house built 1876 for James Cloak of James Cloak & Co., wholesale grocer, with offices at Friar Lane, Nottingham. House also known as Holyrood House. Now converted for use by two households.

desc: Two storeys plus attic and basement. Red brick with stone dressings and hipped and gabled slate roof. Garages added, replacing part of boundary wall adjacent to Cavendish Crescent South. Single storey flat roofed, rectangular and pointed window bays on main garden front. To right, entrance facade with single storey square porch. Full height canted window bay on west facing facade plus projecting gabled wing and two storey canted bay on main road front. Profusion of decorative facade detailing including banded brick and stone round arched surround to main entrance doorway with tracery overlight and ogee arched stone hoodmould. Plain and moulded stone surrounds to door and window openings generally plus stone head and sill bands, string courses, hoodmoulds and copings. Decorative brickwork features include corbelled brackets to eaves with nailhead mouldings, projecting band course at first floor level and gables with sawtooth patterning, plus linear arrangement of inset eight pointed star shaped panels above window heads on bays to garden front. Blue brick banding generally. Slate hipped roof with gables. Clay ridge and hip tiles plus iron cresting to main roof. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Roof windows added. Red brick chimney stacks with string course and sawtooth detail at heads. Plain sash windows generally with some alterations and additions. Large timber framed stair window on road front with segmental head and leaded glazing. Double main entrance doors. Prominent brick boundary wall with nailhead mouldings and half round copings. Brick piers with stone caps to gated entrance on Cavendish Crescent South plus adjacent railings on brick plinth with stone copings. Garden area terraced to suit sloping site with garden gate opening to Fiennes Crescent.
history: Detached house c1876, possibly by architect Watson Fothergill. In single household use. House located on prominent corner plot adjacent to Cavendish Crescent South, Hardwick Road and Fiennes Crescent. Built for B. Cleaver, a Nottingham weaver and later occupied by Miss Ada Jessop of the Jessops department store family.

desc: Plan by Perkin and Newbold for garage c1910-20. Existing garage may be alteration or subsequent addition. Plan by Warmington & Co. for alterations c1958. (Possibly to convert the original house into two dwellings) Almost all of original garden area lost to residential development c1980 including six seperate dwellings. Two storeys and basement with original single storey extension adjacent to Cavendish Road East. Projecting gable wing at rear with single storey rectangular window bay. Adjacent two storey canted window bay with hipped plain tile roof. Red brick facades with ashlar dressings. Brick details include decorative band of projecting stretchers and headers at storey height level plus string courses and moulded brick surrounds to window openings. Diaper patterns on facade. Circular ornamental terracotta air vent on gable with eight pointed star motif. Unpainted moulded stone lintels and sills to main window openings. Double pitched, plain tile roof with gables plus hipped roof to canted bay. Hipped plain tile roof to single storey front extension. Clay ridge tiles with broken or missing terracotta finials. Bargeboards to gables. Alterations to existing chimney stack and pots removed. Other stacks have been taken down. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Casement windows generally. some with leaded lights plus a number of alterations. Origianl terraced garden now seperately developed, distorting setting of original house plus consequent loss of original brick and stone boundary wall enclosures to Tunnel Road and Tattershall Drive. Parts of original boundary walls and gate piers adjacent to house remain but altered.
history: Detached house c1890-95, possibly by architects Hine & Son. Now split into two households, one of which is named Cavendish Court with access via original entrance on Tunnel Road. Original house formerly known as ‘Allandale’.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Red brick with ashlar dressings, tile hanging and gabled plain tile roof. Substantial part of main garden area separately developed for three ‘townhouses’ c1975 with access from Tattershall Drive. Gabled wing to left side of road front with central chimney breast and stack. To right, entrance porch and singe storey bow window bay with plain tile roof. Small gable at roof level above bow window with decorative tile hanging. Gabled overhang at attic level to right of road front with moulded wooden soffit and brackets, bargeboards and decorative tile hanging. Later window opening formed in gable. Below overhanging gable, canted oriel window at first floor level with shaped brackets plus moulded base and head. Singe storey extension and outbuildings in yard area on left side of road front with later opening to gabled section adjacent to boundary. Some areas of brickwork in yard area now painted. Moulded brick string courses generally plus blue brick banding and plinth stretchers. Gabled wing to right side of garden front with tile hung attic storey, projecting roof with bargeboards and shaped brackets. Prominent oriel window spanning first and second floor levels with shaped brackets plus moulded base, frame and corniced heads. Pargetting to panel under bay windows at attic level. Brick and stone mullioned window at ground floor level with stone sills, transoms and lintels. To left, open timber framed porch with plain tile lean-to roof. Ashlar surround to staircase window at first floor level above porch, with leaded glazing. To left again, two storey canted window bay with hipped plain tile roof and iron finial. Brick and stone surrounds to canted bay windows at ground floor level with decorative tile hanging between first floor windows. Plain tile roof with mainly tile hung gables. Shaped ridge tiles and bonnet hip tiles. Pargetting to pediment roof panel on road front and to dormer window facing former garden area. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Prominent red brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning, string courses and decorative panels. Mainly casement and fixed light windows with some plain and glazing bar sashes. Leaded glazing to a number of windows. Partly altered red brick boundary wall on road front plus gate piers with moulded brick and stone caps. Remnants of stone balustrading to original terrace and retaining wall on garden front.
history: Large detached house c1885, now converted into flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic. Red brick with ashlar dressings and plain tile roof with gables and hips. Garage building added, probably by Frank Goulding Ltd c1937, replacing section of original boundary wall adjacent to Tattershall Drive. Single storey flat roofed extension in former yard area on main road front, c1965. Moulded stone surround with segment arched head to panelled double leaf main entrance door and overlight on south facing facade. Two storey polygonal window bay at left corner of garden front. To right, two storey rectangular window bay with part timber framed and bargeboarded gable above. On road front, projecting gabled wing to left, central 3 window bay with hipped roof attic storey above plus rectangular window bay to right with bargeboarded gable above. Blue brick bands and moulded brick string courses to all facades plus moulded brick reveals to a number of window openings. Stonework details include moulded surrounds to a number of door and window openings plus separate lintels and sills. Composite plain tile roof with gables, gablet, gable dormers plus hipped and conical roofs to bays. Moulded wood bargeboards to gables, gablet and gabled dormers. Decorative timber framing to upper part of gables. Decorative ridge tiles and terracotta finials. Red brick side wall chimney stacks with corbelled brick caps. Some original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Casement windows generally. Also, fixed lights and leaded lights. A number of alterations and additions to doors and windows. Original boundary wall, gate piers and gates adjacent to Cavendish Road East replaced by later flat roof extension and enlarged drive opening. Terraced garden area with coursed rubble stone retaining walls and railings. Original boundary wall adjacent to Tattershall Drive with coursed rubble stone plinth and partially rebuilt brickwork above.
history: Detached house c1895, now residence of the Bishop of Nottingham.

desc: Single storey extension with double pitched plain tile roof added to original outbuildings in front yard area c1920-30, with lean-to addition abutting main house in the same area and converted for (garage?) use. Original conservatory facing garden has been replaced. Part of original carriage and stable building at bottom of garden adjacent to Tattershall Drive converted into separate dwelling with further dwelling added c1980. Two storey original house with small basement. Red brick facades with ashlar dressings. Prominent two storey polygonal window bay with spire roof at corner of garden facing facade. Also, two storey rectangular window bay with hipped roof on same facade. Two storey canted window bay on side facade adjacent to main entrance doorway. Plain brick facades with moulded brick string courses and some door and window surrounds. Moulded stone details at windows with complete stone surrounds to majority of openings, including mullions and transomes. Separate lintels and sills to a number of mainly first floor windows. Stonework now painted. Complex plain tile roof with double pitches, hips, spire and projecting bargeboards at gables. Half-timbering detail to top of facade at gables plus decorative motif to gable panel on outbuilding adjacent to road. Tall red brick chimney stacks with vertical indentation, splay bricks, string courses, corbelling and shaped caps. A number of original pots are missing or have been replaced. Stone plinth detail at base of stack at roof level. Casement windows generally. Some alterations. Leaded windows with stained glass to many top lights and to large staircase/hall window. Double doors to main entrance with overlight and moulded brick segmental arched head above. Each main entrance door leaf has five panels. Stepped garden, as original, with terrace and balustraded stone retaining wall. Original drive linking Cavendish Road East with gated opening on Tattershall Drive. Original red brick and plain tile outbuildings form large part of Cavendish Road East boundary together with original gate piers. Small section of original brick boundary wall survives following later additions.
history: Detached house c1895, also known as Gartree House. In single household use.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Red brick with stone dressings and gabled plain tile roof. Part of original lower garden area adjacent to Tattershall Drive developed as two separate dwellings c1970-80. Original glazed porch to main entrance removed and replaced with tiled and boarded pitched roof canopy plus extended brick pier. Possible single storey, glazed lean to extension on north facing facade removed and raised drive formed to create car access from Cavendish Road East. Drive extended c1985-86 into garden area with part of basement on garden front converted for garage use. Single storey gabled roof extensions on road front with main body of house behind. Gabled entrance front with two oriel windows at first floor level plus polygonal corner window bay with first floor timber framing topped by octagonal spire. Pair of two storey window bays on gabled garden front. Partly altered north facing facade with steel fire escape staircase and platforms. A number of brick and terracotta details including string and band courses, gable verges, decorative panels and circular vents. Stonework features include moulded door and window surrounds with tracery and shafts with floriated capitals. Also, moulded stone lintels, sills, sill bands and brackets. Stonework now painted. Complex roof form with a number of intersecting double pitched, plain tile roofs creating gables on elevations and at a number of levels. Ornamental clay ridge tiles. Octagonal spire with plain tiles and finial. Moulded brick details to gable verges generally except on garden front plus small hipped roof dormer with decorative brick panels above canted window bay. To right, gabled roof with projecting bargeboards plus tie and post. Red brick side wall chimney stacks with string courses, panels and altered caps. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Cross windows generally, also single casements, stone framed windows and leaded glazing. Some door and window alterations. Red brick boundary wall on road front rebuilt with railings added plus later and enlarged openings. Original garden walls to adjacent properties. Original terraced garden reshaped to accommodate drive with platform built above garage door entrance.
history: Detached house c1899 for Mr Marsden, paper merchant. Also known as Castle Dene. Now converted into flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic storey with single storey rear wing and outbuildings. Red brick facades with canted and polygonal window bays, timber framework at attic level, ashlar dressings and hipped and gabled plain tile roof. Domestic Revival style. Flat roofed glazed porch and fire escape staircase added on garden side adjacent to polygonal window bay plus gabled brick extension to original single storey hipped roof rear wing. Outbuilding known as ‘The Coach House’ probably built as a garage c1909 by same architect, Stockdale Harrison & Sons. Now converted into separate dwelling. Substantial part of lower garden area adjacent to Tattershall Drive now separately developed into four dwellings c1977-79. Residual garden area to original house now subdivided to create separate garden areas for flats. Blue brick band courses on facades w ith half-round and segmental brick arched heads to a number of window openings. Moulded brick pilasters to rear wing. Stonework features include moulded surrounds to ground floor window bays and round arched main entrance doorway, facade banding, window sills and datestone panel mounted on road front. Irregular main roof form with prominent half-timbered gables plus hips, octagonal spire above polygonal bay and dormer windows. Hipped roof on garden front with lead covered ridge vent. Recently reroofed with red plain tiles, bonnet hip tiles and clay ridge tiles. Iron finial to octagonal spire. Timber framed gables with overhanging decorated bargeboards. Jettied gable over canted bay on road front with large moulded wood brackets. Prominent modillion brackets to overhanging wood eaves. Red brick chimney stacks with moulded brick string courses, brick arched panels, splay bricks and stone dressings. Some original rainwater fittings have been replaced. Mainly sash windows, a number with glazing bars. Also casements, mainly at attic storey level plus leaded lights, including decorative Art Nouveau styling. Glazed and panelled main entrance door. Original brick boundary wall on road front with red brick plinth, square pillars and stone dressings. Only wrought iron posts of original railing between brick pillars now survive.
history: Large detached house by architects Stockdale Harrison & Sons for J. Woodward 1898. Formerly known as ‘Redcliffe House’. Now converted into flats c1975. One flat occupied by architect Robert Cullen.

name: Cavendish House:
desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Red brick with stone dressings and gabled slate roof with dormers. Two storey brick extension added to rear wing adjacent to road front c1939. Pair of window bays overlooking garden. Canted left hand bay with hipped slate roof at attic level and rectangular right hand bay with gabled dormer above. Stepped gabled wing on road front with flat roofed rectangular window bay set at angle to side of main building. Decorative brick details to verges, eaves and string courses with corbelling, mouldings, dentillation and patterning. Also, moulded brick surrounds to some window openings. Stone door and window surrounds with chamfered reveals and moulding details. Stone surround to Venetian window opening at attic level on rectangular bay overlooking garden. Large stone framed and mullioned window on north facing facade. Slate roof with clay ridge tiles, finials and prominent gables and dormers. Lead hips to roof of canted window bay. Red brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning, corbelled caps and buff crown pots. Roof windows added. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Red brick boundary wall to Cavendish Road East with inset panels, saddleback copings and gated openings with red brick piers, moulded brick and stone caps and stone plinths. Lantern over gated opening to main entrance. Large conservatory adjacent to house overlooking garden. Steps from garden to gated opening on Holles Crescent. Original boundary wall to Holles Crescent with coursed rubble stone plinth as retaining wall and red brick garden wall above. Opening in stone wall to former carriage house, with arched head and flanked by stone piers, now used as a garage. Later adjacent opening also used as a garage.
history: Large detached house c1890-95. One of a group of similarly styled houses including ‘Overdale’ and ‘Elmhurst’. Judges’ residence.

name: Elmhurst:
desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Red brick with stone dressings plus slate roof with gables and gabled dormers. Main entrance passageway and yard area adjacent to Cavendish Road East with terrace and garden facing Holles Crescent. Pair of window bays overlooking garden. Canted left hand bay with hipped roof at attic level and rectangular right hand bay with gabled dormer above. Stepped gabled wing on main road front with single storey lean-to extension. Single storey pointed window bay adjacent to main entrance doorway. Decorative brick details to verges, eaves and string courses with corbelling, mouldings and dentillation. Brick semicircular arched heads to a number of window openings, particularly at attic level. Blue brick damp courses. Stone surrounds with chamfered reveals to majority of window openings on side and garden facing facades. Elsewhere, separate stone lintels and sills. Prominent, moulded stone surround to main entrance doorway with pointed arch head, mullioned overlights, shafts with floriated capitals and steps. Gabled roof with slates (partly re-roofed) and ornamental clay ridge tiles and finials. Prominent gables on all frontages plus gabled dormers. Lead hips to canted bay roof. Red brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning, corbelled caps and crown pots. Some pots replaced. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Roof windows added plus flat roofed dormer window. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Panelled main entrance door and service doors. Glazed door added to canted bay, replacing sash window and part of brick and stone plinth. Paved garden terrace plus garden bank and steps down to main garden area. Original boundary wall to Holles Crescent with coursed rubble stone plinth as retaining wall and red brick garden wall above. Original steps from garden to gated opening on Holles Crescent with stone piers and original panelled door/gate with decorative grille. Red brick boundary wall to Cavendish Road East with gated openings and piers.
history: One of a pair of houses c1890-95. Now converted into flats.

name: Overdale:
desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Red brick with stone dressings and slate roof with gables and gabled dormers. Main entrance passageway and yard area adjacent to Cavendish Road East with terrace and garden facing Holles Crescent. Pair of window bays overlooking garden. Right hand bay canted with hipped roof at attic level and rectangular left hand bay with gabled dormer above. Stepped gabled wing on road front with single storey lean-to extension. Single storey pointed window bay adjacent to main entrance doorway. Decorative brick details to verges, eaves and string courses with corbelling, mouldings and dentillation. Brick semicircular arched heads to a number of window openings, particularly at attic level. Blue brick damp courses. Stone surrounds with chamfered reveals to majority of window openings on side and garden facing facades. Elsewhere, separate stone lintels and sills. Prominent, moulded stone surround to main entrance doorway with pointed arch head, mullioned overlights, shafts with floriated capitals and steps. Gabled roof with slates (partly re-roofed) and ornamental clay ridge tiles and finials. Prominent gables on all frontages plus gabled dormers. Lead hips to canted bay roof. Red brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning, corbelled caps and crown pots. Some pots replaced. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Roof windows added plus flat roofed dormer window. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Panelled main entrance door and service doors. Glazed door added to canted bay, replacing sash window and part of brick and stone plinth. Paved garden terrace with brick retaining wall plus railings and steps down to main garden area. Original boundary wall to Holles Crescent with coursed rubble stone plinth as retaining wall and red brick garden wall above. Original steps from garden to gated opening on Holles Crescent with stone piers and original panelled door/gate with decorative grille. Red brick boundary wall to Cavendish Road East with gated opening and piers to main entrance passageway at the side. Original gated opening with piers to yard now enlarged to provide car access.
history: One of a pair of houses c1890-95. Now converted into flats.

desc: Three storeys including attic plus cellars. In single household use. Red brick facades with brick plinth plus string courses at window head and sill heights. String courses form half round and segmental arches on pair of windows and door on garden front with decorative brick and terracotta infill patterning. Original single storey extension abuts road facing facade. Two storey brick canted window bay facing garden with stone window surrounds. Stone sills and chamfered lintels to openings generally. Inset brick panels to window bay with corbelled brick eaves to hipped bay roof. Open, moulded timber framed porch with hipped plain tile roof to main entrance doorway. Part of original porch framework has been altered. Brick garage building added at bottom of garden facing Huntingdon Drive forming breach in original brick boundary wall. Hipped plain tile roof with half-timbered gable over bay on rear facade. Dormer windows on front and back with plain tile hipped roofs and side tile hanging. Clay ridge tiles and bonnet hip tiles. Plain tile roof coverings have recently been renewed. Projecting timber eaves with replaced gutters and downpipes. Red brick chimney stacks with corbelled and projecting brick details. Central stack has added vertical brick relief patterning. A number of original pots are missing or have been replaced. Original sash windows, a number with glazing bars. Some minor alterations. Original casement windows to dormers and gable window plus side windows to entrance hall and first floor landing, possibly fitted originally with stained glass panels. Original brick boundary walls with plinth, piers and concrete copings adjacent to Clare Valley and Huntingdon Drive. Original brick gate piers with stone caps. Original brick and stone retaining wall to terrace and steps leading to lower garden area. The house remains substantially intact with few significant external alterations.
history: Semi-detached house, c1890, forming part of a group of five houses facing southern end of the ‘Bowl’ recreational area.

desc: Three storeys including attic plus cellars. In single household use. Red brick facades with brick plinth plus string courses at window head and sill heights. String courses form half round and segmental arches on pair of windows and door on garden front with decorative brick and terracotta infill patterning. Original single storey extension abuts road facing facade. Original brick boundary wall facing Clare Valley now breached by large flat roofed double garage building with two storey, pitched roof extension behind, c2000. Recent additions have resulted in loss of original porch, gate piers and entrance area. Two storey brick canted window bay facing the garden with stone window surrounds. Stone sills and chamfered lintels to openings generally. Inset brick panels to window bay with corbelled brick eaves to hipped bay roof. Hipped plain tile roof with half-timbered gable over bay on rear facade. Dormer windows on front and back with plain tile hipped roofs and side tile hanging. Clay ridge tiles and bonnet hip tiles. Hipped plain tile roof covering to original single storey extension recently renewed. Projecting timber eaves with replaced gutters and downpipes. Red brick chimney stacks with corbelled and projecting brick details. Central stack with vertical brick relief patterning. A number of original pots are missing or have been replaced. Original sash windows generally, a number with glazing bars. Some alterations or replacements. Original casement windows to dormers and gable windows at roof level. Original brick and stone retaining wall to terrace and steps leading to lower garden area.
history: Semi-detached house, c1890, forming part of a group of five houses facing southern end of the ‘Bowl’ recreational area.

desc: Three storeys including attic plus cellars. In single household use. Pair of pitched roof single storey garages now replace original small extension / outbuildings and part of brick boundary wall enclosing former yard area. Predominantly red brick facades with extensive half-timbered styling to first floor and projecting gable bay facing the garden. Single flat roofed box window bay at ground floor level on garden facade. Stone sills and chamfered lintels to openings generally with brick segmental arched head over door opening to garden. Hipped plain tile roof with bonnet hip tiles and clay ridge tiles. A number of ridge tiles appear to have been replaced with flashings. Dormer windows to front, back and both sides of house with hipped plain tile roofs and side tile hanging. Projecting timber eaves with replaced gutters and downpipes. Red brick chimney stacks with corbelled and projecting brick details. A number of original chimney pots have been replaced. Combination of original casement and sash windows with variety of glazing bar arrangements. Original brick and stone retaining wall to terrace and steps leading to lower garden area. Apart from alteration works as part of later garage additions, the house remains substantially intact with few other significant external changes.
history: Detached house at centre of a row of five houses, probably by same builder c1890, facing southern end of the ‘Bowl’ recreational area. House is flanked by pair of matching semi-detached houses.

desc: Three storeys including attic plus cellars. In single household use. Red brick facades with brick plinth plus string courses at window head and sill heights. String courses form half round and segmental arches on pair of windows and door on garden facing elevation with decorative brick and terracotta infill patterning. Original single storey brick utility extension abuts road facing facade but now rendered and painted. Two storey canted window bay facing garden with stone window surrounds. Stone sills and chamfered lintels to openings generally. Inset brick panels to window bay with corbelled brick eaves to hipped bay roof. Open, moulded timber framed porch with hipped plain tile roof to main entrance doorway. Original timber balustrading panels to porch removed. Flat roofed brick garage building added c1928, replacing small original outbuilding. Part of adjacent brick boundary wall facing road has been partially rebuilt with new gates added. Hipped plain tile roof with half-timbered gable over bay on rear facade. Dormer windows on front and back with plain tile hipped roofs and side tile hanging. Clay ridge tiles and bonnet hip tiles. Plain tile roof covering to hipped single storey extension recently renewed. Projecting timber eaves with replaced gutters and downpipes. Red brick chimney stacks with corbelled detail at head and projecting course at cap. Central stack has added vertical brick relief patterning. A number of original pots are missing or have been replaced. Original sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Replacement doors and windows particularly to single storey utility extension and to dormers and gable at roof level. Original casement side windows to entrance hall and first floor landing, possibly fitted originally with stained glass panels. Original brick gate piers with stone caps on Clare Valley boundary with original brick wall to part of boundaries adjacent to Nos. 3 and 5. Original brick and stone retaining wall to terrace and steps leading to garden. Recent wooden fences form boundaries with adjacent properties at lower garden level.
history: Semi-detached house, c1890, forming part of a group of five houses facing southern end of the ‘Bowl’ recreational area.

desc: Three storeys including attic plus cellars. In single household use. Red brick facades with brick plinth plus string courses at window head and sill heights. String courses form half round and segmental arches on pair of windows and door on garden facing elevation with decorative brick and terracotta infill patterning. Single storey brick extension abuts road facing facade. Two storey canted window bay facing garden with stone window surrounds. Stone sills and chamfered lintels to openings generally. Inset brick panels to window bay with corbelled brick eaves to hipped bay roof. Open, moulded timber framed porch with hipped plain tile roof to main entrance doorway. Porch now enclosed and partly altered. Flat roofed brick garage building added c 1969 at bottom of garden facing Tattershall Drive. Hipped plain tile roof with half-timbered gable over bay on garden front. Dormer windows on front and back with plain tile hipped roofs and side tile hanging. Clay ridge tiles and bonnet hip tiles. Plain tile roof covering has recently been renewed. Projecting timber eaves with replaced gutters and downpipes. Red brick chimney stacks with corbelled detail at head and projecting course at cap. Central stack has vertical brick relief patterning. A number of original pots are missing or have been replaced. Original sash windows generally some with glazing bars. Glazing bars removed from first floor sash windows facing Clare Valley. Single storey glazed garden room / conservatory added c2000. Original side windows to entrance hall and first floor landing, fitted with stained glass panels. Original brick gate piers with stone caps on Tattershall Drive boundary with original brick boundary wall adjacent to roads. Original brick and stone retaining wall to terrace and steps leading to garden. Recent wooden fence forms the boundary with No.4 at lower garden level.
history: Semi-detached house, c1890, forming part of a group of five houses facing southern end of the ‘Bowl’ recreational area.

name: Grove House:
desc: Two storeys with large, storey high brick plinth facing the garden. Detached garage building, with pitched roof, possibly added within a few years of original house. House at base of steep bank forming edge of the ‘Bowl’ and eastern boundary of The Park. Large front garden at lower level and shared drive with ‘The Spinney’. Style influenced by latter part of Arts and Crafts movement. Combination of painted render and brick facades. Single storey canted bays, with flat lead covered roofs, to front and south facing facades. Prominent chimney stack on garden facing facade with twin decorative window openings forming part of wider plinth at ground level. Plain detailing to facades generally with decorative brick segmental arches over main first floor windows plus brick heads and sills to other openings. Gabled and hipped roof with clay plain tiles, ridge tiles and bonnet hip tiles. Wooden eaves and bargeboards. Red brick chimney stacks with pattern detail at head plus stone caps. Variety of chimney pots. Predominantly wood casement windows, mainly fitted with lead / lattice panes. Single oculus window on front facade at first floor level. Retractable sun blind fitted above window head adjacent to main entrance door. Panelled main entrance door, half glazed with lead / lattice panes, plus semicircular arched fanlight frame and flat projecting canopy on shaped wooden bracket. Extensive groundworks with original stone retaining walls to lower front garden area. Steeply banked rear garden with retaining wall at base adjacent to the house.
history: One of four detached houses c1905-1916 at cul-de-sac end of Clare Valley overlooking tennis and bowls clubs. This part of Clare Valley originally named Huntingdon Drive. Plans for Grove House prepared in 1913 possibly by architects Jeffries and North for Frank Hodson (Senior). In single household use.

name: Ribble Lodge:
desc: . House at base of steep bank forming edge of ’Park Bowl’ and eastern boundary of The Park. Rear garden has been terraced with grassed areas. Two storeys including attic storey with projecting front and rear wings. Predominantly render with brick plinths on main facades and brickwork elsewhere, now partly painted. Single storey flat roofed box and bow window bays on front facade and projecting window on side elevation. Main entrance doorway sheltered by overhanging gable supported by single column. Brick sills and segmental arched heads to many brickwork openings. Hipped and gabled roof with clay plain tiles plus ridge tiles, hip tiles and flush verges. Exposed timber tie beam on front gable plus verge fascia. Projecting wood eaves and iron strap gutter brackets. Flat roofed dormer window overlooking front garden and inset roof window with lead apron on rear facing roof. Original rainwater fittings replaced plus additional pipework. Rendered chimney stacks, (originally exposed brickwork ?), with blue brick caps and red clay beehive pots. Stack on rear roof has been altered. Mainly wood casement windows with lead / lattice panes. Prominent Diocletian style window adjacent to main entrance doorway. Panelled and partly glazed main entrance door. Some minor door and window alterations. Extensive groundworks to form garden terraces and sunken front garden area with stone retaining walls, steps, paths and terraces.
history: One of a group of four detached house built at beginning of 20th century (c1905-1916), towards cul-de-sac end of Clare Valley (Formerly named Huntingdon Drive). Plans for Ribble Lodge (originally known as The Bungalow) were prepared by architects Jeffries & North c1908 for Frank Hodson (Junior) followed by later plans for garage c1922, also for Frank Hodson (Junior). In single household use. Style influenced by latter stages of Arts and Crafts movement

name: Springdale:
desc: Although relatively plain, house has variety of brick details with segmental arched heads over a number of openings with brick surrounds to oval windows. Corbelled brick stop detail at base of render at first floor level. Stone sills to some openings. Hipped and gabled roof with clay plaintiles, ridge tiles with finials, bonnet hip tiles and valley tiles. Painted wooden bargeboards at gables with overhanging wooden eaves and fascia. Upper parts of chimney stacks taken down and capped above eaves level. Single storey, flat roofed bow window bay to principal room overlooking garden with canted oriel window on double wooden brackets at first floor level, directly above. Smaller oriel window at first floor level on side gable. Mainly casement windows, some with glazing bars and lead / lattice panes. Single storey, canted main entrance porch with entrance doorway flanked by brick panels, one with small window. Flat roofed projecting wooden canopy over front door with twin support brackets and moulded edge detail. Flat roofs over porch, bay and oriel windows are covered in lead sheet.
history: Earliest of group of four detached houses c1905-1916 at cul-de-sac end of Clare Valley (formerly named Huntingdon Drive). Style influenced by latter stages of Arts and Crafts movement. House at base of steep bank forming edge of ‘Park Bowl’ and eastern boundary of The Park. Two storey ‘L’ shaped building with combination of painted render and brick facades. Brickwork mainly to ground floors and rear facades. No original plans available for house but plan for works to chimney stack prepared for James Wilson c1907. Plans also exist for garage for William Bayram c1951 and by architect Lloyd Smith for William Munro in 1952 and 1953.

name: The Spinney:
desc: Two storey house at bottom of steep bank forming edge of the ‘Bowl’ and eastern boundary of The Park. No significant alterations apart from addition of small lean-to coal shed c1940 and re-planning of part of rear ground floor to include area, originally used as toilet with external access, as part of house. Single storey garage building, with hipped plain tile roof and rendered walls and brick plinth, built in front garden area adjacent to access road c2000. Combination of rendered and brick facades. Veranda area with rendered columns adjacent to main entrance doorway. Plain detailing to openings on brick facades with decorative brick lintels and projecting brick sills. Two storey rendered box window bay on front facade with lead covered flat roof. Single storey flat lead roofed bow window bay with brick plinth on south facing facade. Large flat roofed dormer window above veranda area. Retractable sunblind to part of veranda area. Hipped section of roof facing the garden with pair of gables at rear. Clay plain tiles with clay ridge tiles and bonnet hip tiles. Combination of projecting wooden eaves and flush verges. Red brick chimney stacks with stepped heads and stone caps. Variety of chimney pots with some missing and later replacements. Predominantly wood casement windows, many with lead / lattice panes. A number of window alterations and replacements. Half glazed main entrance door and screen with lead / lattice glazing. Extensive groundworks with original stone terrace and steps adjacent to house, retaining walls to lower front garden area and stone gate piers and boundary wall to Clare Valley. Steeply banked rear garden area with access steps and retaining wall at base adjacent to the house.
history: One of four detached houses built c1905-1916 at cul-de-sac end of Clare Valley overlooking tennis and bowls clubs. This part of Clare Valley originally named Huntingdon Drive. Plans for The Spinney were prepared in 1915, possibly by architects Jeffries and North for a Mr Thomson (owner or builder). In single household use. Style influenced by latter part of Arts and Crafts movement.

desc: Various external alterations and additions including main entrance doorway with added canopy, new door and window openings, removal of chimney stacks plus flat roofed garage building at corner of Clifton Terrace and Park Ravine. Garage building replaced section of original brick boundary wall and gated opening. Boundary wall originally continued along Clifton Terrace frontage. Substantial part of original terraced garden area adjacent to Park Ravine and Fishpond Drive lost to residential developments c1970-80. Two storeys plus basement. Red brick with stone dressings and slate roof with twin gables and overhanging eaves on main frontages. Pair of single storey canted window bays overlooking garden flanking central, single storey lean-to extension with stone arched glazed openings. Decorative buff brick banding at eaves and storey height levels with brick stretchers and square inset panels. Stone door and window surrounds with combination of straight and semicircular arched heads. Chamfered reveals and moulded details plus shaft pilasters with floriated capitals to door openings. Distinctive ‘Italianate’ splayed corner window detail at first floor level on main front overlooking garden, each with colonnette. Stonework now painted. Slate roof with twin gables on main frontages. Overhanging eaves with ornamental iron corner brackets. Hipped slate roofs to canted window bays with lead flashings. Red brick chimney stacks removed above eaves level. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Sash windows generally, a number with glazing bars. Half glazed panelled door to original main entrance in recessed porch facing Park Ravine. A number of door and window alterations. Section of original stepped brick boundary wall to Park Ravine survives plus gated opening with brick piers and stone plinths and caps.
history: Detached house c1856-60, possibly by T.C. Hine architect. Also known as Denehurst. Now converted into flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic. Red brick with stone dressings and gabled slate roof. Now converted into two flats. Original lower garden area, previously accessed by steps, now part of garden of separate dwelling. Two storey canted window bay on garden front with hipped slate roof. To left, single storey rectangular bay with half glazed central doorway flanked by sidelights and pairs of brick pilasters. Prominent pair of bargeboarded gables on road front, either side of central main entrance doorway with timber framed lean-to canopy. Gabled wing to left of main entrance doorway, with later monopitched extension, extends to road front boundary. Lean-to conservatory abuts side of house and opens into garden. Decorative brickwork features include ornamental banding with mouldings at eaves and capitals, ornamental panels on canted window bay, moulded red brick string courses plus plain blue and buff brick banding at a number of levels on each of main facades. Also, red brick voussoirs to half round, segmental and flat arched window head openings on road front facade. Part of brickwork plinth flanking garden doorway has been rendered and painted. Moulded stone lintels to door and window openings on garden front with moulded stone sills generally, some on corbelled brick brackets. Stonework now painted. Sash windows generally with glazing bars to upper sashes. Gabled slate roof with clay ridge tiles plus iron finial to hipped canted bay roof. Projecting and partly decorated bargeboards to gables on road front. A number of smaller original and later lean-to abutments. Red brick chimney stacks with moulded caps. Side wall stack taken down above roof level. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Low brick boundary wall with painted stone copings and railings enclosing entrance yard area on road front plus square brick gate pier with painted moulded stone cap. Original brick garden wall adjacent to No.2 Park Ravine. Although wooden fence now forms garden boundary with No.4 Clifton Terrace, it is possible that gardens of Nos.3 and 4 may once have been used communally as single garden without division.
history: One of a pair of houses c1890. Although early road layout plans of The Park Estate indicated a link between Clifton Terrace and Hermitage Walk this was not implemented, probably because of considerable groundworks that would have been involved.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic. Red brick with stone dressings and gabled slate roof. In single household use. Two storey canted window bay on garden front with hipped slate roof. To right, single storey rectangular bay with central doorway flanked by sidelights and pairs of brick pilasters. Prominent bargeboarded gable on road front to right of main entrance doorway with timber framed lean-to canopy. Gabled wing extends to road front boundary. Later building work abuts side of house adjacent to rock face. Decorative brickwork features include ornamental banding with mouldings at eaves and capitals, ornamental panels on canted window bay, moulded red brick string courses plus plain blue and buff brick banding at a number of levels on each of main facades. Also, red brick voussoirs to half round and segment arched window head openings on road front facade. Some areas of brickwork now rendered and painted. Moulded stone lintels to door and window openings on garden front with moulded stone sills generally, some on corbelled brick brackets. Stonework now painted. Sash windows generally with glazing bars to upper sashes. Gabled slate roof with clay ridge tiles. Missing iron finial to hipped canted bay roof. Projecting bargeboards on shaped wooden brackets to gabled wing on road front. Red brick chimney stacks with moulded caps. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Low brick boundary wall with painted stone copings and railings enclosing entrance yard area on road front plus square brick gate pier, now rendered, with painted moulded stone cap. Cut rock face encloses garden on western side. Although wooden fence now forms garden boundary with No.3 Clifton Terrace, it is possible that gardens of Nos.3 and 4 may once have been used communally as a single garden without division.
history: One of a pair of houses c1890. Although early road layout plans of The Park Estate indicated a link between Clifton Terrace and Hermitage Walk this was not implemented, probably because of considerable groundworks that would have been involved.

desc: Four storeys plus basement. Terrace now much altered and converted into mixture of flats and commercial premises. Alterations and additions to original terrace building include loss of forecourt paving, steps, walls and railings; conversion of private gardens into car parks; removal of key architectural features including entrance porches, parapets/balustrading, cornices and chimney stacks plus alteration or replacement of a number of original doors and windows. Built c1859, architect T.C. Hine. Terrace of similar scale to adjacent Derby Terrace by architect P.F. Robinson c1829. Although these two large terraces may have represented early models of development as envisaged in The Park, no other buildings of this type, character or scale were subsequently built. Red brick facades with buff brick panel band detail below cornices. Variety of stone/stucco features including cornices, string courses and moulded door and window surrounds. Part of cornice on south facing facade is missing. Also removed, parapet/balustrading with ornaments above upper cornices on front and rear facades. Significant alterations to roof to create additional accommodation with raised section and full width glazing. Red brick chimney stacks with replaced copings and pots. Two storey, square entrance porch bay with flat roof. Three storey, rectangular window bay overlooking original garden area. Predominantly rectangular window openings with round and segmental arched openings on entrance bay. Combination of plain and glazing bar sash windows with some alterations. Panelled main entrance door with overlight. Part of original forecourt area adjacent to Derby Road conveyed to Corporation of Nottingham in 1959, with subsequent alterations. Each house in terrace had garden area extending to stone retaining wall on Newcastle Drive, (formerly named Pelham Terrace), with gated access to Newcastle Drive from No.1 Clinton Terrace.
history: One of seven houses within, originally, an imposing terrace with gardens overlooking The Park.

desc: Four storeys plus basement. Terrace now much altered and converted into mixture of flats and commercial premises. Alterations and additions to original terrace building include loss of forecourt paving, steps, walls and railings; conversion of private gardens into car parks; removal of key architectural features including entrance porches, parapets/balustrading, cornices and chimney stacks plus alteration or replacement of a number of original doors and windows. Built c1859, architect T.C. Hine. Terrace of similar scale to adjacent Derby Terrace by architect P.F. Robinson c1829. Although these two large terraces may have represented early models of development as envisaged in The Park, no other buildings of this type, character or scale were subsequently built. Originally, red brick facades with buff brick panel band detail below cornices, plus variety of stone/stucco features including cornices, string courses and moulded door and window surrounds. Almost all these key architectural features now replaced with brick facings added to front facade with painted render at rear. Also removed, parapets/balustrading with ornaments above upper cornices on front and rear facades and chimney stacks. Original two storey, square entrance porch bay with flat roof now demolished. Three storey, rectangular, flat roofed window bay overlooking original garden area. Originally, rectangular window openings generally, plus round and segmental arched openings on entrance bay and combination of plain and glazing bar sash windows. Now numerous alterations and additions. Part of original forecourt area adjacent to Derby Road conveyed to Corporation of Nottingham in 1959, with subsequent alterations. Each house in terrace had garden area extending to stone retaining wall on Newcastle Drive, (formerly named Pelham Terrace), with gated access to Newcastle Drive from No.1 Clinton Terrace.
