history: One of seven houses within, originally, an imposing terrace with private gardens overlooking The Park.

desc: Four storeys plus basement. Terrace now much altered and converted into a mixture of flats and commercial premises. Alterations and additions to original terrace building include, loss of forecourt paving, steps, walls and railings; conversion of private gardens into car parks; removal of key architectural features including entrance porches, parapets/balustrading, cornices and chimney stacks plus alteration or replacement of a number of original doors and windows. Built c1859, architect T.C. Hine. Terrace of similar scale to adjacent Derby Terrace by architect P.F. Robinson c1829. Although these two large terraces may have represented early models of development as envisaged in The Park, no other buildings of this type, character or scale were subsequently built. Red brick facades with buff brick panel band detail below cornices. Variety of stone/stucco features including cornices, string courses and moulded door and window surrounds. Cornices on south facing facade have been removed. Also removed, parapets/balustrading with ornaments above upper cornices on front and rear facades plus chimney stacks. Two storey, square entrance porch bay with flat roof. Three storey, rectangular, flat roofed window bay overlooking original garden area. Predominantly rectangular window openings with round and segmental arched openings on entrance bay. Originally, combination of plain and glazing bar sash windows now all replaced or altered. Original panelled main entrance door and overlight replaced by window. Parts of brickwork facades have been rendered. Garage opening formed at rear. Part of original forecourt area adjacent to Derby Road conveyed to Corporation of Nottingham in 1959, with subsequent alterations. Each house in terrace had garden area extending to stone retaining wall on Newcastle Drive, (formerly named Pelham Terrace), with gated access to Newcastle Drive from No.1 Clinton Terrace.
history: One of seven houses within, originally, an imposing terrace with gardens overlooking The Park.

desc: Four storeys plus basement. Terrace now much altered and converted into mixture of flats and commercial premises. Alterations and additions to original terrace building include, loss of forecourt paving, steps, walls and railings; conversion of private gardens into car parks; removal of key architectural features including entrance porches, parapets/balustrading, cornices and chimney stacks plus alteration or replacement of a number of original doors and windows. Built c1859, architect T.C. Hine. Terrace of similar scale to adjacent Derby Terrace by architect P.F. Robinson c1829. Although these two large terraces may have represented early models of development as envisaged in The Park, no other buildings of this type, character or scale were subsequently built. Red brick facades with buff brick panel band detail below cornices. Variety of stone/stucco features including cornices, string courses and moulded door and window surrounds. Cornices on south facing facade have been removed. Also removed, parapet/balustrading with ornaments above upper cornices on front and rear facades plus chimney stacks. Two storey, square entrance porch bay with flat roof. Three storey, rectangular flat roofed window bay overlooking original garden area. Predominantly rectangular window openings with round and segmental arched openings on entrance bay. Originally, combination of plain and glazing bar sash windows, now replaced or altered. Original panelled main entrance door and overlight replaced by window. Single storey extension to front facade to form new entrance. Parts of brick facades have been rendered. Garage opening formed at rear. Part of original forecourt area adjacent to Derby Road conveyed to Corporation of Nottingham in 1959, with subsequent alterations. Each house in terrace had garden area extending to stone retaining wall on Newcastle Drive, (formerly named Pelham Terrace), with gated access to Newcastle Drive from No.1 Clinton Terrace.
history: One of seven houses within, originally, an imposing terrace with gardens overlooking The Park.

desc: Four storeys plus basement. Terrace now much altered and converted into a mixture of flats and commercial premises. Alterations and additions to original terrace building include, loss of forecourt paving, steps, walls and railings; conversion of private gardens into car parks; removal of key architectural features including entrance porches, parapets/balustrading, cornices and chimney stacks plus alteration or replacement of a number of original doors and windows. Built c1859, by architect T.C. Hine, terrace of similar scale to adjacent Derby Terrace by architect P.F. Robinson c1829. Although these two large terraces may have represented early models of development as envisaged in The Park, no other buildings of this type, character or scale were subsequently built. Red brick facades with buff brick panel detail below cornices. Variety of stone/stucco features including cornices, string courses and moulded door and window surrounds. Cornices have been removed. Also removed parapet/balustrading with ornaments above original upper cornices and substantial parts of chimney stacks. Three and four storey, rectangular, flat roofed window bays on side and rear facades. Predominantly rectangular window openings with round arched openings on side bay. Combination of plain and glazing bar sash windows with a number of alterations and additions. Panelled main entrance door and overlight, with canopy and brick pilasters added, late 20c. Part of original forecourt area adjacent to Derby Road conveyed to Corporation of Nottingham in 1959, with subsequent alterations. Two storey side extension added c1965 in part of large original terraced garden area, blocking up window openings on side bay of original house.
history: One of seven houses within, originally, an imposing terrace with gardens overlooking The Park.

desc: Red brick with ashlar dressings and slate hipped and gabled roof. Two storeys plus basement and attics. Original single storey wing to yard area now converted for garage use, possibly by Swann & Wright c1925. Three storey rectangular window bay on road front plus three storey canted window bay with hipped roof on side. Two storey canted window bay with hipped roof overlooking garden. Bull nose brick detail with corbelling at eaves and gable verges plus string course with nailhead brick moulding at first floor level. Moulded and splay brick details to a number of window reveals with semicircular arched heads with voussoirs to second floor windows on side bay. Part of ground floor brickwork now rendered and painted. Stonework details include head and sill bands, door and window surrounds including moulded main entrance doorway, large mullioned window plus shaped lintels and sills including Venetian window opening on road front gable. Also, stone kneelers to gables with angled and corner details on bays and chimney stacks. Slate roof with gables, gabled dormer and hipped bays. Clay ridge and hip tiles. Lead flashings to canted bay facing garden. Roof windows added. Original red brick chimney stack removed. Central red brick stack rebuilt. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Originally, combination of plain and single bar sash windows, now a number of alterations and replacements. Prominent original boundary wall enclosing garden adjacent to Cavendish Crescent South with coursed rubble stone plinth, blue brick plinth stretchers and red brick garden wall with moulded string course and saddleback copings. Original brick boundary wall adjacent to Clumber Crescent South now partly rebuilt with enlarged opening to provide access for car parking in former yard area.
history: One of a pair of houses (with 4 South Road). Possibly by architects Hine & Son c1870-75. Now converted into flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic. Red brick with ashlar dressings and hipped and gabled slate roof. Flat roofed garage building added c1931 between original outbuildings and road front boundary. Also, single storey flat roofed later extension to right of entrance porch. Main entrance door with round arched head in gabled brick front to porch with glazed pitched roof. Part gabled road front with gabled dormers. Pair of central, single doorways on garden front with brick and stone steps, flanked by two storey canted and square window bays. Side entrance door with stone steps and two storey canted window bay on west facing facade. Variety of brick/terracotta detailing including moulded cornice band at first floor level on all facades with pediments and floral festoon panels over a number of window openings. Similar moulded details at eaves and to some gables forming pediments. Also, moulded reveals to attic windows and oculus on road front plus eight pointed star gable vents, dentillation at gable verges and string courses generally. Stonework features include moulded surrounds to ground and first floor window openings, separate lintels and sills plus plinths and copings. Some areas of brickwork and stonework now rendered and painted. Slate hipped main roof with gables and gable dormers. Hipped roof to canted window bay on garden front. Prominent red brick side wall chimney stacks with brick relief patterning, corbelled caps and crown chimney pots. Cast iron rainwater fittings with added pipework. Mainly casements with a number of cross windows. Also, plain sash windows. Large pair of timber framed windows to staircase hall on west facing facade with round arched heads and leaded lights. Panelled porch entrance door with half glazed panelled side door. A number of alterations and additions to doors and windows. Original garden area remains intact with paved terrace adjacent to house and stone side steps to lower lawn area enclosed by trees and shrubs. Large coursed rubble stone retaining wall and gate to boundary with Cavendish Crescent South plus red brick garden wall with saddleback copings above. Original red brick boundary wall on main road front with plinth and saddleback copings. Square gate piers, red brick with panels and string course plus moulded stone caps. Gabled brick porch front flanked by ornamental stone brackets. Part of wall replaced by later garage.
history: Detached house c1881. Part of house converted into separate flat.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic storeys. Red brick with ashlar dressings and hipped slate roof. Original outbuildings adjacent to Cavendish Crescent South converted and extended for separate residential use c1970-80. Character and appearance of original house adversely affected by later external alterations and additions, including single and two storey flat roofed extensions possibly by Child and Sherwin for Hugo Likiernik c1934, plus rebuilding of boundary wall adjacent to Clumber Crescent South. Steel fire escape staircases also added. Single storey flat roofed later extension on road front. Above at eaves level, decorative brick panel with pediment and to right, prominent Flemish gabled dormer window. Two storey canted window bay and pedimented entrance bay on east facade with oriel window facing west. Three storey canted and rectangular window bays on garden front with shaped gabled dormer windows above. Prominent tower with hipped roof between gabled dormers at attic storey level. Canted bay to basement at centre of garden front with conservatory above. Originally, stepped access into house from both sides, now partly altered. Rich variety of brick/terracotta detail including moulded cornice band at first floor level, corniced eaves with modillion brackets plus plinths, string courses, pediments, moulded reveals to openings, dentils and decorative panels. Stonework features include moulded surrounds to door and window openings, head and sill bands, separate lintels and sills plus copings and brackets. Some areas of brickwork and stonework now rendered or painted. Hipped slate roof with crested clay ridge tiles. Flat roofed dormer window and other roof windows added. Red brick side wall chimney stacks with brick relief patterning, panels and corbelled caps. Some stacks altered or partly removed. Original rainwater fitting removed and further pipework added. Variety of window sizes, openings and groupings with square and round arched heads. Sash and later casement windows generally, many with leaded lights. A number of door and window alterations and additions. Substantial part of original terraced garden area altered and converted for car use with access drives and grouped garages.
history: Detached house c1890, now converted into flats.

name: Adam House:
desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic storey. Red brick with timber framework and tile hanging at attic level, ashlar dressings and hipped and gabled plain tile roof. Alterations, additions and later extensions include three storey flat roofed red brick extension to north facade, flat roofed glazed pavilion with balcony between bays on garden front, conversion and enlargement of former carriage house at bottom of garden adjacent to Cavendish Road East into garages plus flat roofed double garage building adjacent to house on main road front. Three storey gabled main entrance bay on road front, three storey gabled wing and two storey canted window bay on south facade plus pair of single storey flat roofed rectangular and polygonal window bays on garden front. Decorative brick pilasters to side gable plus banding and string courses at storey height level. Also, moulded brick sills generally plus shaped brick lintels and sill aprons to canted window bays. Some areas of brickwork, mainly to original outbuildings, now rendered and painted. Timber framework at attic level with plain, shaped and moulded posts, sills and brackets plus white painted plaster infill panels. Stonework features include plain and moulded lintels, complete window surrounds to bays on garden front plus moulded surround to main entrance doorway. Irregular roof form with plain tiled hips, gables, gabled dormers and dormer windows. Decorative ridge tiles and iron finials. Overhanging bargeboards to gables and dormers. Tall red brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning, moulded brick cornices and copings plus stone dressings. Red clay roll top chimney pots. Flat roofed dormer window added. Casement windows generally, many with glazing bars and leaded lights. Some alterations and additions to doors and windows. Altered red brick boundary wall with railings on main road front with enlarged opening for car access. Original coursed rubble stone boundary wall plinth on Cavendish Road East with red brick garden wall with saddleback copings above.
history: Large detached house 1885, by architect Arthur Marshall for Samuel Bourne, photographer. Formerly known as ‘Brightlands’.

name: Edale House :
desc: Red brick with stone dressings and hipped and gabled roofs. Two and three storeys plus basement and attics. Possible late 19c and early 20c additions include large two and three storey red brick extensions with hipped and gable roofs abutting north front of original house plus single storey flat roofed red brick wing with stone balustrading on garden front, replacing original glasshouse/ conservatory. 20c garage extension. Porch entrance doorway on road front boundary with panelled door, moulded stone surround, round arched head and twin side shafts with floriated capitals. Brick and stone open pediment above. Original glazed porch now replaced. Plain sash windows on road front with moulded brick surrounds and stone sills. Moulded brick string courses and cornice band with moulded brick dentillation to eaves and gable verges. Prominent red brick chimney stack on road front gable with shaped flues, inset panels, patterning, string courses and corbelled cap. Flemish gable to central bay on garden front with brick festoon relief ornamentation and stone dressings. 3no. plain sash windows at ground floor level on central bay with moulded brick surrounds, festoon reliefs and pediment above central window opening. Pair of windows above with moulded brick surrounds, stone central and side shafts with floriated capitals and brick segmental pediment. Plain sashes either side of central bay with later glazed porch addition. To the left, large flat roofed, single storey later extension with stone balustrading. Alterations to openings at first floor level above extension roof. Above again, tall moulded, shaped and patterned brick side wall chimney stack. To the right, on garden front, large two storey extension. Pair of canted window bays on south facade. Flat roof bay, possibly later addition. Octagonal spire roof to right hand bay with plain sash windows, moulded brick surrounds, festoon reliefs and pediments. Large stone mullioned window with leaded lights on north facade with moulded brick open pedimented gable above and central decorative circular terracotta vent. Plain tiled roofs with bonnet hip tiles, ornamental ridge tiles and finials. Original brick and stone boundary walls and gate piers. Later gates.
history: Large detached house c1890, possibly by architects Hine & Son. Now converted into flats.

name: Jelenice:
desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Red brick with stucco and hipped and gabled plain tile roof. Early 20c garage building with pitched roof added at bottom of garden adjacent to Cavendish Road East. Later flat roofed double garage building on road front, breaching original brick boundary wall. Stuccoed gable on front with two storey polygonal window bay and single storey lean-to entrance porch with plain tile roof. To the right, main entrance facade with moulded timber framed canopy to doorway, single storey rectangular window bay with plain tile hipped roof plus large first floor timber framed window to staircase/hall with stained glass panels. Three storey garden front with stuccoed gable, pair of canted window bays and prominent wood framed verandah/balcony with balustrading. Part of balcony area infilled with glazing c1980. Balcony partly roofed with plain tiles and partly glazed. Polygonal window bay emerges through glazed balcony roof above lower bay. Plain brick north facing facade with stuccoed gable. Plain tile main roof with hips and gables. Bargeboards to gables. Plain projecting eaves. Originally, single flat roofed dormer window to north and south facing sections of roof. Later flat roofed dormer added to north facing roof. Red brick chimney stacks with moulded brick cornices and caps. Red clay tapered chimney pots with hoods. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Sash windows, with glazing bars to both sashes or just top sash. Also, casements and leaded lights. Glazed four panelled main entrance door. Large, coursed rubble stone retaining wall and steps separating upper and lower garden areas. Basement storey fully exposed on garden front. Red brick boundary wall with stone capped gate piers adjacent to Clumber Road East, now partly demolished or altered to create car access. Original red brick garden wall adjacent to Cavendish Rod East with plinth and saddleback copings. Part of garden wall replaced with later garage building.
history: Detached house c1898, possibly by architect E.M. Lacey. Now converted into flats.

name: Penryhn House :
desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic. Red brick with ashlar dressings and hipped slate roof. Stables and substantial part of original garden now converted and developed for separate residential accommodation. Other 20c alterations and additions include flat roofed first floor extension to north wing and substantial bow fronted conservatory to main garden front. Flat roofed garage building also added behind boundary wall at junction of Clumber Road East and Tunnel Road. Moulded stone surround to doorway on main entrance front, continued around plain sash window opening to first floor with stone and brick open pediments above. Pair of panelled doors to main entrance. Plain sash windows on entrance front, all with moulded stone surrounds. Stone cornice band at first floor level. Pair of two storey canted window bays on main garden front, flanking garden doorway and later bow fronted conservatory. Central canted window bay at first floor level above conservatory. Plain sash windows with moulded stone window surrounds, sill bands and cornices to bays. Comparatively plain east front with variety of openings including plain sashes with moulded stone surrounds to original window openings. One of 2no. original door openings with moulded stone surrounds on east front altered to form window opening plus first floor window added. Central bay on north front with prominent stone mullioned window with leaded lights at first floor level and above, shaped gabled dormer at attic level flanked by hipped roofs. Original single storey side entrance wing on north front now altered with first floor extension and new windows added. Slate hipped roof with clay ridge and hip tiles plus finials. Painted eaves cornice. Lead flashings to hips on canted window bays. Red brick ridge and side wall chimney stacks, originally with stone dressings. Now some alterations. Prominent boundary walls on Clumber Road East and Tunnel Road with random coursed rubble stone plinths, red brick walls and blue brick detailing. Square, red brick gate piers with coursed rubble stone plinths and moulded stone caps.
history: Detached house 1879 by architect T.C.Hine. Now converted into two flats.

name: Westwood:
desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic storeys. Red brick with hipped and gabled plain tile roof. Arts and Crafts style. Part of house converted into separate flat. Plan for garage by Swann and Wright for William Warner c1920-25. Only comparatively small part of original garden area now survives following residential development c1975 to right of original house and across lower part of garden adjacent to Cavendish Road East. Round arched brick opening to recessed main entrance porch on road front with flat roof and parapet at first floor level above. Also, similar round arched recess at basement level on garden front. To right of main entrance porch, single storey flat roofed bow window bay with canted oriel window above. To left of porch, twin gabled wing, single storey outbuilding with lean-to roof plus later garage building. Half-hipped gable to south facade, flanked by prominent chimney stacks, plus single storey rectangular bay with hipped roof. Gabled north facing facade with late 20c steel spiral fire escape staircase serving upper floors. Pair of two storey canted window bays with hipped plain tile roofs on symmetrical garden front flanking central terrace/balcony, now infilled, plus tile hanging above. Some areas of brickwork have been rendered and painted. Segmental and semicircular arched heads to a number of window openings with decorative blue brick band patterns in some areas. Steeply pitched plain tile roof with hips, half-hip and gables. Tile hanging to gables. Overhanging eaves generally. Red brick side wall and ridge chimney stacks with decorative banding and moulded copings. Ridge stacks altered and rendered. Mainly glazing bar casements with some alterations. Panelled and partly glazed main entrance door flanked by narrow single sidelights. Original coursed rubble stone boundary wall and gate piers on road front. Parts of original wall replaced by later garage building and adjacent residential development.
history: Detached house c1900-1905. Previously named ‘Cuylerholme’.

name: Holly Lodge :
desc: Red brick with stone dressings and hipped slate roof. Two storeys plus basement and attic. Original house extended and converted into flats. Stable building converted for residential use and other original outbuildings adjacent to former stable building replaced by additional residential unit c1965-70. Part of large original garden area now separately developed for 4no. detached houses. Flat roofed, rectangular brick porch on main entrance front with round arched heads to panelled door overlight and side lights. Moulded stone surrounds to porch door and window openings. Stone quoins above plus string course, cornice, blocking course and segmental panel. Part of porch facade now rendered and painted. Stone dressed original openings on main entrance front of original house with plain and glazing bar sash windows. Some door and window alterations and additions. Pair of window bays on south garden front. To the left, flat roofed canted bay with 3no. plain sash windows at ground and first floor levels. Brick with stone surrounds and dressings. Garden bank previously concealing basement storey now partly excavated with door and windows added. Exposed basement walls rendered and painted. To the right, flat roofed rectangular bay with doorway at ground floor level. Plain and glazing bar sashes. This bay may be very early addition (Late 19c). Glazing bar sash window with stone surround between bays. Large two storey canted window bay with hipped roof on west garden front with, to the left, smaller and later two storey canted bay. Plain and glazing bar sash windows with stone surrounds. Mid-late 20c extensions abut west front. Bricked up first floor window opening with stone surround to north facing wing. Glazing bar sash windows with stone lintels and sills to other parts of north facade with a number of alterations and additions. Flat roofed dormer window and roof windows added. Hipped slate roof with painted overhanging eaves and moulded band. Side wall and ridge chimney stacks with string courses and corbelled caps. A number of stacks now altered. Originally, large glasshouse adjacent to main entrance gateway, now only tiled floor remains. Original brick (and stone?) boundary walls enclosing garden and yard with gate piers to main entrance on Clumber Road West. Walls now rendered and painted.
history: Detached house c1875 with late 19c and 20c alterations and additions.

name: Linden House:
desc: Red brick with ashlar dressings and gabled slate roof. Two storeys plus cellar and attic storey. Two storey rear wing. Substantial part of lower garden area adjacent to Maxtoke Road developed as separate single storey dwelling c1978, architect Julian Marsh. Various alterations to main house including two storey, flat roofed, side extension with single storey, square, main entrance porch adjacent to Clumber Road West. Also, conversion and extension of former carriage house and outbuildings in rear yard area into garage and additional accommodation plus colonnaded garden loggia with ionic columns, corniced fascia and rusticated pilasters replacing original outbuildings adjacent to the house in that area. Alterations may be result of plans prepared by Niven and Wigglesworth for Otto Hornberger c1917. Steel fire escape platform and staircase from upper floors added plus covered access to cellar on garden front for air raid shelter purposes. Bull nosed brick and dentillation detail at eaves and gable verges. Half round arched window heads with brick voussoirs on north front. Pair of flat roofed, two storey, canted window bays on garden front with gable and gabled dormer over. Stonework features include canted window bays on garden front, door and window surrounds including Venetian windows at attic level on garden front, lintels and sills, cornice and entrance arch details to side extension and porch, hoodmoulds plus kneelers, copings, finials and decorative circular air vents to gables and gabled dormer. Slate roof with plain clay ridge tiles, gables gabled dormer and hipped dormer windows. Red brick chimney stacks with string courses, corbelled caps and stone dressings. Buff crown chimney pots. One stack altered. Original and replacement rainwater fittings and further pipework added. Mainly glazing bar sash windows plus casements and leaded lights. A number of alterations and additions to door and windows. Original brick boundary wall with saddleback copings and coursed rubble stone plinth. Original gate openings with piers to rear yard area now altered and enlarged to provide car access and parking. Later garden gate opening with red brick piers and stone caps.
history: Detached house c1874, possibly by architects Hine & Son. Now converted into flats.

desc: Two storey original house now much altered with a number of single and double storey flat roofed extensions added at different times. Formerly known as Clinton Villa. Plan for alterations to house by Child and Sherwin for Mrs Child c1940. In single household use. Painted stucco facades. Hipped slate roof to original house with plain band in shallow relief and dentillation at overhanging eaves. Moulded architraves to original window openings. Symmetrical arrangement of three openings at ground and first floor levels on original front and rear facades. Cornices to opening surrounds on ground floor facing road. Originally, stucco facades may have been lined to imitate masonry joints. Flat roofed window bay has replaced original window opening on garden facing facade. Alterations to original double hung glazing bar sash windows have been replaced. Upper sections of all four original chimney stacks have been taken down. Lead flashings to roof hips. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further downpipes added. Original boundary wall and gate piers adjacent to Derby Road, now roughcast rendered. Terraced area adjacent to house on steeply sloping garden site. A row of garages has been built at bottom of original garden adjacent to Newcastle Drive, (formerly named Pelham Terrace).
history: One of very early group of houses in The Park c1828-30, (with Nos. 119, 121, 123 and 125 Derby Road). Possibly by architect P.F. Robinson. Regency style.

desc: Two storeys and garret above Derby Road level plus habitable basement at lower ground level with direct access onto rear terrace. Part of basement extends under paved forecourt to main entrance. External steps to basement via lightwell from forecourt. Vaulted brick cellar extends from basement to under Derby Road pavement (formerly known as Clinton Terrace). Painted stucco facades with, originally, dentillated eaves and pediment to gable plus blocking course. Dentillation missing at eaves on garden facing facade and part of gable pediment has been removed. Moulded architraves to window openings. Stucco lined out to imitate masonry joints. Two storey, flat roofed square entrance porch with panelled double door flanked by pilasters. Later lean-to extension adjacent to porch. Gabled roof with slates and pair of central chimney stacks. Original cornice detail on one stack has been partially lost. Chimney pots missing. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Original double hung sash windows with glazing bars, some alterations. Garret window added adjacent to original in gable. Paved forecourt at Derby Road level. Steeply sloping terraced garden. Private upper terrace to garden with access from basement. Substantial original stone retaining wall with steps from upper garden level to lower garden terrace adjacent to Newcastle Drive (previously named Pelham Terrace). Originally, lower garden terraces of Nos. 119 and 121 were shared, with single stepped access leading to Pelham Terrace (Newcastle Drive).
history: One of a pair of houses (with No.121) similar to adjacent pair at 123 and 125. One of the earliest group of houses in The Park c1828-30, possibly by architect P.F. Robinson. House, boundary wall (Derby Road), gate piers and attached wrought iron railings are Grade II Listed. Regency Classical Revival style. In single household use.

desc: Two storeys and garret above Derby Road level plus habitable basement at lower ground level with direct access onto rear garden terrace. Part of basement extends under paved forecourt to main entrance. External steps to basement via lightwell from forecourt. Vaulted brick cellar extends from basement to under Derby Road pavement (formerly known as Clinton Terrace). Painted stucco facades with dentillated eaves and pediment to gable plus blocking course. Moulded architraves to window openings. Originally, stucco may have been lined out to imitate masonry joints. Two storey, flat roofed square entrance porch with panelled double door flanked by pilasters. Small flat roofed extension (storeroom) built c2004 in basement lightwell adjacent to Derby Road boundary wall. Gabled roof with slates and pair of central chimney stacks. Original cornice detail on one stack has been partially lost. Chimney pots missing. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Original double hung sash windows with glazing bars, now replaced. Semicircular arched window to entrance porch with margin lights. Garret window in gable. New large door opening from basement to upper garden terrace has replaced single segmental arched sash window opening with plain reveals. Flat roofed dormer window added overlooking garden. Paved forecourt at Derby Road level. Steeply sloping terraced garden. Private upper terrace to garden (now paved) with access from basement. Substantial original stone retaining wall and balustrade with steps from upper garden level to lower garden terrace adjacent to Newcastle Drive (previously named Pelham Terrace). Originally, lower garden terraces of Nos. 119 and 121 were shared, with single stepped access leading to Pelham Terrace (Newcastle Drive).
history: One of a pair of houses (with No.119) similar to adjacent pair at 123 and 125. One of the earliest group of houses in The Park c1828-30, possibly by architect P.F. Robinson. House, boundary wall (Derby Road), gate piers and attached wrought iron railings are Grade II Listed. Regency Classical Revival style. In single household use.

desc: Two storeys and garret above Derby Road level plus basement at lower ground level with direct access onto rear garden terrace. Vaulted brick cellar extends from basement to under Derby Road pavement (formerly known as Clinton Terrace). Painted stucco facades, with, (originally) dentillated eaves and pediment to gable plus blocking course. Dentillation only remains on garden facing facade. Moulded architraves to window openings. Originally, stucco may have been lined out to imitate masonry joints. Two storey, flat roofed square entrance porch with entrance door originally flanked by pilasters. Single storey flat roofed later extension adjacent to porch entrance. Gabled roof with slates and pair of central corniced chimney stacks. Original pots have been replaced. Lead flashings added to stone gable copings. Original rainwater fittings replaced. A number of original double hung glazing bar sash windows have been replaced. Segmental arched window opening to basement overlooking garden with plain reveals. Semicircular arched window to entrance porch with margin lights. Garret window in gable. Semicircular arched doorway from basement to upper garden terrace level. Paved forecourt at Derby Road level. Steeply sloping garden, now much altered. External steps from forecourt to basement. Private upper terrace to garden (now paved) with access from basement. Substantial original stone retaining wall and balustrade with steps from upper garden level to former lower garden terrace adjacent to Newcastle Drive (previously named Pelham Terrace). Originally, lower garden terraces of Nos. 123 and 125 were shared.
history: One of a pair of houses (with No.125) similar to adjacent pair at 119 and 121. One of the earliest group of houses in The Park c1828-30, possibly by architect P.F. Robinson. House, boundary wall (Derby Road) and attached wrought iron railings are Grade II Listed. Regency Classical Revival style. Building now used as Nottingham First Spiritualist National Church.

desc: Two storeys and garret above Derby Road level plus basement with direct access onto rear garden terrace at lower ground level. Vaulted brick cellar extends from basement to under Derby Road pavement (formerly known as Clinton Terrace). Painted stucco facades with, originally, dentillated eaves and pediment to gable plus blocking course. Remains of dentillation now only on garden facing facade. Moulded architraves to window openings. Originally, stucco may have been lined out to imitate masonry joints. Two storey, flat roofed square entrance porch with single storey flat roof extension covering original main entrance doorway flanked by pilasters. Gabled roof with slates and pair of central corniced chimney stacks. A number of original pots have been replaced. Lead flashings added to stone gable copings. Original double hung glazing bar sash windows have been replaced. Segmental arched window opening to basement overlooking garden with plain reveals. Semicircular arched window to original entrance porch with margin lights. Garret window opening in gable. Semicircular arched doorway from basement to upper garden terrace level. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Paved forecourt at Derby Road level. Original steeply sloping terraced garden, now much altered. External steps from forecourt to basement. Private upper terrace to garden (now paved) with access from basement. Substantial original stone retaining wall with steps from upper garden level to former lower garden terrace adjacent to Newcastle Drive (previously named Pelham Terrace). Original stone balustrading to stone retaining wall now replaced with low brick wall. Originally, lower garden terraces of Nos. 123 and 125 were shared.
history: One of a pair of houses (with No.123) similar to adjacent pair at 119 and 121. One of the earliest group of houses in The Park c1828-30, possibly by architect P.F. Robinson. House, boundary wall (Derby Road) and attached wrought iron railings are Grade II Listed. Regency Classical Revival style. In single household use.

desc: Three storeys with two storeys above ground level on Barrack Lane and three storeys facing Derby Road. Red brick with double pitched slate roof. Imposing four storey circular tower with flat roof, (previously domed?), at one end of wedge shaped building completely infilling plot between Barrack Lane and Derby Road. Tower may have been an early addition to terrace. Shop premises at ground floor on Derby Road front. Above at first floor level, row of 6no. replaced glazing bar sash type windows, repeated at second floor level with variation in size. Moulded eaves cornice above. Brick plinth to tower now rendered and painted. Single window opening to first and second floors of tower with three windows at third floor level. Original sash windows to tower replaced with plastic replicas similar to other windows generally. Irregular arrangement of doors and windows facing Barrack Lane. Original windows replaced and new openings added. Brickwork details include segmental and flat arched heads to window openings, splay brick sills and first floor storey height string course on Derby Road front. Splay brick corbelled head to tower. Variety of timber framed shop fronts at ground floor level on Derby Road. Slate roof, wedge shaped in plan formed by 2no. double pitched roofs with hips to roof adjacent to Derby Road and gable end to section of roof adjacent to Barrack Lane. No eaves cornice on Barrack Lane front. Clay ridge and hip tiles. 3no. red brick chimney stacks with string courses, corbelled caps and buff fireclay pots. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added.
history: Originally, terrace of six houses forming part of group of buildings adjacent to Derby Road known as Rock Terrace c1860. Now combination of shops and residential. Prominently positioned at junction of Barrack Lane and Derby Road. Plot backs onto Barrack Lane, previously named Barrack Road. Barrack Road formed main access to Army Barracks in The Park from Derby Road. Built c1793 and demolished c1870 the Barracks were replaced by new estate roads Pelham Crescent and Cavendish Crescent North.

desc: Red brick, now rendered on Derby Road front and side elevation. Originally, hipped slate roof, now replaced by tiles. Much altered. Lower part of building converted into shop adjacent to Derby Road with residential above. Original two storey red brick rear wing, now partly rendered, with monopitch slate roof. Double pitched 20c brick extension to rear wing in former yard area adjacent to Barrack Lane. Original windows on rear elevation replaced and new window openings added. Original glazing bar sash window opening with segmental arched head on side elevation with later smaller window opening below. Original brickwork and details on side elevation now obliterated by later rendering. Majority of original openings on Derby Road front replaced with three storey canted window bay. Original brickwork facade details and features obliterated with later rendering. Part of brickwork facade above shop has been painted. Only surviving original brickwork detail of note is shaped brick dentillation with string course at gutter level on rear facade. Red brick side wall and ridge chimney stacks altered or partially rebuilt. Roof windows added. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Original brick walls enclosing rear yard area replaced by 20c extension and other alterations.
history: One of pair of houses c1860 forming part of group of buildings on Derby Road known originally as Rock Terrace. Plot backs on to Barrack Lane previously named Barrack Road. Barrack Road formed main access to Army Barracks in The Park from Derby Road. Built c1793 and demolished c1870 the Barracks were replaced by new estate roads Pelham Crescent and Cavandish Crescent North. Two storeys plus attic and basements.

desc: Two storeys plus attic and basements. Red brick, now rendered on Derby Road front. Originally, hipped slate roof, now replaced by tiles. Much altered. Lower part of building converted into shop adjacent to Derby Road with residential above. Original two storey red brick rear wing, now altered with original monopitched slate roof replaced by flat roof, plus single storey, flat roofed 20c extension. Original windows on rear elevation replaced and new window openings added. Majority of original openings on Derby Road front replaced with three storey canted window bay. Original brickwork facade details and features obliterated with later rendering. Part of brickwork facade above shop has been painted. Only surviving original brickwork detail of note is shaped brick dentillation with string course at gutter level on rear facade. Red brick side wall and ridge chimney stacks altered or partially rebuilt. Roof windows added. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Small rear yard area adjacent to Barrack Lane with section of original brick boundary wall adjacent to Rocky Mount. Rebuilt boundary wall to Barrack Lane.
history: One of pair of houses c1860 forming part of group of buildings on Derby Road known originally as Rock Terrace. Plot backs on to Barrack Lane previously named Barrack Road. Barrack Road formed main access to Army Barracks in The Park from Derby Road. Built c1793 and demolished c1870 the Barracks were replaced by new estate roads Pelham Crescent and Cavandish Crescent North.

name: Rocky Mount :
desc: Two storeys with basement. Located on steeply sloping site between Barrack Lane and Derby Road with basement storey exposed on main Derby Road front. Barrack Lane, previously named Barrack Road, formed main access to Army Barracks in The Park from Derby Road. Barracks, built 1793 demolished c1870, replaced by sections of new estate roads Pelham Crescent and Cavendish Crescent North. Site of house and other houses adjacent to Barrack Lane previously used as allotment gardens. Red brick with stone dressings plus hipped and gabled plain tile roof. Two storey rear wing with gabled roof. Coursed rubble stone retaining wall adjacent to Derby Road front plus two storey canted window bay with hipped roof. Three plain sashes to bay at each level, now partly boarded up. Above, pair of windows with glazing bar upper sashes and gable over. Decorative gable vent. To right of canted bay, doorway at basement level with hipped plain tile canopy on timber posts and brick plinth walls. Above, single sash window at each storey level. Stone surrounds to bay windows with stone lintels and sills to other windows, plus stone head and sill bands. Moulded brick string courses plus decorative terracotta panels to bay. Canopied main entrance on side elevation with a number of single sash window openings including glazing bar upper sashes and stone lintels and sills. Canopied entrance on rear facade facing Barrack Lane with segmental brick arched and stone dressed window openings on rear wing. Later door opening at first floor level on rear wing with staircase and railing. Hipped plain tile roof with decorative ridge tiles and bonnet hips. Bargeboard to front gable plus shaped brick brackets to eaves. Red brick side wall chimney stacks with corbelled head detail and stone coping. Some stacks now altered. Early 20c garage building added on Derby Road boundary replacing part of original stone boundary wall. Originally, path access to main entrance on side of house from gated opening in stone boundary wall on Derby Road. Boundary wall to Barrack Lane now demolished and replaced with temporary metal fence panels.
history: Detached house c1900-1905, currently vacant.

desc: Five storeys including attic and basement. Large four storey extension with pedimented gable c1870 added to side of original house adjacent to original entrance porch block. Various extensions or separate developments built at back of building in original garden area. Obtrusive steel fire escape platforms and staircase attached to rear of building. Original basement projection from base of building to Derby Road footway now removed. Stucco with slate roof. Rusticated ground floor (i.e. terrace level). First floor sill band. Moulded eaves cornice with plain band in shallow relief to main facades plus moulded cornice and blocking course to attic storey. Two rendered sidewall chimney stacks. Original pots replaced with red clay hooded pots and flue terminals. Windows mainly original glazing bar sashes. Street facade of original part of house has two segment arched windows and door at basement level; three windows to rusticated ground floor section; three tall windows with moulded surrounds and cornices to first floor with renewed steel balconettes; three windows with moulded surrounds at second floor level and three windows with plain reveals at attic level. Projecting corner extension block has similar window arrangements but with only one window on each floor. Original porch block on side of original house with entrance door flanked by pilasters. A number of original openings at rear, originally facing garden, now obscured by later extensions. A number of later window alterations or additions. Originally, probably cast iron railings across the front of the house at terrace level. Railings added at road level c1995-2000 to enclose forecourt area for car parking. Gate piers in rendered boundary wall on North Road.
history: House, now converted into flats, comprises end bay of terrace which forms a prominent feature at north entrance to The Park Estate. c1829-30. Probably by architect P. F. Robinson. Of particular interest as the Terrace is considered a model of the architectural style and scale of house building that Robinson had envisaged in his ‘Plan for Nottingham Park’ 1827 for the 4th Duke of Newcastle. Grade II Listed.

desc: Five storeys including basement and attic. Late 20c flat roof extensions at rear. Original projecting basement with railings from base of Terrace to Derby Road footway, now partially rebuilt without railings. Stucco facades with slate double pitched roof. Rusticated ground floor, (i.e. terrace level on front facade), first floor sill band and altered coped parapet. Original moulded eaves cornice and parapet (with openings) now removed. Rendered sidewall chimney stack at ridge. Original pots replaced with red clay hooded pots. Windows mainly original glazing bar sashes with some alterations. Ground floor has fielded panel main entrance door with overlight and single, twelve pane sash window. Two, tall eighteen pane sash windows at first floor level with moulded surrounds and cornices. Originally, iron balconettes to first floor windows, now only four brackets remain. Two, nine pane sash windows at second floor level with moulded surrounds. Fenestration pattern originally repeated on rear facade but now compromised by later alterations and extensions. Attics have flat roofed box dormers at front and rear. At ground floor terrace level on front facade, original cast iron railings with steps to main entrance doorway. Railings also Grade II Listed. Originally, external steps down from terrace level to basement in same area. Original rear garden area now mainly used for garaging or car parking.
history: House, now converted into offices, comprises part of prominent terrace adjacent to north entrance to The Park Estate c1829-30. Probably by architect P.F. Robinson. Of particular interest as the Terrace is considered a model of the architectural style and scale of house building Robinson had envisaged in his ‘Plan for Nottingham Park’ 1827 for the 4th Duke of Newcastle. Grade II Listed.

desc: Five storeys including basement and attic. Plans prepared c1936 for conversion of 3 and 4 Derby Terrace into six flats. Original rear porch block now enlarged. Original projecting basement with railings from base of Terrace to Derby Road footway, now partially rebuilt without railings. Stucco facades with slate double pitched roof. Rusticated ground floor, i.e. terrace level on front facade, first floor sill band and altered coped parapet. Original moulded eaves cornice and parapet (with openings) now removed. Rendered sidewall chimney stack at ridge. Buff canon head chimney pots. Windows mainly original glazing bar sashes with some alterations. Ground floor has fielded panel main entrance door with overlight and single, twelve pane sash window. Two, tall eighteen pane sash windows at first floor level with moulded surrounds and cornices. Iron balconettes to first floor windows renewed. Two, nine pane sash windows at second floor level with moulded surrounds. Fenestration pattern repeated on rear facade but now partially compromised by later alterations and extension. Altered flat roof box dormer to attic. At ground floor terrace level on front facade, original cast iron railings with steps to main entrance doorway. Railings are also Grade II Listed. Originally, external steps down from terrace level to basement in same area. Original rear garden area now mainly used for car parking.
history: House, now converted into offices, comprises part of prominent terrace adjacent to north entrance to The Park Estate c1829-30. Probably by architect P.F. Robinson. Of particular interest as the Terrace is considered a model of the architectural style and scale of house building Robinson had envisaged in his ‘Plan for Nottingham Park’ 1827 for the 4th Duke of Newcastle. Grade II Listed.

desc: Five storeys including basement and attic. Plans prepared c1936 for conversion of 3 and 4 Derby Terrace into six flats. Original rear porch block now enlarged. Also, plan by Starr, Hall and Clifford for extension c1958. Originally projecting basement with railings from base of Terrace to Derby Road footway, now removed. Stucco facades with slate double pitched roof. Rusticated ground floor, i.e. terrace level on front facade, first floor sill band and altered coped parapet. Original moulded eaves cornice and parapet (with openings) now removed. Rendered sidewall chimney stack at ridge. Original pots replaced with red clay roll tops. Windows mainly original glazing bar sashes with some alterations. Ground floor has fielded panel main entrance door with overlight and single, twelve pane sash window. Two, tall eighteen pane sash windows at first floor level with moulded surrounds and cornices. Originally, iron balconettes to first floor windows now removed. Two, nine pane sash windows at second floor level with moulded surrounds. Fenestration pattern originally repeated on rear facade but now partially compromised by later alterations and extension. Attics have flat roofed box dormers. At ground floor terrace level on front facade, original cast iron railings with steps to main entrance doorway. Railings are also Grade II Listed. Originally, external steps down from terrace level to basement in same area. Original rear garden area now mainly used for car parking.
history: House, now converted into offices, comprises part of prominent terrace adjacent to north entrance to The Park Estate c1829-30. Probably by architect P.F. Robinson. Of particular interest as the Terrace is considered a model of the architectural style and scale of house building Robinson had envisaged in his ‘Plan for Nottingham Park’ 1827 for the 4th Duke of Newcastle. Grade II Listed.

desc: Five storeys including basement and attic. Plan for extension in car park, (original rear garden area), for Douglas Estates c1972. Originally, projecting basement with railings from base of Terrace to Derby Road footway, now removed. Stucco facades with slate double pitched roof. Rusticated ground floor (i.e. terrace level on front facade), first floor sill band and altered coped parapet. Original moulded eaves cornice and parapet (with openings) now removed. Rendered sidewall chimney stack at ridge. Buff canonhead chimney pots. Windows mainly original glazing bar sashes with some alterations. Ground floor has fielded panel main entrance door with overlight and single, twelve pane sash window. Two, tall eighteen pane sash windows at first floor level with moulded surrounds and cornices. Originally, iron balconettes to first floor windows, now removed. Two, nine pane sash windows at second floor level with moulded surrounds. Fenestration pattern originally repeated on rear facade but now compromised by later alterations and extensions. Flat roofed box dormers to attic. At ground floor terrace level on front facade, original cast iron railings with steps to main entrance doorway. Railings also Grade II Listed. Originally, external steps down from terrace level to basement in same area. Large extension (3 flats) now covers original rear garden area.
history: House, now converted into offices and flats, comprises part of prominent terrace adjacent to north entrance to The Park Estate c1829-30. Probably by architect P.F. Robinson. Of particular interest as the Terrace is considered a model of the architectural style and scale of house building Robinson had envisaged in his ‘Plan for Nottingham Park’ 1827 for the 4th Duke of Newcastle. Grade II Listed.

desc: Five storeys including basement and attic. Late 20c flat roof extensions at rear. Originally projecting basement with railings from the base of Terrace to Derby Road footway, now removed. Stucco facades with slate double pitched roof. Rusticated ground floor, (i.e. terrace level on front facade), first floor sill band and altered coped parapet. Original moulded eaves cornice and parapet (with openings) now removed. Rendered sidewall chimney stack at ridge. Original pots replaced with red clay canonheads. Windows mainly original glazing bar sashes with some alterations and additions. Ground floor has fielded panel main entrance door with overlight and single, twelve pane sash window. Two, tall eighteen pane sash windows at first floor level with moulded surrounds, plain head panels and cornices. Originally, iron balconettes to first floor windows, now removed. Two, nine pane sash windows at second floor level with moulded surrounds. Fenestration pattern originally repeated on rear facade but now compromised by later alterations and extensions. Flat roofed box dormers to attics. At ground floor terrace level on front facade, original cast iron railings with steps to main entrance doorway. Railings also Grade II Listed. Originally, external steps down from terrace level to basement in same area. Later building extensions and car parking now cover original rear garden area.
history: House, now converted into offices, comprises part of prominent terrace adjacent to north entrance to The Park Estate c1829-30. Probably by architect P.F. Robinson. Of particular interest as the Terrace is considered a model of the architectural style and scale of house building Robinson had envisaged in his ‘Plan for Nottingham Park’ 1827 for the 4th Duke of Newcastle. Grade II Listed.

desc: Five storeys including basement and attic. 20c extension to original rear porch. Originally, projecting basement with railings from base of Terrace to Derby Road footway, now removed. Stucco facades with slate double pitched roof. Rusticated ground floor, (i.e. terrace level on front facade), first floor sill band and altered coped parapet. Original moulded eaves cornice and parapet (with openings) now removed. Rendered sidewall chimney stack at ridge. Original pots replaced with red clay roll tops. Windows mainly original glazing bar sashes with some alterations. Ground floor has fielded panel main entrance door with overlight and single, twelve pane sash window. Two, tall eighteen pane sash windows at first floor level with moulded surrounds and cornices. Originally, iron balconettes to first floor windows, now removed. Two, nine pane sash windows at second floor level with moulded surrounds. Fenestration pattern originally repeated on rear facade but now partially compromised by later alterations and extension. At ground floor terrace level on front facade, original cast iron railings with steps to main entrance doorway. Railings also Grade II Listed. Originally, external steps down from terrace level to basement in same area. Original rear garden area now mainly used for car parking.
history: House, now converted into offices, comprises part of prominent terrace adjacent to north entrance to The Park Estate c1829-30. Probably by architect P.F. Robinson. Of particular interest as the Terrace is considered a model of the architectural style and scale of house building Robinson had envisaged in his ‘Plan for Nottingham Park’ 1827 for the 4th Duke of Newcastle. Grade II Listed.

desc: Five storeys including basement and attic. 20c flat roof extension at rear. Originally, projecting basement with railings from base of Terrace to Derby Road footway, now removed. Stucco facades with slate double pitched roof. Rusticated ground floor, (i.e. terrace level on front facade), first floor sill band, moulded eaves cornice and attic storey. Rendered sidewall chimney stacks. Stacks altered and pots removed. Some original glazing bar sashes with a number of alterations and replacements. Ground floor has fielded panel main entrance door with overlight and single, twelve pane sash window. Two, tall eighteen pane sash windows at first floor level with moulded surrounds and cornices. Originally, iron balconettes to first floor windows, now removed. Original nine pane sash windows at second floor level with moulded surrounds, now replaced with casements. Casements have replaced sash windows to attic storey. Fenestration pattern originally repeated on rear facade but now compromised by later alterations and extensions. At ground floor terrace level on front facade, original cast iron railings with steps to main entrance doorway. Railings are also Grade II Listed. Originally, external steps down from terrace level to basement in same area. Original rear garden area now mainly used for garaging or car parking.
history: House, now converted into offices, comprises part of prominent terrace adjacent to north entrance to The Park Estate c1829-30. Probably by architect P.F. Robinson. Of particular interest as the Terrace is considered a model of the architectural style and scale of house building Robinson had envisaged in his ‘Plan for Nottingham Park’ 1827 for the 4th Duke of Newcastle. Grade II Listed.

desc: Five storeys including basement and attic. 20c flat roof extension at rear. Original projecting basement with railings from base of Terrace to Derby Road footway, now partially rebuilt and without railings. Stucco facades with slate double pitched roof. Rusticated ground floor, (i.e. terrace level on front facade), first floor sill band, moulded eaves cornice and attic storey. Rendered sidewall chimney stacks. Stacks altered and pots removed. Windows mainly original glazing bar sashes with some alterations. Ground floor has fielded panel main entrance door with overlight and single, twelve pane sash window. Two, tall eighteen pane sash windows at first floor level with moulded surrounds and cornices. Originally, iron balconettes to first floor windows, now removed. Two, nine pane sash windows at second floor level with moulded surrounds. Casements have replaced sash windows to attic storey. Fenestration pattern originally repeated on rear facade but now partially compromised by later alterations and extension. At ground floor terrace level on front facade, mainly original cast iron railings with steps to main entrance doorway. Railings also Grade II Listed. Originally, external steps down from terrace level to basement in same area. Original rear garden area now mainly used for car parking.
history: House, now converted into flats, comprises part of prominent terrace adjacent to north entrance to The Park Estate c1829-30. Probably by architect P.F. Robinson. Of particular interest as the Terrace is considered a model of the architectural style and scale of house building Robinson had envisaged in his ‘Plan for Nottingham Park’ 1827 for the 4th Duke of Newcastle. Grade II Listed.

desc: Red brick with stone dressings and hipped slate roof. Two storeys with attic and basement plus original single storey, hipped roof side wing. Coach house and stable buildings at bottom of garden adjacent to Clumber Road East demolished c1970 and replaced with terrace of 4no. ‘townhouses’. Pointed brick arch to main entrance doorway on road front with stonework features including hoodmould, keystone and side shafts with floriated capitals. Panelled door with overlight. To left, pair of windows with pointed arch stone heads and stone mullion shaft. Moulded stone plinth to brick and stone canted oriel bay at first floor level directly above. Part moulded stone, part moulded brick surrounds to plain sash windows of oriel bay with moulded brick panels to parapet above. Moulded brick band at first floor storey level across road front, oriel bay and encircling other facades. Above oriel bay at roof level, elaborately shaped brick gabled dormer with stone dressings including copings, kneelers, finial and pinnacles on brick pedestals. Pointed arch stone head and sill to dormer window with moulded brick reveals and altered window. Square headed stone lintels and sills to other window openings on road front with plain and glazing bar sashes. Coved eaves with moulded brick band course. Pair of two storey canted door and window bays on garden front with stone head and sill bands and moulded brick shafts to reveals. Plain glazing bar sashes to canted bays with altered doors. A number of alterations and additions to openings including flat roofed dormer windows added to altered roofs of canted bays. Large stone framed window with panel tracery and leaded glazing on north facing facade flanked by large panelled brick chimney breasts and stacks, pierced at roof level with dormer window. Elsewhere, plain sash windows generally with flat or pointed arch stone heads and surrounds. Slate hipped roof with original and later dormer windows. Red brick chimney stacks with blue brick band courses, corbelled heads and ‘crown’ pots. Originally, large glasshouse abutted south facing side facade. Brick boundary wall to Duke William Mount and part of Lincoln Circus, now rendered.
history: Detached house c1878-80 by architect T.C.Hine. Previously converted into flats c1952 but now back in single household use.

desc: Red brick with stone dressings and gabled slate roof. Two storeys plus basement and attic. Single storey 20c extension adjacent to main entrance area. Two storey rectangular window bay to gabled road front facade including pair of sash window openings with round arched heads at ground and first floor levels. Bay reduces to single window opening at attic level with round arched head and brick pediment above. Similar brick window bay to gabled garden front with pair of round arched window openings at ground floor and first floor level and pedimented single window opening at gable level. Decorative brickwork features include moulded brickwork with dentillation detail at eaves and gable verges plus moulded cornices, string courses and door and window reveals. Window openings on road front bay framed by brick pilasters with capitals. Stonework details include door and window sills plus cornices, kneelers and copings. Slate roof with prominent, steeply pitched gable on each elevation. Pair of tall, shared red brick chimney stacks with brick relief detail, string courses and corbelled brick and stone caps. Buff ‘crown’ chimney pots. Roof windows. Combination of plain and glazing bar sash windows, mainly with round arched heads. Coursed rubble stone boundary wall plinth on main road front with ashlar copings. Original iron railings may have been requisitioned during Second World War. Brick garden walls. Brick and stone boundary wall to Clumber Road East with garages added during first half of 20c.
history: One of a pair of houses c1878-80 by architect T.C. Hine. In single household use. Also known as ‘Selsoe’.

desc: Red brick with stone dressings and gabled slate roof. Two storeys plus basement and attic. A number of later additions and alterations including single storey flat roofed extension adjacent to main entrance plus two storey gabled side wing with brick facades and slate roof. Two storey rectangular window bay to gabled road front facade including pair of sash window openings with round arched heads at ground and first floor levels. Bay reduces to single window opening at attic level with round arched head and brick pediment above. Similar brick window bay to gabled garden front with pair of round arched window openings at ground floor and first floor level and pedimented single window opening at gable level. Decorative brickwork features include moulded brickwork with dentillation detail at eaves and gable verges plus moulded cornices, string courses and door and window reveals. Window openings on road front bay framed by brick pilasters with capitals. Stonework details include door and window sills plus cornices, kneelers and copings. Some stonework has been painted. Slate roof with prominent, steeply pitched gable on each elevation. Pair of tall, shared red brick chimney stacks with brick relief detail, string courses and corbelled brick and stone caps. Buff ‘crown’ chimney pots. A number of original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Roof windows. Combination of plain and glazing bar sash windows, mainly with round arched heads. Coursed rubble stone boundary wall plinth on main road front with ashlar copings. Original iron railings may have been requisitioned during Second World War. Brick garden walls. Brick and stone boundary wall to Clumber Road East with garages added during first half of 20c. ‘Fairlawn’, a large imposing original estate house c1880 to right of No.3, demolished during 1960’s and replaced by eight dwellings in two separate developments.
history: One of a pair of houses c1878-80 by architect T.C. Hine. In single household use.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with three storey canted bay and two storey box bay either side of recessed main entrance porch on front facade. No significant extensions to original building. Red brick facades with corbelled brick / terracotta detailing at eaves and gable verges plus brick ovolo mouldings to window reveals. Decorative brick / terracotta band course at first floor level with recessed brick panels under sills on window bays. Decorative brick / terracotta motif above window lintel on gable dormer. Lancet arched head to main entrance porch with brick voussoirs and hoodmould. Stone lintels and sills to main window openings plus keystone and imposts to arched opening over entrance porch. The stonework has now been painted. Saddleback roof with hipped gable, hipped roofs to bays plus gabled dormer. Originally, clay plain tiles with decorative, perforated ridge tiles, (see 12 Hope Drive), now replaced. Brick chimney stacks with single string course and red and blue brick caps. A number of the original buff ‘crown’ pots survive. Alterations to caps of gable stacks including removal of some pots. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Casement windows to second floor of canted bay. Original panelled and partly glazed main entrance door. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further downpipes, waste pipes and hopper heads added. Small front and rear garden areas. Rebuilt low brick boundary wall and gate piers adjacent to Fish Pond Drive and Hope Drive.
history: One of a pair of houses c1888-90 possibly for Frederick Messom. Now converted into flats. Probably built as a group with houses of similar style on Hope Drive (12-26). Area had been previously used, since the beginning of the 19c, as garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with three storey canted bay and two storey box bay either side of recessed main entrance porch on front facade. No significant extensions to original building. Red brick facades with corbelled brick / terracotta detailing at eaves and gable verges plus brick ovolo mouldings to window reveals. Decorative brick / terracotta band course at first floor level with recessed brick panels under sills on window bays. Decorative brick / terracotta motif above window lintel on gable dormer. Lancet arched head to main entrance porch with brick voussoirs and hoodmould. Stone lintels and sills to main window openings plus keystone and imposts to arched opening over entrance porch. The stonework has now been painted. Saddleback roof with hipped roofs to bays plus gabled dormer. Originally, clay plain tiles to roof with decorative, perforated ridge tiles, (see 12 Hope Drive), now replaced. Brick chimney stacks with single string course and red and blue brick caps. A number of the original buff ‘crown’ pots survive. Alterations to caps of gable stacks including removal of pots. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Casement windows to second floor of canted bay. Some alterations to windows. Original panelled and partly glazed main entrance door. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further down pipes, waste pipes and hopper heads added. Small front and rear garden areas. Rebuilt low brick boundary wall and gate piers adjacent to Fish Pond Drive and Hope Drive.
history: One of a pair of houses c1888-90, possibly for Frederick Messom. Now converted into flats. Probably built as a group with houses of similar style on Hope Drive (12-26). Area had been previously used, since the beginning of the 19c, as garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens.

desc: Three storeys including attic space plus basement with two storey canted bay on front facade, three storey canted bay on side facade and two storey rear wing. Originally, outbuilding / privy in back garden area. Red brick facades with single string course, plus projecting brick headers, at first floor storey height level and at second floor window sill level to canted bay. Stepped brickwork detail at verges on front facade gable with chevron patterned decorative brickwork in framed panels at second floor level on side bay. Chamfered brick reveals to some window openings. Brick segmental arched openings over rear windows. Part of brick facades to rear wing now painted white. Stone dressings to main door and window apertures with a number of complete stone surrounds, including Tudor arched head with openings to main entrance doorway, otherwise mainly chamfered lintels and sills. Stonework now painted. Steeply pitched saddleback roof with hipped roofs to bays and rear wing. Clay plain tiles with bonnet hip tiles and ornamental, perforated clay ridge tiles and finials. Painted, projecting wooden eaves with bargeboards at gable. Original rainwater fittings have been replaced. Prominent brick chimney stacks with string courses, corbelled caps and buff, louvred chimney pots. Some pots missing or have been replaced. Original casement windows on main facades with single transoms and mainly single mullions. Also, sash windows at rear. Apart from white painted brick facades at rear, no other significant external alterations or extensions. Brick boundary wall to Hamilton drive with saddleback copings and stone capped brick piers. Openings enlarged in boundary wall to allow off street car parking in part of former front garden area.
history: One of a pair of semi-detached houses (1 and 1a), c1886-1890, possibly built as a group with similar houses at 1, 2, 3, 6, 7 Peveril Drive and ‘Ferndene’, Hamilton Drive. Area previously used for garden allotments, known as Fishpond Gardens, since beginning of 19c. In single household use.

desc: Three storeys including attic space plus basement with two storey canted bay on front facade, three storey canted bay on side facade and two storey rear wing. Originally, outbuilding / privy in back garden area. Red brick facades with single string course, plus projecting brick headers, at first floor storey height level and at second floor window sill level to canted bay. Stepped brickwork detail at verges on front facade gable with chevron patterned decorative brickwork in framed panels at second floor level on side bay. Chamfered brick reveals to some window openings. Brick segmental arched openings over rear windows. Part of brick facades to rear wing now painted white. Stone dressings to main door and window apertures with a number of complete stone surrounds, including Tudor arched head with openings to main entrance doorway, otherwise mainly chamfered lintels and sills. Stonework now painted. Steeply pitched saddleback roof with hipped roofs to bays and rear wing. Clay plain tiles with bonnet hip tiles and ornamental, perforated clay ridge tiles and finials. Painted, projecting wooden eaves with bargeboards at gable. Original rainwater fittings have been replaced. Prominent brick chimney stacks with string courses, corbelled caps and buff, louvred chimney pots. Some pots missing or have been replaced. Original casement windows on main facades with single transoms and mainly single mullions. Also, sash windows at rear. Apart from white painted brick facades at rear, no other significant external alterations or extensions. Brick boundary wall to Hamilton drive with saddleback copings and stone capped brick piers. Openings enlarged in boundary wall to allow off street car parking in part of former front garden area.
history: One of a pair of semi-detached houses (1 and 1a), c1886-1890, possibly built as a group with similar houses at 1, 2, 3, 6, 7 Peveril Drive and ‘Ferndene’, Hamilton Drive.
