desc: Two storeys with projecting side wing. Single storey flat roofed canted bay on front facade. Originally, outbuilding / privy in back garden / yard area. Red brick facades with range of brick / terracotta ornamentation including moulded projections with dentils at eaves and gable verges, string courses of varying detail plus prominent decorative corrugated brick band on gables. Chamfered brick reveals to a number of openings. Stone surrounds to window openings on front facade with moulded reveals and stone shaft mullion with capital at first floor level. Stone sills and chamfered lintels to other window openings, some with brick mullions. Stonework now painted. Gabled roof. Clay plain tiles and ridge tiles. Lean-to canopy with plain tiles supported on wooden brackets over main entrance doorway on side facade. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Originally, two brick stacks with buff ‘crown’ pots. Generally original sash windows with glazing bars. Original brick plinth with stone copings to boundary wall adjacent to Hamilton Drive. Original railings replaced and enlarged opening formed to allow off street parking on part of former garden area.
history: One of a pair of semi-detached houses, (1b and 1c), c1886-1890. Area previously used, since beginning of 19c, for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens. In single household use. Significant part of existing side garden area to house previously used for rear access to adjacent (now converted) former mews building located between back of house and Hope Drive. T

desc: Two storeys with projecting rear wing and single storey flat roofed rectangular box bay on front facade. Originally, outbuilding / privy in back garden / yard area. Red brick facades with range of brick / terracotta ornamentation including moulded projections with dentils at eaves and gable verges, string courses of varying sizes and detail plus prominent decorative corrugated brick band on gables. Chamfered brick reveals to a number of openings. Original brick facades to rear wing now rendered or painted. Stone surrounds to window openings on front facade with moulded reveals and stone shaft mullion with capital. Stone sills and chamfered lintels to other window openings. Stonework now painted. Gabled roof. Clay plain tiles and ridge tiles. Lean-to canopy with plain tiles supported on wooden brackets over main entrance doorway on side facade. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Originally, two brick stacks with buff ‘crown’ pots. Generally original sash windows with glazing bars. Original brick plinth with stone copings to boundary wall adjacent to Hamilton Drive. Original railings replaced and enlarged opening formed to allow off street parking on part of former garden area.
history: One of a pair of semi-detached houses, (1b and 1c), c1886-1890 in an area previously used, since beginning of 19c, for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens. In single household use.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with a part two storey and part single storey rear wing plus, originally, small outbuilding / privy. No significant extensions to original building. Red brick facades with common bricks used on side and rear elevations, now partly rendered. Two storey canted window bay on front facade with flat roof. Corbelled brick / terracotta detailing at eaves and gable verges with double and single, shaped or moulded brick / terracotta string courses at storey height levels on front facade. Stone dressings to door and window openings including complete surrounds on front facade with lintels and sills on side and rear elevations. The stonework has now been painted. Gabled roof with large side gable plus gable over brick canted bay on front facade and gable dormer facing garden. Gables to stepped roof on rear wing. Plain tiles with decorative perforated clay ridge tiles. Original brick chimney stacks with string course and corbelled red and blue brick caps. Buff ‘crown’ chimney pots. Originally, pair of stacks to main house plus single gable stack to rear wing. Some stacks now altered. Original lean-to canopy over main entrance door with patterned plain tiles and carved wood bracket with decorative side panel. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars, plus six panel partly glazed main entrance door. Roof windows added. Original cast iron rainwater fittings replaced and further downpipes added. Small rear garden area with garden wall adjacent to rear gardens of houses on Hope Drive. Original brick garden wall adjacent to Hamilton Drive now removed to provide car parking area in place of original front garden.
history: One of a group of eight similar semi-detached houses built c1886-88, possibly for Frederick Messom, now converted into flats or bed-sit accommodation. Area previously used for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with part two storey and part single storey rear wing plus, originally, a small outbuilding / privy. No significant extensions to original building. Originally, red brick facades with common bricks used on side and rear elevations, now obliterated by white paint. Three storey canted window bay on front facade with hipped, plain tile roof. Corbelled brick / terracotta detailing at eaves and gable verges with double, shaped or moulded brick / terracotta string courses at storey height levels on front facade. Stone dressings to door and window openings including complete surrounds on front facade with lintels and sills on side and rear elevations. The stonework has now been painted. Gabled roof with large side gable, hipped canted bay on front facade plus gable dormer facing garden. Gables to stepped roof on rear wing. Plain tiles, bonnet tile hips and decorative perforated clay ridge tiles. Original brick chimney stacks with string course and corbelled red and blue brick caps. Buff ‘crown’ chimney pots. Originally pair of stacks to the main house plus single stack at gable to rear wing. Original lean-to canopy over main entrance door with patterned plain tiles and carved wood bracket with decorative side panel. Originally, sash windows generally with six panel partly glazed main entrance door. Casements to second floor bay window. Some alterations to original doors and windows. Original cast iron rainwater fittings replaced and further downpipes added. Small rear garden area with boundary wall adjacent to rear gardens of houses on Hope Drive. Remains of original brick boundary wall to Hamilton Drive, now painted.
history: One of a group of eight similar semi-detached houses built c1886-88, possibly for Frederick Messom, now converted into flats or bed-sit accommodation. Area previously used for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens.

Originally sash windows generally, some with glazing bars, plus six panel partly glazed main entrance door. Original cast iron rainwater fittings replaced and further downpipes added. Small rear garden area with boundary wall adjacent to rear gardens of houses on Hope Drive. Most of the original brick boundary wall, enclosing front garden area adjacent to Hamilton Drive, now removed.
history: One of a group of eight similar semi-detached houses built c1886-88, possibly for Frederick Messom. In single household use. Area previously used for garden allotments known as Fishond Gardens.

Original brick boundary wall adjacent to Hamilton Drive partly removed to provide car parking area.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with part two storey and part single storey rear wing plus, originally, a small outbuilding / privy. No significant extensions to original building. Red brick facades. Three storey canted window bay on front facade with hipped plain tile roof. Corbelled brick / terracotta detailing at eaves and gable verges with double, shaped or moulded brick / terracotta string courses at storey height levels on front facade. Stone dressings to door and window openings including complete surrounds on front facade with lintels and sills on side and rear elevations. The stonework has now been painted. Gabled roof with large side gable, hipped gable over canted bay on front facade and gable dormer facing garden. Gables to stepped roof on rear wing. Plain tiles with decorative perforated clay ridge tiles. Original brick chimney stacks with string course and corbelled red and blue brick caps. Buff ‘crown’ chimney pots. Two shared stacks to main house plus single stack at gable to rear wing. Original lean-to canopy over main entrance door with patterned plain tiles and carved wood bracket with decorative side panel. Original sash windows generally, some with glazing bars, plus six panel partly glazed main entrance door. Casements to second floor bay window. Roof window added. Original cast iron rainwater fittings replaced and further downpipes added. Small back garden area with rebuilt brick rear boundary walls and gate piers. Original brick boundary wall and gate piers, with caps, enclosing small front garden area adjacent to Hamilton Drive.
history: One of a group of similar semi-detached houses, on Hamilton Drive, built c1886-1888, possibly for Frederick Messom. Area previously used for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens. In single household use.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with projecting wing on rear facade. No significant extensions to original building. Red brick facades, all exposed to adjacent roads on this corner plot. Three storey canted window bays on north and west facing facades with hipped plain tile roofs. Corbelled brick / terracotta detailing at eaves and gable verges with double, shaped or moulded brick / terracotta string courses at storey height levels on main facades. Stone dressings to door and window openings including complete surrounds on front facades with lintels and sills on rear elevation. The stonework has now been painted. Gabled roof with hipped roof over brick canted bays and large gable dormer facing garden .Plain tiles with bonnet hip tiles and decorative perforated clay ridge tiles. Original brick chimney stacks with string course and corbelled red and blue brick caps. Buff ‘crown’ chimney pots. Original lean-to canopy over main entrance door with patterned plain tiles and carved wood bracket with decorative side panel. A number of original sash windows, some with glazing bars, survive, together with original six panel partly glazed main entrance door. Casements to second floor bay windows. Some alterations to windows. Small casement openings and roof window added. Original cast iron rainwater fittings replaced and further downpipes added. Small garden areas adjacent to the house remain enclosed by original brick boundary wall adjacent to Hamilton Drive and Fish Pond Drive. Details of parts of the original wall include saddleback copings, moulded brick string course and brick plinths.
history: One of a group of similar semi-detached houses built c1886-88, possibly for Frederick Messom, the majority now converted into flats or bed-sit accommodation. Area previously used for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens.

desc: Three storeys above ground including attic storey plus two storey original rear extension and lean-to. Red brick facades with a paler brick used on the two storey extension. Render has been added to the base of the rear extension. Stone lintels, sills and surrounds to original door and window openings now painted over. Brick / terracotta string courses at storey height levels with corbelled brick eaves at rear. Single storey flat roofed, rectangular and canted brick window bays either side of main entrance doorway with stone lintels, shaft mullions and sills. Gabled roof with clay plain tiles and decorative perforated clay ridge tiles. Prominent painted barge boards at gables with corbelled raking brick cornice to gable verges on rear extension. Simple projecting timber eaves to main house. Prominent brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning and corbelled capping. Some original chimney pots have been replaced. Original rainwater fittings have been replaced with plastic. Predominantly sash windows with some original casements on front bay and attic windows. Original door opening in rear extension now partially bricked up to form window. Roof window added. Canopy over main entrance door. Part of the original brick boundary wall has been removed to create access to a car parking area replacing the rear garden. The surviving original brick boundary wall and gate piers adjacent to Hamilton Drive and Peveril Drive have been rendered. Although there have been some alterations, the main body of the house remains mainly intact and there have been no extensions to the original building. The setting of the house has been compromised by the inappropriate and unsightly rendering of the original brick boundary walls adjacent to the roads, creating a visually disturbing element on thestreetscene. The brick garden walls at the rear of the building appear to be original and a fence forms the front garden boundary with 8 Peveril Drive.
history: Semi-detached house (adjacent to 8 Peveril Drive) on a prominent corner plot at the junction of Hamilton Drive and Peveril Drive. Built c1890 on an area previously used as garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens. Part of a group of similarly styled late Victorian villas also including 8, 10, 12 and ‘Parkdale’ on Peveril Drive. Now converted into three flats.

name: Weston Lodge:
desc: Plans by Tom Loach for additions to house for W. Edgar c1905 probably included single storey extension to east facade, plus gablet adjacent to main entrance doorway. Alterations to the original verandah area on the main south facade, including glazed infill screen, may also have occurred at this time. A number of significant additions c1995-2005 including large gabled double garage building and single storey games/garden room abutting enlarged former stable and carriage house. Part of eastern end of garden has been developed to form separate dwelling. Conservatory originally abutted west facing elevation. Red brick facades with ashlar dressings. Prominent features include pair of Flemish gable wings on south facade, flanking former recessed open verandah/balcony area; main entrance doorway with moulded brick surround and semicircular arched head plus adjacent brick oriel window on stone bracket. Other original brick and terracotta features include moulded eaves detail, string courses, segmental arched heads to openings, undersill brackets and aprons plus plinth stretchers. Unpainted original stone details include stone copings to gables, moulded sills to a number of window openings plus step to main entrance door. Gabled and hipped roof. Existing slates probably replaced original plain tiles. Partially rebuilt red brick chimney stacks with panels, brick reliefs, moulded brick string course and corbelled head. Buff ‘crown’ chimney pots. Generally double hung sash windows with small pane glazing bars in upper sash. Casements with leaded lights to flat roofed window bay at corner of later extension plus screen infill to former verandah area. A number of bulls eye and other decorative small windows. Panelled and half glazed double door to main entrance with semicircular head. Some recent alterations and additions to windows and doors. Original garden area enclosed by brick boundary wall now increased in height in some areas. Original drive and gated entrances on Lenton Road now bricked up and new wide vehicular entrance with piers recently formed on Hardwick Road.
history: Detached, mainly single storey house built 1889 on triangular plot enclosed by Lenton Road, Fiennes Crescent and Hardwick Road. In single household use.

desc: House forms central part of single building also including 3 Park Drive and 3 Holles Crescent. Original house now converted into flats. Three storeys plus basement/cellars. Red brick facades with stone dressings. Decorative brickwork features include buff brick string courses and bands of small, recessed rectangular buff brick panels. Two storey canted window bay on front facade with slate, hipped roof. Bull nose brick detail at eaves and gable verges. Projecting brick plinth at base of main facade with stone capping. Combination of rectangular and semicircular arched openings with stone surrounds to original doors and windows. Stonework detailing generally plain with splay reveals, plus mouldings to cornice detail on bay and to main entrance doorway. Stonework now painted. Slate main pitched roof and rear wing with clay ridge tiles and lead valleys. Gabled dormer on front facade. Red brick chimney stack with stone capped plinth, buff brick string course plus small recessed rectangular panels, corbelled brick head and stone cap. Buff ’crown‘ chimney pots with some missing or replaced. Roof window added. Original c.i. rainwater fittings have been replaced and further pipework added. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Brick and stone boundary wall to the adjacent roads. Brick gate piers with stone plinths and caps have been rendered and painted. Small front garden and enclosed yard at rear.
history: Part of group of similarly styled houses on triangular plot of land enclosed by Lenton Road, Park Drive and Holles Crescent. Built c1865-70, possibly by John Loverseed with architects Hine & Evans.

desc: End part of single building, also including 3 Park Drive and 1 Holles Crescent. Original house now converted into flats. Three storeys plus basement/cellars. A number of alterations including plans by Michael Moss c1962 for A.T. Horbury. Three flat roofed garages added in part of garden adjacent to Holles Crescent. Red brick facades with stone dressings. Decorative brickwork features include buff brick string courses, bands of small recessed rectangular buff brick panels plus buff brick diaper pattern on entrance facade, now badly disfigured by insertion of later window opening. Bull nose brick detail at eaves and gable verges. Two storey canted window bays on front and side facades with slate hipped roofs. Projecting brick plinth at base of facades with stone capping. Combination of rectangular openings, some with segmental or semicircular arched heads. Stone surrounds to original doors and windows. Stonework generally plain with splay detail at reveals and lintels, plus mouldings to cornice detail on bays and main entrance doorway. Much of the stonework survives in its natural unpainted finish. Double pitched slate roof with gables. Clay ridge tiles (partly replaced) and lead valleys. Original red brick chimney stack partly taken down to just above ridge level. Flat roof dormer windows added, destroying decorative brickwork at eaves level. Roof window added. Original c.i. rainwater fittings replaced with further pipes added. Some original sash windows remain; others have been altered or replaced. New window openings formed. Brick and stone boundary wall and gate piers to Holles Crescent. Part of the original wall lost when garages were added.
history: Part of group of similarly styled houses on triangular plot of land enclosed by Lenton Road, Park Drive and Holles Crescent. Built c1865-70, possibly by John Loverseed with architects Hine & Evans.

desc: Three storeys plus basement/cellars. Flat roofed garage/extension added. Also, small flat roofed extension adjacent to dining room window. Red brick facades with stone dressings. Decorative brickwork features include buff brick string courses, bands of small, rectangular buff brick recessed panels and buff brick diaper pattern on entrance facade. Bull nose brick detail at eaves and gable verges. Two storey canted window bays on front and side facades with hipped slate roofs. Projecting brick plinth at base of facades with stone capping. Combination of rectangular openings, some with segmental or semicircular arched heads. Stone surrounds to original doors and windows. Stonework generally plain with splay detail at reveals and lintels plus mouldings to cornice detail on bays and main entrance doorway. Much of the stonework survives in its original, natural and unpainted finish. Double pitched slate roofs at varying levels with gables. Clay ridge tiles and lead valleys. Majority of original red brick and stone chimney stacks rebuilt on original plinths. Original buff ‘crown’ chimney pots replaced. Original c.i. rainwater fittings replaced and further pipes added. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Six panelled, half-glazed main entrance door with semicircular overlight. Original brick and stone boundary wall, (together with boundary wall of No.8 Lenton Road), in prominent position at junction of Lenton Road and Holles Crescent. Original brickwork above stone plinth on boundary wall has now been rendered and painted. Similarly, original brick and stone gate piers also rendered. Original boundary wall gate. Part of original boundary wall lost when garage was added.
history: Part of group of similarly styled houses on triangular plot of land enclosed by Lenton Road, Park Drive and Holles Crescent. Built c1865-70, possibly by John Loverseed with architects Hine & Evans. One of a pair of houses (with 8 Lenton Road). In single household use.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with two storey rear wing, now extended. Plain red brick facades with little or no ornamentation. Facing bricks on front facade and chimney stacks with commons elsewhere. The character of the red brick terrace has been adversely affected by the unsightly rendering and painting of parts of the facades, particularly at plinth level on the window bays and adjacent to the entrance porch. Single storey canted window bay on front elevation with hipped slate roof. Recessed porch to main entrance doorway. Stone lintels and sills to window openings and shaped stone lintel over entrance porch. Much of the stonework has now been painted. Gabled slate roof with decorative clay ridge tiles and finial. Lead valleys. Original cast iron rainwater fittings have been replaced. Hipped slate roof to canted bay on front facade with lead roll hips and flashings. Projecting timber eaves with bargeboards at gables. Originally, corbelled brick chimney stack on side elevation with buff fireclay louvre pots, now removed. Originally, sash windows generally, many of which survive. Roof windows added. Small back garden / yard area, with communal passage to provide rear access to each of the houses in the terrace, now partly altered. Original railings on brick and stone plinth to front boundary on Hope Drive now replaced with low brick wall. A small section of the original iron railings including a gate survives at the entrance to 2 and 4 Hope Drive.
history: One of a group of four houses forming a terrace (2-8 Hope Drive), built c1888-89 possibly for Frederick Messom with architect Watson Fothergill. Area previously used for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with two storey rear wing, now extended. Plain red brick facades with little or no ornamentation. Facing bricks on front facade and chimney stacks with commons elsewhere. The character of the red brick terrace has been adversely affected by the unsightly rendering and painting of parts of the facades, particularly at plinth level on the window bays and adjacent to the entrance porch. Single storey canted window bay on front elevation with hipped slate roof. Recessed porch to main entrance doorway. Stone lintels and sills to window openings and shaped stone lintel over entrance porch. Much of the stonework has now been painted. Slate roof with gabled dormer. Decorative clay ridge tiles and finial. Original cast iron rainwater fittings have been replaced. Hipped slate roof to canted bay on front facade with lead roll hips and flashings. Projecting timber eaves with bargeboards at gables. Corbelled brick chimney stack with buff fireclay louvre pots. Originally, sash windows generally, now replaced with casements etc. Roof windows added. Small back garden / yard area, with communal passage to provide rear access to each of the houses in the terrace, now partly altered. Original railings on brick and stone plinth to front boundary on Hope Drive have been removed. Only a small section of the original iron railings plus a gate survive at the entrances to 2 and 4 Hope Drive.
history: One of a group of four houses forming a terrace (2-8 Hope Drive), built c1888-89 possibly for Frederick Messom with architect Watson Fothergill. Area previously used for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens. In single household use.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with two storey rear wing. Plain red brick facades with little or no ornamentation. Facing bricks on front facade and chimney stacks with commons elsewhere. The character of the red brick terrace has been adversely affected by the unsightly rendering and painting of parts of the facades, particularly at plinth level on the window bays and adjacent to the entrance porch. Single storey canted window bay on front elevation with hipped slate roof. Recessed porch to main entrance doorway. Stone lintels and sills to window openings and shaped stone lintel over entrance porch. Much of the stonework has now been painted. Slate roof with gabled dormer on front facade. Decorative clay ridge tiles and finial. Original cast iron
history: One of a group of four houses forming a terrace (2-8 Hope Drive), built c1888-89 possibly for Frederick Messom with architect Watson Fothergill. Area previously used for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens. Now converted into flats or shared accommodation.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with projecting wing on front facade and two storey rear wing. Plain red brick facades with little or no ornamentation. Facing bricks on front facade and chimney stacks with commons elsewhere. The character of the red brick terrace has been adversely affected by the unsightly rendering and painting of parts of the facades, particularly at plinth level on the window bays and adjacent to the entrance porch. Corbelled brick splayed corner detail on front wing. Later outside door added to former open recessed porch to main entrance doorway. Stone lintels and sills to window openings and shaped stone lintel over entrance porch. Much of the stonework has now been painted. Gabled slate roof with decorative clay ridge tiles and finial. Lead valleys. Original cast iron rainwater fittings have been replaced. Projecting timber eaves with bargeboards at gables. Corbelled brick chimney stacks on side elevation with buff fireclay louvre pots, now mainly removed. Originally, sash windows generally, now replaced plus numerous alterations and additions to openings. Dormer window and fire escape added at rear. Roof windows added. Small back garden / yard area, with communal passage to provide rear access to each of the houses in the terrace, now partly altered. Original railings on brick and stone plinth to front boundary on Hope Drive now replaced with low brick wall. A small section of the original iron railings including a gate survives at the entrances to 2 and 4 Hope Drive.
history: One of a group of four houses forming a terrace (2-8 Hope Drive), built c1888-89 possibly for Frederick Messom with architect Watson Fothergill. Area previously used for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens. In single household use.

desc: Red brick with gabled slate roofs. Mainly two storeys with a number of alterations and additions including flat roofed, first floor infill with slate hanging between gabled wings on road front and alterations to original gated opening. Also various removals, additions and alterations in former stable yard area including garaging for cars. Prominent pair of gabled wings on road front, now partly altered, with blue brick plinth band, moulded brick/terracotta banding at storey height level and moulded detailing with dentillation at gable verges. Square brick piers at main entrance with adjacent later door openings. Single window opening to each gable on road front with round arched heads and shaped brick reveals. Later windows at ground floor level. Original stable buildings in yard area now much altered with some demolition and a number of extensions. Apart from small areas of brickwork including sawtooth details at eaves, much of original facades now rendered or painted. Original doors and windows replaced or altered plus a number of additional openings. Gabled slate roof with later roof windows and dormers. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Small outdoor amenity space replaces part of former access drive from Hamilton Drive.
history: Formerly mews c1890, now converted into a number of separate dwellings with shared courtyard. Area, previously known as Fish Pond Gardens, had been established as garden allotments c1792-94 when soil removed from foundations of Army barracks at north end of The Park was used to infill long neglected Castle fishpond. Originally, mews yard could be accessed from Hamilton Drive as well as Hope Drive.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with rear wing plus outbuilding / privy in rear garden area. No significant extensions to the original building. Red brick facades with facings on front elevation plus chimney stacks and commons elsewhere. Two storey canted window bay with hipped tiled roof on front facade and gabled dormer above. Corbelled brick / terracotta detailing at eaves and gable verges with moulded brick string course at storey height level and recessed brick panels under sills on canted bay. Lancet arched heads to main entrance porch plus first floor and gabled dormer windows on front facade with brick voussoirs and hoodmoulds. Stone dressings to door and window openings on front facade including keystones and imposts to arched openings plus lintels and sills. The stonework has now been painted.
history: One of a group of eight similar semi-detached and terraced houses built c1888-90, possibly for Frederick Messom. Area had been previously used, since the beginning of the 19c, for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens. In single household use.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with rear wing plus outbuilding / privy in rear garden area. No significant extensions to the original building. Red brick facades with facings on front elevation plus chimney stacks and commons elsewhere. Two storey canted window bay with hipped tiled roof on front facade and gabled dormer above. Corbelled brick / terracotta detailing at eaves and gable verges with moulded brick string course at storey height level and recessed brick panels under sills on canted bay. Lancet arched heads to main entrance porch plus first floor and gabled dormer windows on front facade with brick voussoirs and hoodmoulds. Stone dressings to door and window openings on front facade including keystones and imposts to arched openings plus lintels and sills. The stonework has now been painted. Saddleback roof. Originally, plain tiles with decorative perforated ridge tiles, now replaced with concrete tiles and ridges. Originally, brick chimney stacks with string course and corbelled red and blue brick caps plus buff ‘crown’ pots. Stacks now removed or partly taken down. Most of the original sash windows have been replaced or altered. Originally, six panelled partly glazed main entrance door. Original cast iron rainwater fittings replaced and further downpipes and waste pipes added. Small rear garden area with boundary wall adjacent to rear gardens of houses on Hamilton Drive. Original brick boundary wall and gate piers with stone caps to front garden, partly altered.
history: One of a group of eight similar semi-detached and terraced houses built c1888-90, possibly for Frederick Messom, now converted into flats. Area had been previously used, since the beginning of the 19c, for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens.

desc:
Three storeys including attic space with rear wing, plus outbuilding / privy in rear garden area. No significant extensions to the original building. Red brick facades with facings on front elevation plus chimney stacks and commons elsewhere. Two storey canted window bay with hipped plain tile roof on front facade and gabled dormer above. Corbelled brick / terracotta detailing at eaves and gable verges plus string course and decorative foliate patterned brick tiles at storey height level and under sills on canted bay. Semicircular arched head to main entrance porch on front facade with stone dressings and hoodmoulds. Stone dressings to door and window openings on front facade including lintels and sills. All the stonework has now been painted. Saddleback roof with plain tiles and decorative, perforated ridge tiles. Originally, brick chimney stacks with string course and corbelled red and blue brick caps plus buff ‘crown’ pots. Stacks now removed or partly taken down. Original sash windows replaced or altered. Originally, six panelled partly glazed main entrance door. Original cast iron rainwater fittings replaced and further
downpipes and waste pipes added. Small rear garden area
with boundary wall adjacent to rear gardens of houses on Hamilton Drive. Original brick boundary wall and gate piers with stone caps to front garden area survive or partly rebuilt.

Small rear garden area with boundary wall adjacent to rear gardens of houses on Hamilton Drive. Original brick boundary wall and gate piers with stone caps to front garden area.
history: One of a group of eight similar semi-detached and terraced houses built c1888-90, possibly for Frederick Messom, now converted into flats. Area had been previously used, since the beginning of the 19c, for garden allotments, known as Fishpond Gardens.

desc: Three storeys including attic space with rear wing plus outbuilding / privy in rear garden area. No significant extensions to the original building. Red brick facades with facings on front elevation plus chimney stacks and commons elsewhere. Two storey canted window bay with hipped tiled roof on front facade and gabled dormer above. Corbelled brick / terracotta detailing at eaves and gable verges with decorative, foliate patterned brick tile string course at storey height level and under sill panels on canted bay. Small rear garden area with boundary wall adjacent to rear gardens of houses on Hamilton Drive. Original brick boundary wall and gate piers with stone caps to front garden area.
history: One of a group of eight similar semi-detached and terraced houses built c1888-90, possibly for Frederick Messom, now converted into flats. Area had been previously used, since the beginning of the 19c, for garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens.

desc: Hipped, slate roofed conservatory / garden room added to facade overlooking garden. Glazed porch to rear entrance added c1985-90 and single storey, toilet side extension, with monopitched plain tile roof, built 1998. Although previously converted into two flats the house is once again in single household use. Three storeys including attic plus basement / cellars. Part rendered and part red brick facades with white painted rendered areas at first floor level. Brick at ground floor level. Brick segmental arches and brick sills to a number of window openings on brick facades with a variety of moulded brick / terracotta string courses and other details including stop detail at abutment with rendered facade at first floor storey height level. Stone surrounds to window openings on bays and to sills on other openings. Stonework now painted. Blue brick damp proof course. Prominently jettied gables with decorative half–timbering over pair of two storey canted window bays. Gabled roof with clay plain tiles, ridge tiles and valley tiles. Painted wood bargeboards to gables and projecting eaves and fascias elsewhere, with decorative iron strap gutter brackets. A number of original c.i. rainwater fittings remain. Red brick chimney stacks with plain and decorative string courses and blue brick cap. Many original chimney pots remain. A combination of original sash and casement windows, with and without glazing bars. Elaborately shaped painted wood framework to entrance / porch doorway, half glazed with leaded lights and panelled wood stallrisers either side of half-glazed panelled doors. Leaded light panels to inner glazed door screen at main entrance. Original Bulwell stone boundary wall with gate piers and original brick garden wall adjacent to No.2 Huntingdon Drive.
history: One of a pair of similar Edwardian detached houses on Huntingdon Drive (Nos. 1 and 2) c1908. Also known as ‘Barton House’. Possibly by Lawrence Bright & Son Architects. Later Arts and Crafts with Art Nouveau details. House similar in style to a number of other detached houses on Huntingdon Drive, also by Lawrence Bright & Son. Plan of garage building prepared for Horatio James c1926.

desc: Pitched roof single garage building also added. (For Claude Newham c1928-40 ?). Plans for conversion to create separate flat c1950 by Caparne Baldry and Gabb for Herbert Collier. House once again in single household use. Three storeys including attic plus basement / cellars. Part rendered and part red brick facades with white painted rendered areas, mainly at first floor level. Brick at ground level and to some first floor areas. Brick segmental arches and brick sills to a number of window openings on brick facades with moulded brick / terracotta brick stop detail at abutment with render at first floor storey height level. Stone sills to some openings, now painted. Blue brick damp proof course. Prominently jettied gable with decorative half–timbering over pair of canted window bays on garden facing facade. Gabled roof with clay plain tiles and ridge tiles. Painted wood bargeboards to gables and projecting eaves and fascias elsewhere, with decorative iron strap gutter brackets. Original c.i. rainwater fittings replaced. Red brick chimney stacks with plain and moulded string courses. Many original red cannonhead chimney pots remain. Roof windows added. Combination of original sash and casement windows, with and without glazing bars. Elaborately shaped painted wood framework to entrance / porch doorway, half glazed with leaded lights and panelled stallrisers either side of half-glazed panelled doors. Half glazed door from each bay facing the garden. Casement windows in half-timbered gables may be a later addition. Parts of original Bulwell stone boundary wall and gate piers adjacent to Huntingdon Drive. Original stone boundary wall to Tattershall Drive and brick garden walls adjacent to Nos.1 and 3 Huntingdon Drive.
history: One of a pair of similar Edwardian detached houses on Huntingdon Drive (Nos. 1 and 2) c1908. Possibly by Lawrence Bright & Son Architects. Later Arts and Crafts with Art Nouveau details. House similar in style to a number of detached houses on Huntingdon Drive, also by Lawrence Bright & Son. Single storey side extension with pitched roof, possibly by Swann & Wright Architects for Robert Walker, c1922.

desc: Significant external alterations include removal of conical turret above first floor oriel window on garden facing facade, addition of garage building replacing part of original front boundary wall plus glazed lean-to extension between rear of garage and main building. Four storeys including attic and basement / cellars. Red brick facades with variety of decorative features including plain and moulded, red and blue brick string courses, brick segmental arched heads to windows, stone dressings to openings and half-timbered framework adjacent to the dormer windows. Relatively plain road and side facades, in contrast to garden facing facade with timber framed oriel window, plain tiled canopy over box window bay to ground and basement crowned with low decorative timber balustrading and canted window bay at first floor directly above. Complex plain tiled roof with combination of gables, valleys and hips. Clay ridge tiles with bonnet hip tiles and finials over dormers. Iron cresting to top section of roof facing the garden, but now missing from similar position on roof facing Huntingdon Drive. Hipped roofs to dormers, window bays and canopy. Overhanging eaves and projecting verges at gable with timber post and tie. Ornate red and blue brick chimney stacks with variety of decorative brick and terracotta details including string courses, corbelling, patterning and reliefs. Original chimney pots. Combination of original windows, mainly sash and casements. Glazing bars with small panes to upper part of a number of windows. Original brick and stone doorway to glazed entrance passage on Huntingdon Drive boundary, now partly concealed by overgrown shrubbery. Original external store building abutting pavement, now altered but retaining original hipped plain tile roof with finials. Original brick boundary wall adjacent to Tattershall Drive.
history: One of a pair of semi-detached houses c1888-9 by prominent local architect Watson Fothergill. Originally in single household use but now converted into flats.

desc: Significant external alterations include removal of conical turret above first floor oriel window on garden facing facade, conversion of former entrance passage doorway area into a garage plus single storey extension. External fire escape staircase added. Four storeys including attic and basement / cellars. Red brick facades with variety of decorative features including plain and moulded red and blue brick string courses, brick segmental arched heads to windows, stone dressings to openings and half-timbered framework adjacent to dormer windows. Relatively plain road and side facades, in contrast to garden facing facade with timber framed oriel window, plain tiled canopy over box window bay to ground floor and basement crowned with decorative low timber balustrading and canted window bay at first floor directly above. Complex plain tiled roof with combination of gables, valleys and hips. Clay ridge tiles with bonnet hip tiles and finials over dormers. Iron cresting to top section of roof facing the garden, but now missing from similar position on roof facing Huntingdon Drive. Hipped roofs to dormers, window bays and canopy. Overhanging eaves and projecting verges at gable with timber post and tie. Ornate red and blue brick chimney stacks with variety of decorative brick and terracotta details including string courses, corbelling, patterning and reliefs. Original chimney pots. Combination of original windows, mainly sash and casements. Glazing bars with small panes to upper part of a number of windows. Original brick external store building abutting pavement, now altered and rendered but retaining original hipped plain tile roof with finials. Original brick boundary wall adjacent to Tattershall Drive.
history: One of a pair of semi-detached houses c1888-9 by prominent local architect Watson Fothergill. Originally in single household use but now converted into flats.

desc: First garage at Napier House planned by Woodhouse and Company for Harry Ritchie c1932-36 with consequent loss of original brick boundary wall to Huntingdon Drive. Single storey glazed lean-to extension added between house and garage building. In single household use. Three storeys including attic plus basement. Originally, part rendered and part red brick facades with white painted rendered areas, mainly at first floor level. Brick mainly to ground floor but some former brickwork areas now rendered or painted. Brick quoins to rendered areas at first floor level and corbelled brick stop detail at abutment with render at first floor storey height level. Half-timbered styling to rear gable and upper parts of garden front. Two storey canted window bay facing garden plus first floor oriel window on single timber bracket. Single storey, flat roofed, octagonal bay window at corner of side and rear facades. Possibly added later. Clay plain tiles and ridge tiles to gabled roof. Painted wood bargeboards to gables and projecting wood eaves and fascias. Flat roofed dormer window overlooking garden. Red brick chimney stacks with decorative stone bands and projecting stone caps. Mainly red clay louvre type chimney pots. Combination of sash and casement windows plus prominent box shaped oriel window over main entrance doorway, supported on single wood bracket, with projecting flat roof. Glazing bars and leaded lights to some casements. Original main entrance doorway with twin glazed apertures and projecting flat roofed canopy. Original brick boundary wall and copings adjacent to Tattershall Drive.
history: One of a number of similar Edwardian houses on Huntingdon Drive c1906-8, probably by Lawrence Bright & Son Architects. Later Arts and Crafts with Art Nouveau details. Formerly known as ‘Napier House’.

desc: Single storey extensions and alterations. Plan prepared by W. Higginbotham for garage for Percy Johnson c1922 replacing original brick boundary wall. Later replacement garage building by architect John Marshall c1977, including rebuilding of boundary wall on Tattershall Drive. Plans for alterations for P.Johnson c1920 / 30 with inglenook by architect Wilson & Rigley c1925. Conservatory added c1992, possibly in position of original veranda. Three storeys including attic plus basement. Originally, part rendered and part red brick facades with white painted rendered areas mainly at first floor level. Originally, brick to all ground floor facades but now rendered or painted. Brick quoins to some rendered areas at first floor level and corbelled brick stop detail at abutment with render at first floor storey height level. Half - timbered styling to rear gable and parts of garden front. Two storey canted window bay facing the garden. First floor oriel window on road front. Original clay plain tiles and ridge tiles to gabled roof. Painted wood bargeboards to gables with projecting wood eaves and fascias. Original dormer window overlooking garden. Red brick chimney stacks with decorative stone bands and projecting stone caps. Upper part of one stack has been removed. Red clay louvre type chimney pots. Mainly casement windows including a number of alterations and replacements. Prominent box shaped oriel window over main entrance doorway, with projecting flat roof and supported on wood bracket. Glazing bars and leaded lights to some casements. Original main entrance doorway with twin glazed apertures and projecting flat roofed canopy. Original brick boundary wall and copings adjacent to Tattershall Drive.
history: One of a number of similar Edwardian houses on Huntingdon Drive c1906-8, probably by Lawrence Bright & Son Architects. Later Arts and Crafts with Art Nouveau details. Original plan of house by Bright & Son for Carr & Co.1906. Also named ‘Avoca’. In single household use.

desc: Two flat conversion scheme including small extension and garages prepared by Michael Moss Architect for A.T. Horbury c1966. Later glazed, single storey, small lean-to building abuts house behind garage building. Pair of flat roofed garages with drive access replaces original boundary wall adjacent to Huntingdon Drive. House now converted back into single household use. Three storeys including attic plus basement. Originally, part rendered and part red brick facades with white painted rendered areas mainly at first floor level. Brick facades at ground floor. Brick quoins to rendered areas at first floor level and corbelled brick stop detail at abutment with render at first floor storey height level. Half - timbered styling to rear gable and upper parts of garden front. Two storey canted window bay facing garden and first floor balcony with originally, wood balustrading, now replaced with metal railings. Original clay plain tiles and ridge tiles to roof replaced with non-matching type. Painted wood bargeboards to gables and projecting wood eaves and fascias. Original dormer window overlooking garden. Red brick chimney stacks with decorative stone bands and projecting stone caps. One stack altered and original cap missing. Red clay louvre type chimney pots with some replacements. Mainly casement windows plus prominent box shaped oriel window over main entrance doorway, with projecting flat roof and supported on wood bracket. Glazing bars and leaded lights to some casements. Oculus window opening in gable facing Huntingdon Drive. Original main entrance doorway with projecting flat roofed canopy. Original brick boundary wall and copings adjacent to Tattershall Drive.
history: One of a number of similar Edwardian houses on Huntingdon Drive c1906-8, probably by Lawrence Bright & Son Architects. Later Arts and Crafts with Art Nouveau details. Formerly known as Ravenswood.

desc: A number of later small side extensions plus single garage building facing Huntingdon Drive replacing part of original brick boundary wall. In single household use. Three storeys including attic plus basement. Originally, part rendered and part red brick facades with white painted rendered areas mainly at first floor level. Brick facades to ground floor areas on front road now rendered or painted. Brick quoins to rendered areas at first floor level and corbelled brick stop detail at abutment with render at first floor storey height level. Half - timbered styling to gables and first floor of garden facing facade. Twin, two storey, flat roofed, bow window bays facing the garden. Canted oriel window at first floor level on road facing facade. Clay plain tiles and ridge tiles to gabled roof. Painted wood bargeboards to gables and projecting wood eaves and fascias. Dormer window overlooking garden. Red brick chimney stacks with decorative stone bands and projecting stone caps. Mainly red clay louvre type chimney pots, one replaced with gas flue terminal. Combination of sash and casement windows with box shaped bay over main entrance doorway. Leaded lattice lights to many casements. Original brick boundary wall and copings adjacent to Tattershall Drive.
history: One of a number of similar Edwardian houses on Huntingdon Drive c1906-8, probably by Lawrence Bright & Son Architects. Later Arts and Crafts with Art Nouveau details.

desc: Original brick boundary wall to Huntingdon Drive now completely removed and replaced with large flat roofed double carport and open car park area c1980. In single household use. Three storeys including attic plus basement. Part rendered and part red brick facades with white painted rendered areas mainly at first floor level. Original brick facades to ground floor areas, now partly rendered or painted. Brick quoins to rendered areas at first floor level and corbelled brick stop detail at abutment with render at first floor storey height level. Pair of flat roofed, single storey canted window bays at ground floor level facing the garden with canted oriel windows on timber brackets at first floor level. Single door in each ground floor bay with access to garden via stepped platform. Original clay plain tiles and ridge tiles to gabled roof replaced with non-matching type. Painted wood bargeboards to gables with projecting wood eaves and fascias. Original dormer window overlooking garden, with ‘Venetian like’ semicircular arched head to central casement. Original red brick chimney stacks completely altered and rendered. Original c.i. rainwater fittings replaced. Combination of sash and casement windows with prominent box shaped oriel window over main entrance doorway, with projecting flat roof and supported on single wood bracket. Glazing bars and leaded lattice lights to some casements. A number of alterations to doors and windows. Original main entrance doorway with twin circular glazed apertures, overlight, and projecting flat roofed canopy, supported on wooden brackets. Original brick boundary wall and copings adjacent to Tattershall Drive.
history: One of a number of similar Edwardian houses on Huntingdon Drive c1906-8, probably by Lawrence Bright & Son Architects. Later Arts and Crafts with Art Nouveau details.

desc: Large three storey extension added for Samuel Jackson c1913–14, also by Lawrence Bright, providing a billiard room at basement level, large dining room at ground floor level and day and night nursery at first floor. At same time single storey bow window bay added to original house at ground floor level on road facing facade plus small single storey extension to left of main entrance doorway providing additional lavatory facilities. This small side extension later altered to form separate entrance to upper floor flat as part of previous conversion. House now converted back into single household use. Original brick boundary wall adjacent to Huntingdon Drive partly breached to build flat roofed garage building, possibly by H. D. Schofield c 1950. Original part of house is three storeys including attic plus large basement. Part rendered and part red brick facades with the rendered areas mainly at first floor level. Brick facades to the ground floor and lower ground floor. Some sections of the ground floor facades have also been rendered but this appears to be a later addition. Brick quoins to part of first floor facade adjacent to main entrance and corbelled brick stop detail at abutment with render at first floor storey height level. Red and blue brick plinths at base of facades. Pair of flat roofed, two storey bow window bays on original house facing the garden with three storey bow window bay on side extension. Clay plain tile gabled roof with bargeboards plus conical roof and finial over extension bay. Single flat roofed dormer window overlooking garden. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Red and brick chimney stacks with moulded brick string course and corbel detailing. Original red cannonhead chimney pots. Combination of original sash and casement windows with prominent box and canted oriel windows on timber brackets at first floor level on road front. Glazing bars and ornamental leaded lights to some casements. Original main entrance doorway with twin circular glazed apertures and projecting flat roofed canopy with wood support brackets. Door leading to garden via original stepped platform from each of the original bays. Original brick boundary wall and copings adjacent to Tattershall Drive survives plus section of original wall adjacent to Huntingdon Drive.
history: One of a number of similar Edwardian Houses on Huntingdon Drive c1908, probably by Lawrence Bright & Son Architects. Architectural styling influenced by latter stages of Arts and Crafts movement with Art Nouveau details. Formerly known as Kenmare House.

desc: Two storeys on Kenilworth Road elevation and three storeys on garden front plus attic storey and cellars. Original lean-to main entrance porch at the junction of side and garden elevations has been replaced and first floor open balcony with balustrading, which originally spanned the width of the house overlooking the garden, has been largely removed. Single storey pitched roof extension added to form garage adjacent to Kenilworth Road. Red brick facades and tile hung gables. Moulded brick/terracotta and decorative tile string courses and sills with elaborate shaped brick lintel voussoirs and under sill panels. Plain and moulded stone surrounds, now painted, to a number of openings particularly the canted window bays on the garden facade. Plain tile pitched roof with prominent tile hung gables. Clay ridge tiles with iron finials. Projecting painted wood bargeboards to gables, some with wood ties and carved brackets.Projecting wood eaves, fascia and soffit. Gables on garden elevation jettied over canted window bays below on painted moulded wooden beam supported by prominent carved wooden brackets. Red brick chimney stacks with moulded string course and corbelled caps. A number of the original chimney pots remain. Cast iron rainwater fittings. Mainly casement windows with small pane upper panels. Small pane casement panels replaced by single pane casements to one of the bay windows. Sash windows on ground floor bay. Original curved brick base and steps down from main entrance level to upper garden area with further steps down to lower garden area. Original garden area survives. Original brick garden wall boundary adjacent to ‘Parkdale’ and recent fence separating garden from 3 Kenilworth Road. Original brick garden wall and gate piers with steps to Hamilton Drive. The original brick boundary wall adjacent to Kenilworth Road has been replaced with garage doors, a gated entrance to a yard drive and a pedestrian gate.
history: ‘Iveston’ is one half of a prominent pair of houses built c1888 in an area previously used as garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens. House is again in single household use after previously being tenanted and used as bed-sits. Characteristic fusion of late Victorian domestic revival architectural styles.
style: Late Victorian domestic revical architetural style

desc: Two storeys plus attic storey and converted basement. Kitchen extension added in rear yard area c1980 and replaced by two storey extension c1989. Architect Tony Smith. Utility rooms in basement converted for family residential use c1989. Prefab garage replaced c1989 with brick and slate pitched roof building. Red brick facades with rich variety of brick and stone details. Corbelled bull nose brick detail at eaves and gable verges, forming open pediments, plus a combination of flush or projecting red and blue brick string courses mainly at storey height levels. Projecting brick pilasters at first floor level on west facing wing with pointed arches, brick voussoirs and stone imposts. Pointed brick arches generally to first floor window openings plus stone surrounds to door and window openings on ground floor including splayed corner window with stone colonnette adjacent to main entrance door. Stone band course at first floor level with circular ornamental stone patera panels. Also, quatrefoil shaped window opening within moulded stone oculus surround plus small decorative stone under sill panels. Most of the stonework has now been painted. Gabled roof with slates, clay ridge tiles plus lead valleys and flashings. Original brick chimney stack with corbelled brick cap, buff ‘crown’ pots, brick relief patterning and blue brick
history: Also known as Yew Tree House, built c1870-75 with Hine & Evans as the possible architects. The building forms one half of a pair in a prominent corner position at the junction of Peveril Drive and Kenilworth Road. Single household use.

desc: Characteristic fusion of late Victorian domestic revival architectural styles. Two storeys on Kenilworth Road elevation and three storeys on garden front plus attic storey and cellars. Original lean-to main entrance porch on garden front has been removed and conservatory added to side elevation with entrance portico c1907-1909. First floor open balcony with balustrading, originally spanning the width of the house overlooking the garden, was largely removed some time ago. Red brick facades and tile hung gables. Moulded brick/terracotta and decorative tile string courses and sills with elaborate shaped brick lintel voussoirs and under sill panels. Plain and moulded stone surrounds, now painted, to a number of openings particularly the canted window bays on garden facade. Plain tile pitched roof with prominent tile hung gables. Clay ridge tiles with iron finials. Projecting painted wood bargeboards to gables, some with wood ties and carved brackets. Projecting wood eaves, fascia and soffit. Gables on garden elevation jettied over canted window bays below on painted moulded wooden bressumer beam supported by prominent carved wooden brackets. Red brick chimney stacks with moulded string course and corbelled caps. A number of the original chimney pots remain. Cast iron rainwater fittings with some replacements. Mainly casement windows with small pane upper panels. Some alterations. Some casements replaced by leaded lights or fixed glazed panels. Sash windows on ground floor bay. Some alterations to openings particularly on the ground floor of the two storey wing adjacent to Kenilworth Road. Original curved brick base and steps down from main entrance level to upper garden area with further steps down to lower garden area. Original garden area survives. Rebuilt brick garden boundary wall adjacent to 5 Kenilworth Road and recent fence separating garden from 1 Kenilworth Road. Original brick garden wall and gate piers with steps to Hamilton Drive. The original brick boundary wall and gate piers adjacent to Kenilworth Road survive.
history: Also known as ‘Kenilworth House’ the house forms one of a prominent pair built c1888 in an area previously used as garden allotments known as Fishpond Gardens. In single household use.

desc: Red brick with ashlar dressings and slate hipped roof. Three storeys plus attic storey. Sloping site with entrance at Kenilworth Road level and lower ground floor/basement at garden level. Original lower garden area adjacent to Hamilton Drive now developed for separate residential use. Original outbuildings on either side of main house now extended, altered and converted for garage and separate residential use, including removal of large glasshouse which originally abutted east facing facade. Round arched main entrance doorway on road front. To right, large stone framed staircase/hall window opening with gabled dormer window above. Pair of three storey canted window bays on garden front flanking central doorway. Single window openings generally. Decorative brick banding at eaves level with string courses and square inset panels. Also, brick shafts to window reveals and bullnose detail at eaves and gable verges. Stonework features include moulded surrounds to door and window openings on road front, including tracery foil details to staircase/hall window and pointed arched window in gabled dormer, plus separate lintels and sills with moulded details to a number of single window openings. Stone head and sill bands to window openings at each storey level on garden front. Also, stone copings to gables and chimney stacks. Hipped slate roof with gables/gabled dormers. A number of flat roofed dormer windows added. Prominent ridge and side wall red brick chimney stacks with string courses, corbelled caps and buff crown chimney pots. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Gutter brackets. Combination of plain and glazing bar sash windows with a number of replacements and additions. Leaded lights to large staircase/hall window on road front. Panelled double leaf main entrance door with round arched head. Half glazed panelled door on garden front. Original boundary wall and gate piers on road front with coursed rubble stone plinth, red brick wall with piers, brackets and blue brick banding plus saddleback copings. Brick retaining walls, including round arched openings at main level changes in garden.
history: Detached house c1865-70, possibly by architects Hine & Evans. Now converted into flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement. Red brick with stone dressings and hipped slate roof. Substantial part of original garden area to side of original house and adjacent to Hardwick Road lost to separate residential development c1970. Plan by Swan and Wright for conversion of stables to garage for Godfrey Gadd c1930. (Stables/garage building located on opposite side of Lenton Avenue, now demolished and replaced with terraced residential development c1970). Also, plan for alterations for Denis Greensmith c1960. Original house substantially enlarged with two storey brick and hipped slate roof addition to north facing facade plus single storey, flat roofed extension adjacent to Lenton Road. Two storey canted and rectangular window bays on main front overlooking garden. Decorative brick detail at eaves with shaped brackets and inset blue brick panels. Also bands of alternating blue brick stretchers on facades with red and blue brick voussoirs to decorative arches over some window openings. Stone surrounds to a number of window openings plus head and sill bands on bays. Elsewhere, chamfered stone lintels and sills to other openings. Stonework now painted. Slate hipped roof with clay hip and ridge tiles and overhanging eaves. Lead hips to slate roofs over bay windows. Alterations to original red brick chimney stack. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Sash windows generally, many with glazing bars. Some original openings removed or altered during alteration and extension work. New openings added. Original red brick boundary wall with plinth, piers and saddleback copings, now partly altered, rebuilt or removed during later alterations and additions. House name inscribed in stone cap to gate pier on Lenton Road.
history: Detached house c1870-75, also named Glenelg. In single household use.

desc: Originally red brick with hipped slate roof similar to adjacent house at No.5 Lenton Avenue. Now all facades have been rendered and painted. In single household use. House and garden originally occupied full plot between Lenton Avenue and Hardwick Road. Substantial part of garden adjacent to Hardwick Road developed for separate residential use c1965. Gated opening on Hardwick Road originally provided access to main entrance to house facing garden. Late 20c single and two storey additions, extending to Lenton Avenue boundary. These later buildings replaced large original conservatory and other smaller outbuildings. Pair of single storey, flat roofed canted window bays facing garden, flanking central round arched doorway, formerly used as main entrance. Original single storey flat roofed brick entrance porch facing road, now altered and rendered. Originally, red brick facades with ashlar dressings, now completely obscured by render and paint. Original brick details beneath render and paint include ornamental brackets and tiled frieze at eaves level plus moulded voussoirs to garden facing doorway. Stone surrounds to original door and window openings with chamfered reveals, cornices and various moulding details. Stone mullions to original window pairs at first floor level plus floral capped stone pilasters to original door to garden with hoodmoulding and keystone. Stone lintels and sills to original window openings. All surviving stonework now rendered or painted. Hipped slate roof with clay ridge and hip tiles. Two pairs of side wall chimney stacks. Original caps to stacks and pots altered or taken down. Originally, mainly plain sash windows, but some with glazing bars including round arched opening to staircase window facing Lenton Avenue. Originally, red brick boundary wall to Lenton Avenue, now altered and rendered. Originally, mews on opposite side of Lenton Avenue, possibly associated with house. Mews demolished and replaced with terraced residential development c1970-80.
history: Detached two storey house with cellars c1865-75.

desc: Red brick with hipped slate roof. In single household use. House and garden originally occupied full plot between Lenton Avenue and Hardwick Road. Substantial part of garden adjacent to Hardwick Road developed for separate residential use c1965-70. Original main entrance to house was from gated access on Hardwick Road. A number of later side extensions and additions including part pitched and part flat roofed garage/storage buildings c1955-65. Later building adjacent to south facade replaced large original conservatory. Pair of single storey, flat roofed canted window bays facing garden, flanking central round arched former main entrance doorway. Original single storey flat roofed brick entrance porch facing road, now altered and extended with glazed passageway. A number of window opening alterations on road front c1950-60, including ground floor pair either side of porch and to first floor staircase, replacing original round arched sash window. Plain sash window in one canted bay replaced with door and steps leading to garden c1970-80. Red brick facades with ashlar dressings. Brick details include ornamental brackets and tiled frieze at eaves level plus moulded voussoirs to garden doorway. Stone surrounds to original door and window openings with chamfered reveals, cornices and various moulding details. Stone mullions to original window pairs at first floor level above canted bays plus floral capped stone pilasters to original garden doorway with hoodmoulding and keystone. Stone lintels and sills to surviving original window openings on other facades. Stonework now painted. Hipped slate roof with clay ridge and hip tiles. Two pairs of side wall chimney stacks. One stack reduced in height and caps on other stacks have been altered. C.i. rainwater fittings. Original sash windows, mostly plain but some with glazing bars. A number of window alterations. Original pair of shaped, half–glazed panelled doors to entrance from garden. Original doorway to porch facing road replaced. Red brick boundary wall to Lenton Avenue with brick saddleback copings. Originally, mews on opposite side of Lenton Avenue, possibly associated with house. Mews demolished and replaced with terraced residential development c1970-80.
history: Detached two storey house with cellars c1865-75.

desc: Two storey flat roofed extension added c1890, creating new porch/entrance lobby on ground floor. Stables converted for garage use c1923 and subsequently demolished and replaced by ‘town houses’ c1964-65. Originally, large glazed conservatories on both sides of house. Small side extension added (pre-1964) plus lean-to garage c1989/90. Plans prepared by George Goodband for new covered gate entrance off Hardwick Road c1898. Small group of original outbuildings (coal) adjacent to Lenton Road boundary wall now demolished. Previously converted into flats, the building is once again in single household use. Three storeys plus cellars under rear half of house with external access. Red brick facades with stone dressings. Symmetrical garden facing facade with pair of two storey, hipped roof, canted window bays either side of central doorway. Gabled dormers above each bay flanking central turret window with pyramidal roof. Original front facade with symmetrically arranged window openings around central main entrance door now concealed by later two storey extension. Mainly plain brickwork detailing apart from decorative brickwork frieze below eaves on front and rear facades with cruciform shaped inset brick panels, now painted. Decorative inset tiles on canted bays. Brick segmental and semicircular arched heads over some side windows. Splay reveals to brick openings. Some areas of ground floor brickwork have been rendered and painted. Variety of stone dressings to doors and windows including complete surrounds to openings on garden facing facade. Stone ashlar dressed openings, some with arched heads and various mouldings, cornices, pilasters, shaft mullions plus string courses and copings to gable dormers. Cornice detail on one bay has been removed. Generally, stone lintels and sills to brick openings. Stonework now painted. Double pitched slate roof, (re-roofed in 1982), with hipped gables, clay ridge tiles and lead dressings. Iron finials to gable dormers and turret. Dormer window facing Lenton Avenue probably a later addition. Originally, red brick chimney stacks with string course, corbelled head and blue brick plinth bricks and capping. Buff ‘crown’ pots. Two stacks substantially altered. Sash windows generally with some alterations. Coloured glass. Original brick boundary wall to Hardwick Road rebuilt after tree root damage c1994. Brick garden walls adjacent to Nos.5 and 9 Lenton Road survive. Original brick boundary wall to Lenton Avenue substantially altered following addition of two storey front extension and provision of car access to lean to garage. Apart from some additions and alterations, original house with garden and wide range of interesting features and details remains substantially intact. House also makes a prominent impact when viewed from Hardwick Road and Fiennes Crescent.
history: Detached house c1870, originally with separate stable buildings on opposite side of Lenton Avenue.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Red brick with stone dressings and half-hipped slate roof. Large part of original garden area adjacent to Hardwick Road separately developed to create single dwelling c1980. Plans by W.D. Sterland for alterations to house c1959. Two storey brick extension with hipped roof added to road frontage. Steel fire escape platform and staircase from upper flat. Prominent pair of canted window bays with hipped roofs on main front overlooking garden, flanking former main entrance doorway. Flat roofed entrance porch on road front, now altered and rendered. Corbelled brick detail with dentillation at eaves and gable verges. Bull nosed brick detail to gabled dormer verges and heads of canted window bays. Semicircular arched heads to gable windows with brick voussoirs. Inset, decorative buff brick band at eaves level on garden front. Moulded stone surrounds to windows and pointed arch main entrance doorway in central bay on garden front. Elsewhere, chamfered and shaped stone lintels and sills to window openings plus moulded, semicircular arched heads and sill bands to gable windows at attic level. Stonework now painted. Slate half-hipped roof with a number of later roof windows. Gabled dormer to central bay on garden front. Ridges of hipped roofs to bay dormers originally fitted with iron cresting. Red brick side wall chimney stacks with brick relief banding and crown chimney pots. Original rainwater fittings now replaced and further pipework added. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. A number of alterations. Red brick boundary wall with half round copings to Lenton Avenue and central gated opening with square brick piers and later caps. Part of original brick boundary wall adjacent to Hardwick Road removed to provide access to residential development in lower garden area of original house.
history: Detached house 1870-75, possibly by architect T.C.Hine. Now converted into flats.

desc: A number of early 20c side extensions. Plan by Leslie Harris for garage for Claude Stevenson 1913. Part of original garden area to side of house separately developed for residential use c1985, architect Julian Marsh. Two storey original house plus basement and attics. Red brick with stone dressings and slate half-hipped roof. Pair of brick canted window bays on main front overlooking garden with central gabled dormer above. Single storey extension on road front with flat roof and crenellated brick parapet. Corbelled and moulded brick detail at eaves, half–hip gables and gable dormer. Other brick details include moulded surrounds to gable windows with pointed and segmental arched heads, string courses and hoodmoulds plus blue brick band courses. Stone lintels and sills to a number of openings plus head and sill bands to canted bays and kneelers to half-hip gables. Prominent stone mullioned window on road front with brick patterned infill to central panel. Slate half-hipped roof to original house with large central gabled dormer on garden front. Red brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning. One stack has been rebuilt. One stack retains original corbelled head detail, others have been altered. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Sash windows generally, some with single glazing bars. Leaded lights to stone framed window on front road. Original brick gate piers with stone caps on Lenton Avenue boundary. Other sections of original brick boundary wall removed as part of adjacent residential development in garden or opened up to provide alternative gated opening plus access to garage. Sections of Lenton Avenue boundary wall have been rendered. Brick boundary wall with half round copings to Hardwick Road boundary.
history: Detached house c1875. Also known as Fernleigh. In single household use.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Red brick with stone dressings and slate gabled roof with gabled dormers. Road front plain compared to side and garden facing facades. Small yard area adjacent to Lenton Avenue with garden at rear extending to boundary on Hardwick Road. Small original lean-to outbuildings in front yard now removed. Single storey, flat roofed canted and rectangular window bays overlooking garden. Variety of brick/terracotta detail and ornamentation at eaves, gables and string courses plus moulded surrounds to window openings, some with pediments, decorative festoon and panel reliefs, cornices and hoodmoulds. Stonework features include moulded surround to main entrance doorway on side facade plus a number of plain and moulded window sills, bay copings and kneelers to gabled dormer. Stonework now painted. Slate roof with red clay ridge tiles and lead valleys. Originally, perforated clay tiles to all ridges plus finials. Prominent gables and gabled dormers on road and garden fronts with central gable flanked by chimney stacks on side elevation. Original red brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning. Upper part of side wall stacks removed. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Sash windows generally, including a number with glazing bars. Original brick boundary wall to Lenton Avenue plus gated opening with brick piers and stone caps. Rebuilt section of red brick wall with gated opening and brick and stone piers on Hardwick Road boundary.
history: One of a pair of houses c1880, possibly by architects Hine & Son. Now converted into flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Alterations and enlargement of basement area. Full height wing on road front, reducing to single storey adjacent to Lenton Avenue boundary.Red brick with stone dressings and slate gabled roof with gabled dormers. Road front plain compared to side and garden facing facades. Small yard area adjacent to Lenton Avenue with garden at rear extending to boundary on Hardwick Road. Single storey, flat roofed canted and rectangular window bays overlooking garden plus small flat roofed pointed window bay on side facade adjacent to main entrance doorway. Variety of brick/terracotta detail and ornamentation at eaves, gables and string courses plus moulded surrounds to window openings, some with pediments, decorative festoon and panel reliefs, cornices and hoodmoulds. Stonework features include a number of plain and moulded window sills, bay copings and kneelers to gabled dormer. Stonework now painted. Slate roof with red clay ridge tiles and lead valleys. Originally, perforated clay ridge tiles with finials. Prominent gables and gabled dormers on road and garden fronts with central ‘double’ gabled dormer on side elevation. Hipped slate roof to single storey extension adjacent to Lenton Avenue boundary. Roof windows added on main roof. Combination of original red brick chimney stacks, with brick relief patterning, plus later alterations and additions. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars plus a number of minor alterations and additions. Rebuilt section of brick boundary wall to Lenton Avenue, with new opening forming entrance porch and enlarged gated opening with brick and stone piers to allow car access. Rebuilt section of red brick wall with saddleback copings on Hardwick Road boundary.
history: One of a pair of houses c1880, possibly by architects Hine & Son. Now converted into flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Red brick with stone dressings and slate gabled roof. Single storey outbuilding, used as double garage, added late 20c replacing small original outbuilding adjacent to garden wall boundary with No.15.
