Single storey bow and rectangular window bays adjacent to main entrance doorway plus single storey canted and rectangular window bays on other facades. Plain Lenton Avenue facade compared to more exuberant entrance and garden facing facades. Variety of decorative and moulded brick and terracotta detail to gable pediments, eaves, string courses, band courses, voussoirs and to door and window surrounds including a number with pediments and segmental pediments. Also decorative festoon panel reliefs plus circular air vents in gables. Stonework details predominantly on main entrance bay with moulded door and window surrounds, brackets and caps. Semicircular moulded stone arched heads to openings, some with pediments over. Later flat roofed canopy on shaped brackets to main entrance doorway. Also stone sills and sill bands generally. Stone features now painted. Irregular roof with prominent gables, particularly double gable on main front overlooking garden. Lead valleys and decorative clay ridge tiles. Red brick chimney stacks, originally with string courses and corbelled heads. Some stacks altered and original pots replaced. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Combination of sash and casement windows. Sashes mainly at attic and ground storey levels with cross window casements at first floor level, some with glazing bars. Panelled external doors with overlights. House prominently positioned at junction of Lenton Avenue and Maxtoke Road with original brick boundary wall. Coursed rubble stone plinth to sections of wall. Square brick gate piers with stone caps. Upper part of stepped boundary wall rebuilt or raised with original saddleback brick copings reinstated.
history: Detached house c1885, possibly by Hine & Son architects. Also known as Dundee House.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics with part two storey wing on road front. Red brick with ashlar dressings and gabled slate roof. Single storey rectangular window bay facing garden plus single storey canted window bay adjacent to arched main entrance doorway on side facade. Single storey, flat roofed rectangular window bay to right side of main entrance doorway. Full height square extension with flat roof in central courtyard on road front. Decorative brick features at verges and eaves plus string courses, band courses, voussoirs and hoodmoulds. Brick patterning on gable. Part of brickwork at ground floor level in central yard area on road front now rendered. Stone surrounds with splay reveals to ground and first floor windows on garden and side facades. Originally, stone lintels and sills to other windows, a number of which now removed. Also, moulded stone surround with pointed arch head to main entrance doorway plus kneelers to gables and gabled dormer. Stonework now painted. Slate gabled roof with gabled dormer overlooking garden. Flat roofed dormers added. Upper section of red brick chimney stacks removed. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Originally, sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Now a number of alterations, replacements and additions. Sections of original boundary wall with gated openings and brick piers to Lenton Avenue removed to provide access for cars and off street parking. Much of remaining boundary wall to Maxtoke Road and Cavendish Road West survives. Red brick with saddleback copings, blue brick plinth stretchers and coursed rubble stone base.
history: One of a pair of houses c1878-80. Now converted into flats. Steel fire escape platform and ladder added to garden facing facade from top floor flat.

desc: Part of original garden area now developed to create a separate dwelling. Steel fire escape platform and ladder added to garden facing facade from top floor flat. Two storeys plus basement and attics with part two storey wing on road front. Red brick with ashlar dressings and gabled slate roof. Single storey canted window bay and rectangular window bay facing garden plus single storey canted window bay adjacent to arched main entrance doorway on side facade. Full height square extension with flat roof in central courtyard on road front. Decorative brick features at verges and eaves plus string courses, band courses, voussoirs and hoodmoulds. Brick patterning on gables. Part of brickwork at ground floor level in central yard area on road front now rendered. Stone surrounds with splay reveals to ground and first floor windows on garden and side facades. Originally, stone lintels and sills to other windows, a number of which now removed. Also, moulded stone surround with pointed arch head to main entrance doorway plus kneelers to gables and gabled dormer. Stonework now painted. Slate gabled roof with gabled dormer overlooking garden. Flat roofed dormers added. Upper section of red brick chimney stacks removed. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Originally, sash windows generally some with glazing bars. Now a number of alterations, replacements and additions. Only small section of original boundary wall to Lenton Avenue survives. Red brick with saddleback copings, blue brick plinth stretchers and coursed rubble stone base. Other sections of original wall with gated openings and brick piers have been removed to provide access for cars and off street parking. Brick boundary wall to Cavendish Road West.
history: One of a pair of houses c1878-80.Now converted into flats.

desc: Small flat roofed, single storey extension adjacent to main entrance bay on side facade. Previous occupants include local architects Rex Savidge and Bill Crampin. Savidge carried out internal alterations plus a small side extension adjacent to carport. Large carport replaced small original outbuilding adjacent to Lenton Avenue boundary wall. Crampin added glazed single storey, canted bay facing garden and remodelled garden to reduce previous difference in levels. In single household use. Red brick facades with stone dressings. Two storey canted window bay with flat roof facing garden. Prominent gables on all facades with gabled dormer facing garden. Brick details include lancet arched window openings and double string course on front facade, hoodmoulding over windows on side gable and bull nose brick detail at eaves and gable verges. Sections of brickwork at base of facades and in carport has been rendered / painted. Stone dressings to openings generally, including full surrounds to a number of windows and main entrance doorway. Elsewhere, stone lintels and sills to brickwork openings. Stonework now painted. Complex roof form with intersecting double pitches and hips at various levels creating gables on all facades. Slate with red clay decorative ridge tiles and lead valleys and flashings. Red brick chimney stack with string course and corbelled head capping. ‘Crown’ chimney pots. Roof window added. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Some alteration and additions to openings including replacement of original back entrance doorway in front yard area with a window. Original boundary wall and gate piers to Lenton Avenue with stone plinth, blue brick plinth stretchers and red brick wall and copings. Part of front wall taken down or altered to provide opening and enclosure for car port. Original brick boundary wall and gate piers to Cavendish Road West. Apart from some additions and alterations, original house and garden remains substantially intact. House also makes prominent impact when viewed from Cavendish Road West.
history: Part three storey, part two storey detached house and garden occupying plot between Lenton Avenue and Cavendish Road West, c1870.

desc: Two and three storey red brick, hipped and gabled extensions added plus single storey flat roofed extensions between road front and boundary wall to Lenton Avenue. Large part of original garden area converted into car park with enlarged opening in brick boundary wall to Cavendish Road West. Character and distinctiveness of original house adversely affected by over intensive conversion and extension work. Two storeys plus basement and attics to original house. Red brick with stone dressings and hipped and gabled roof with plain tiles. Rectangular window bays to original facade overlooking garden. Flat roofed, canted window bay at ground floor level on left hand side with shaped gabled dormer above at attic level. Flat lead covered roof to two storey right hand bay. Decorative and moulded brick detailing to gable pediments, eaves string courses and door and window surrounds. Semicircular and segmental brick arched heads to original openings, replicated on a number of openings to later extensions. Part of original facing brickwork now rendered and painted. Stone dressings to gabled dormers, string courses plus sills to a number of openings. Hipped and gabled roof to original house with shaped gabled dormers. Similar arrangement of gabled roofs on later extensions. Original house reroofed with plain tiles and decorative ridges tiles to match roof coverings on later extensions. Red brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning, corbelled heads and a number of replacement pots. Dormer window added. Original rainwater fitting replaced and further pipework added. Plain sash windows generally. A number of original openings have been altered by later extension building. Sections of original brick and stone boundary wall and gated openings to Lenton Avenue survive, although partly rendered. Other parts replaced by later extension building.
history: Detached house c1875, now converted into flats with a number of large extensions and alterations c2002.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics with single storey gabled wing on road front. Single storey, pitched roof entrance porch extension added c2000 adjacent to original wing on road front. Red brick with plain tile gabled roof and prominent gabled dormers. Pair of rectangular window bays on garden facing facade with gable and gabled dormer above at attic level. Similar rectangular bay on side facade with main entrance doorway and gabled dormer at attic level. Single storey pointed window bay adjacent to main entrance. Single storey flat roofed rectangular bay on road front. Variety of brick/terracotta detailing and ornamentation including brick cornice at eaves level, dentillation to eaves, gables and gabled dormers, moulded surrounds to door and window openings, some with pediments and segmental pediments, string courses, voussoirs, decorative festoon reliefs and air vents. Stone dressings to a number of openings plus copings to gabled dormers and bays.Steeply pitched plain tile roof with gables and gabled dormers. Perforated, decorative clay ridge tiles and lead valley flashings. Red brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning and corbelled heads, partly altered. Original pots replaced. Replacement of original rainwater fittings and further pipework added. Originally, mainly sash windows, some with glazing bars. A number of window alterations, replacements and additions. Brick boundary wall to Lenton Avenue with saddleback copings and coursed rubble stone plinth. Brickwork now rendered and painted. Brick gate piers with stone plinth and stone caps, also partly rendered and painted. Brick garden wall to Cavendish Road West boundary.
history: One of a pair of houses c1882-83, possibly by architects Hine & Son.Now converted into flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics with single storey gabled wing on road front. Plan by Albert Eberlin for garage for Sir Albert Ball c1930.Red brick with plain tile gabled roof and prominent gabled dormers. Pair of rectangular window bays on garden facing facade with gable and gabled dormer above at attic level. Similar rectangular bay on side facade with main entrance doorway and gabled dormer at attic level. Single storey pointed window bay adjacent to main entrance. Single storey flat roofed rectangular bay on road front. Variety of brick/terracotta detailing and ornamentation including brick cornice at eaves level, dentillation to eaves, gables and gabled dormers, moulded surrounds to door and window openings, some with pediments and segmental pediments, string courses, voussoirs, festoon reliefs and air vents. Stone dressings to a number of openings plus copings to gabled dormers and bays. Steeply pitched plain tile roof with gables and gabled dormers. Perforated, decorative clay ridge tiles and lead valley flashings. Red brick chimney stacks with brick relief patterning and corbelled heads. Part of one of stack removed. Original pots replaced. Flat roofed dormer window added. Replacement of original rainwater fittings and further pipework added. Originally, mainly sash windows, some with glazing bars and added leaded lights. A number of window alterations and additions including oriel window at first floor level. Partially rebuilt brick boundary wall to Lenton Avenue with saddleback copings and coursed rubble stone plinth. Brick gate piers with stone caps. Brick garden wall adjacent to No.31 and to Cavendish Road West boundary.
history: One of a pair of houses c1882-83, possibly by architects Hine & Son.In single household use.

desc: Plans by Cecil Howitt & Partners for flat conversion c1955. Garage plan by Swann & Wright for James Linday c1925. Side extensions and alterations for garaging and access to previous upper floor flat. Two storeys plus attics and cellars. Original single storey, pitched roof extension adjacent to Lenton Avenue. Small original outbuildings, built up against boundary wall to No.33 Lenton Avenue, probably taken down during garage additions. Rendered roughcast facades (previously stucco / pebbledash ?) with stone plinth, door and window surrounds, string courses and other decorative features. The stonework has been coated. Two storey canted window bay facing the garden with adjacent recessed verandah at first floor level. Semicircular arched opening to verandah supported on column pilasters with ornamental stone balustrading. Moulded stonework surrounds to window openings, mainly on garden facing facade, with pediments over first floor windows on canted bay. Plain rendered reveals to window openings elsewhere, some with semicircular arched heads. A number of window alterations plus new openings added. Prominent main entrance doorway on side facade with moulded stonework surround and carved frieze. Slate roof with projecting gables and bargeboards. Re-roofed a number of years ago. Rendered chimney stacks with corbelled caps and buff ‘crown’ chimney pots. One stack facing Lenton Avenue taken down above roof level. A number of original rainwater fittings have been replaced. Sash windows generally with a number of casements added during alterations and to new window openings. Panelled double doors to main entrance, half glazed with semicircular head. Original brick boundary wall with stone plinth and gate piers to Lenton Avenue, now partly rendered. Original gate in one opening. Original brick or brick/stone garden walls at boundaries with Nos.29 and 33 Lenton Avenue. Original boundary wall with gate opening and steps to Cavendish Road West.
history: Detached house, also known as Greylands, c1875. House back in single household use after previous conversion into two flats.

desc: Two storeys plus attics and cellars. Original single storey, pitched roof extension adjacent to Lenton Avenue. Small original outbuildings built up against boundary wall adjacent to No.31 Lenton Avenue. Pitched roof garage building added c1950 in front yard area and original gate opening widened to allow car access. Rendered facades with stone plinth, door and window surrounds, string courses and other decorative features. The stonework has been coated or painted. Two storey canted window bay facing garden adjacent to recessed verandah at first floor level. Semicircular arched opening to verandah supported on column pilasters with ornamental stone balustrading. Moulded stonework surrounds to window openings, mainly on garden facing facade, with pediments over first floor windows on canted bay. Plain rendered reveals to window openings elsewhere, some with semicircular arched heads. A number of window alterations and additions including two oriel windows on south facing facade. Prominent main entrance doorway on side facade with moulded stonework surround and carved frieze. New doorway access to upper flat formed at first floor level on south facade with external steel staircase. Slate roof with projecting gables and bargeboards. Rendered chimney stacks with altered caps and some replaced chimney pots. A number of original rainwater fittings have been replaced. Sash windows generally with a number of alterations. Panelled double doors to main entrance, half glazed with semicircular head. Original gate piers and brick boundary wall with stone plinth to Lenton Avenue and Cavendish Road West. Original brick and brick/stone garden wall boundary with No.31 Lenton Avenue. Later gate opening formed in boundary wall to Lenton Avenue, adjacent to No.31.
history: Detached house, also known as ‘Newlands’, c1875. Similar to No.31 ‘Greylands’. Prominent corner position. Now converted into flats.

desc: Three storeys including attic storey plus extensive basement. Now converted into two flats. Red brick facades with inset decorative buff brick / stone panels at eaves level and at head of canted window bay. Single storey canted window bay on road front with three storey shallow rectangular bay on rear facade terminating at roof level with gabled dormer. Bull nose brick detail at roof verges to gabled dormer. Stone capped plinth on rear facade with blue brick string course. Part of brickwork on front facade has been rendered / painted. Moulded stone surrounds to doors and windows with moulded stone eaves plus string course below inset buff panels. Stone or render surround to attic window in gabled dormer now removed. Prominent open-bed moulded pediment to gable on front facade. Stone balustrading to plinth wall flanking canted window bay at ground floor level. Gabled and hipped slate roof with clay ridge tiles plus lead valleys and flashings to hips, hipped dormers and gabled pediment. Brick chimney stacks with brick and stone plinth, stone string courses plus corbelled brick and stone capping. One of the chimney stacks has been altered. Original buff crown chimney pots. Original c.i. rainwater fittings replaced and waste pipes added. Sash windows with glazing bars, generally. Panelled main entrance door to Lenton Road. Part of the first floor window opening facing Castle Grove has been altered to form new entrance doorway to top flat. Extension c1970 to 7 Castle Grove abuts building. Altered glazed passageway over side entrance from Castle Grove. Later outbuildings altered and converted into garages for Nos. 6 and 7 Castle Grove. Substantial part of original back garden area separated to form garden area for 7 Castle Grove. Original railings adjacent to cellar access steps on Lenton Road.
history: Built c1856-58, Grade II Listed. By architect T.C. Hine, Surveyor of the Duke of Newcastle’s Estate, and responsible for road layout of The Park c1855-56 that remains predominantly intact. House together with houses on Castle Grove and Lenton Road are amongst the earliest in The Park.

desc: Small first floor (wc?) extension c1900 in style of architect Watson Fothergill with black painted timber framework and vertical tile hanging. Originally, single storey outbuildings in small yard area at rear of house, now much changed with various alterations, extensions plus garage building, possibly involving architect Reginald Cooper c1963 and Howard Designs Ltd c1989. Three storeys plus basement/cellars. Red brick facades with stone dressings. Range of decorative brickwork features, including buff brick string courses, band courses of small recessed rectangular brick panels and diaper pattern on entrance facade. Two storey canted window bay on east facing facade with shell shaped lead roof. Similar two storey bay on south facing facade but with hipped slate roof. Bull nose brick detail at eaves and gable verges. Projecting brick plinth to facades with stone capping. Ornamental iron vent grille on facade. Combination of rectangular and semicircular arched openings with stone surrounds to original door and windows. Openings generally plain with splay reveals but mouldings added to cornice detail on bays, main entrance doorway and trefoil shaped small first floor window above entrance doorway. The stonework has remained unpainted. Slate gabled roof with clay ridge tiles and lead valleys. Red brick chimney stacks with corbelled head and stone cap. Buff ‘crown’ chimney pots. Original corbelled capping detail on chimney stack shared with 1 Park Drive has been altered. Original c.i. rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Original sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Original half glazed, six panelled main entrance door. Lean-to canopy over main entrance doorway is later addition. Original red brick boundary wall to Lenton Road with half round copings, blue brick string course and stone plinth. Original pedestrian gate opening now enlarged to allow car access. Original brick garden wall adjacent to No.4 Lenton Road. Hedge boundary adjacent to No.1 Park Drive. No significant external alterations to original house.
history: Part of a group of nine similarly styled houses on triangular plot enclosed by Lenton Road, Park Drive and Holles Crescent. Built c1865-70, possibly by John Loverseed with architects Hine & Evans. One of a pair of houses (with 1 Park Drive) in prominent position at junction of Lenton Road and Park Drive. In single household use.

desc: Three storeys including attic storey plus extensive basement. In single household use. Red brick facades with inset decorative buff brick / stone panels at eaves level and at head of canted window bay. Single storey canted window bays on front and rear facades plus three storey shallow rectangular bay on rear facade terminating at roof level with gabled dormer. Bull nose brick detail at roof verges to gabled dormer. Two storey side wing with pediment and oculus. Part of brickwork on front facade has been rendered / painted. Moulded stone rectangular and round headed surrounds to doors and windows with moulded stone eaves plus string course below inset buff panels. Prominent open-bed pediment to gable on front facade. Plinth wall flanking canted window bay at ground floor level. Gabled and hipped slate roof with dormers. Clay ridge tiles plus lead valleys and flashings to hips, hipped dormers and gabled pediment. Brick chimney stacks with brick and stone plinth, stone string courses plus corbelled brick and stone capping. Original buff crown chimney pots. Some replaced or missing. Original c.i. rainwater fittings have been replaced with downpipes added. Sash windows with glazing bars, generally. Alterations to bay door at rear. Later glazed passageway over steps to main entrance on side elevation together with lean-to conservatory on rear facade. Original railings adjacent to cellar access steps on Lenton Road. Originally, rear garden areas of Nos.1 and 3 combined into single shared garden. Part of garden adjacent to Ogle Drive now lost to separate residential development c1970.
history: Built c1856-58. Grade II listed. By architect T.C. Hine, Surveyor of the Duke of Newcastle’s Estate and responsible for road layout of The Park c1855-56 that remains predominantly intact. House together with houses on Castle Grove and Lenton Road are amongst the earliest in The Park.

desc: Three storeys plus basement/cellars. Later garage building now removed to create access to car parking space in original garden/yard area. Red brick facades with stone dressings. Decorative features include buff brick string courses and band courses of small, recessed rectangular buff brick panels. Red and buff brick cruciform patterns to bay parapet and bull nose brick detail at eaves and gable verges. Two storey canted window bay on front facade and shallow single storey box bay at main entrance doorway. Parts of plinths to facades have been rendered/painted. Combination of openings, some with segmental or semicircular arched heads. Stone surrounds to original door and windows. Stonework generally plain with splay detail at reveals and lintels, mouldings added to cornice detail on bays and to drip mould above large mullioned window at ground floor level on front facade. Stonework now painted. Double pitched slate roof with gables. Clay ridge tiles and lead valleys. Flat roof dormer at rear. Roof windows added. Original red brick chimney stacks with stone plinth stretchers, buff brick patterning, corbelled brick heads and stone caps. A number of the original buff ‘crown’ chimney pots have been replaced. Original c.i. rainwater fittings have been replaced. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Original brick and stone boundary wall to Lenton Road, now partly rendered. Original brick garden walls adjacent to 2 Lenton Road and 3 Park Drive. Original gate opening with piers and steps now enlarged to provide car access into former garden area, including tarmac drive and brick retaining walls. Front garden area originally shared with No.6 Lenton Road.
history: Part of a group of similarly styled houses on triangular plot of land enclosed by Lenton Road, Park Drive and Holles Crescent. Built c1865-70, possibly by John Loverseed with Architects Hine & Evans. One of a pair of houses (with 6 Lenton Road). House now converted into flats.

desc: Three storeys including attic plus basement. Large canted three storey bay adjacent to Lenton Road plus two storey flat roofed canted bay on garden front. Red brick facades with extensive brick and stone decorative patterning. Corbelled brick eaves with sawtooth detailing and linear arrangement of inset eight-pointed stars. Numerous blue brick string and flush courses. Brick drip moulds and voussoirs to half round arched head to main gable window plus brick bowtell roll mouldings to a number of window reveals. Elaborate moulded stone door and window dressings with band courses at window head and sill levels. Complex roof with hipped bay, coped gables with kneelers plus gabled dormer. Slate with lead flashings to ridges and valleys. Iron finials. Dual tone linear slate patterning on roof. Some original c.i. rainwater fittings replaced. Prominent tapered brick chimney stacks with extensive brick relief detailing, blue brick string courses plus corbelled and stepped cappings. One chimney stack has been partially rebuilt. Buff ‘louvre’ pots. Sash windows generally. Some minor alterations to windows. Alterations to conservatory and yard entrance wall. Original brick boundary wall and gate piers adjacent to Lenton Road including shaped brick balustrading, blue plinth bricks and stone saddleback copings. Stone caps to main entrance gate piers. Entrance porch and steps added c1898. More recent single storey flat roofed extension in yard. Plans for garage on Ogle Drive 1936. Part of original Lenton Road boundary wall has been rebuilt and altered. Originally, rear garden areas of Nos.5 and 7 were combined into single shared garden.
history: One of architect Watson Fothergill’s earliest houses in The Park c1873. Grade II Listed. Similar in scale and style to the adjacent house (No.7) also by Watson Fothergill. Now converted into two flats.

desc: Three storeys plus basement/cellars. Plan by architects Sutton, Pearce and Dick for garage and cloakroom for Mr J. A. MacKerral c1957. Red brick facades with stone dressings. Decorative features include buff brick string courses and band courses of small, recessed rectangular buff brick panels. Red and buff brick cruciform patterns to bay parapet and bull nosed brick detail at eaves and gable verges. Two storey canted window bay on front facade and shallow single storey box bay at main entrance doorway. Combination of openings, some with segmental or semicircular arched chamfered heads and reveals. Stone surrounds to original door and windows. Stone lintels and sills to rear windows. Stonework generally plain with chamfered reveals and lintels, mouldings added to cornice detail on bays and drip mould above large mullioned window at ground floor level on front facade. Stonework, now painted. Double pitched slate roof with gables. Clay ridge tiles and lead valleys. Flat roof dormer at rear. Red brick chimney stacks, originally with stone plinth stretchers, buff brick patterning, corbelled brick heads and stone caps, now altered. A number of the original buff ‘crown’ chimney pots have been replaced. Original c.i. rainwater fittings have been replaced and downpipes added. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Original brick and stone boundary wall to Lenton Road, now partly rendered. Original gate opening with piers and steps now enlarged to provide car access into former garden area, including tarmac drive and brick retaining walls. Front garden area originally shared with No.4 Lenton Road.
history: Part of a group of similarly styled houses on triangular plot of land enclosed by Lenton Road, Park Drive and Holles Crescent. Built c1865-70, possibly by John Loverseed with architects Hine & Evans. Semi-detached. In single household use.

desc: Three storeys including attic plus basement. Red brick facades with extensive brick and stone decorative patterning. Corbelled brick eaves with sawtooth brick detailing and linear arrangement of inset eight–pointed stars. Numerous blue brick string and flush courses. Brick drip moulds and voussoirs to half round arches over gable windows and brick scroll mouldings to a number of window reveals. Geometric brick pattern over main entrance doorway. Moulded stone door and window dressings with band courses at window head and sill levels. An area of facade brickwork adjacent to the pavement on Lenton Road has been rendered. Complex roof with gables, hipped canted bay, hipped square turret, dormers and gabled dormer. Slate with lead flashings to ridges and valleys. Iron finials. Dual tone linear slate patterning on roof. Original c.i. rainwater fittings replaced. Prominent tapered brick chimney stacks with brick relief detailing, blue brick string courses plus corbelled and stepped cappings. Buff ‘louvre’ pots. Boarded main entrance door. Sash windows generally. Some minor alterations to windows. Alterations to conservatory and yard wall. Original brick boundary wall and gate piers adjacent to Lenton Road including shaped brick balustrading, blue plinth bricks and stone saddleback copings. Stone caps to main entrance gate piers. Half round brick copings to brick garden wall. Originally, rear garden areas of Nos.5 and 7 were combined into single shared garden. Garages facing Ogle Drive built 1963 at the bottom of the garden. The original house remains mainly intact externally with no significant alterations or extensions.
history: One of architect Watson Fothergill’s earliest houses in The Park c1873. Grade II Listed, also known as Fothergill House. Similar in scale and style to adjacent house Castle Bank (no.5), also by Watson Fothergill.

desc: Predominantly single storey with accommodation in roofspace. Half-timbered Tudorbethan architectural style to all facades with white painted render or red brick infill panels. Red brick plinth at varying heights. Covered porch to office entrance with half open moulded timber arched and framed external screen. Projecting wings to former agent’s room and ante room with bow and canted window bays respectively. Timber bressumer beams above bays supported by shaped timber brackets. Double pitched roof with hips and prominent gables. Plain tiles with clay ridges. Projecting timber bargeboards at gables. Plain red brick chimney stacks with simple brick relief pattern below shaped projecting stone cap. Original plain roll top red chimney pots. One pot missing. Splayed plinth at lower level of chimney stack facing the garden with crowstep copings. Casement windows generally with leaded lights. Flat roofed, rectangular oriel window to gable on east facing facade supported by twin shaped timber brackets. Lead covered flat roofed dormers with casements. Some minor window alterations. Original panelled main entrance door to office. Small fully glazed flat
history: Plans for new Newcastle Estate Office, (now known as The Park Estate Office), by Frank Littler architect of Lowdham, Nottingham c1907. Office building designed to harmonise with residential surroundings. Grade II Listed. Building remains Estate office and caretaker’s residence as originally planned.

desc: Three storeys plus basement/cellars. Flat roofed garage added. Later single storey brick lean-to extension with slate roof added. Red brick facades with stone dressings. Decorative brickwork features include buff brick string courses, band courses of small, rectangular buff brick recessed panels and buff brick diaper pattern on entrance facade. Bull nosed brick detail at eaves and gable verges. Two storey canted window bays on front and side facades with slate, hipped roofs. Projecting brick plinth at base of facades with stone capping. Combination of openings, some with segmental or semicircular arched heads. Stone surrounds to original doors and windows. Stonework generally plain with chamfered reveals and lintels, mouldings added to cornice detail on bays and main entrance doorway. Stonework now painted. Double pitch slate roofs at varying levels with gables. Clay ridge tiles and lead valleys. Original red brick chimney stacks with stone capped plinths, corbelled brick heads and buff ‘crown’ pots. Original c.i. rainwater fittings have been replaced and further pipes added. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Original brick and stone boundary wall, (together with boundary wall of No.5 Holles Crescent), in prominent position at junction of Lenton Road and Holles Crescent. Original brickwork to part of boundary wall above stone plinth now rendered and painted. Similarly, original brick and stone gate piers have also been rendered. Part of original boundary wall lost when garage added.
history: Part of a group of similarly styled houses on triangular plot of land enclosed by Lenton Road, Park Drive and Holles Crescent. Built c1865-70, possibly by John Loverseed with architects Hine & Evans. Semi-detached and in single household use.

desc: Original outbuildings adjacent to the Lenton Road boundary have been altered and converted for garage use. Red brick facades with ornamental brick detailing including corbelled dentilation at eaves and gable verges, forming open pediments, with a combination of projecting, flush or inset red and blue brick string courses at storey height levels. Single storey canted brick door and window bay on garden elevation plus ground floor pointed window bay on east facing facade. The canted bay may originally have been flat roofed. A number of the original door and window openings have semicircular arched heads with alternating red and blue brick voussoirs and plain stone keystones. Remaining original openings have stone segmental or shaped square lintels and sills. All the stonework is now painted. Pitched roofed timber canopy on brackets over main entrance door on side elevation is a later addition. Ground floor brick facades facing east and towards Lenton Road have been painted or rendered. Gabled roof with clay plain tiles and ridge tiles, some of which have been removed. Brick stacks have been partly taken down and rebuilt. Original c.i.rainwater fittings have been replaced with aluminium. Original sash windows generally, in singles or pairs with brick mullions. A number of the windows have single central glazing bars. Alterations to original external doors. Large original garden survives apart from a small rectangular area adjacent to Kenilworth Road which has been reshaped and enclosed c1890 by a curved stone boundary wall with rear entrance gate and piers. Original brick boundary wall adjacent to Peveril House survives and brick garden wall adjacent to 13 Lenton Road has been partially rebuilt. Original brick boundary wall adjacent to Lenton Road also survives but has been partially demolished to form a drive access.
history: Detached house, also known as ‘Fern Lodge’, built c1865 with no significant extensions to the original building apart from a flat roofed glazed porch added to the road facing facade in recent years.

desc: Significant external alterations and extensions possibly by Arthur Calvert c1898 including the replacement of single storey outbuildings on the west facade with a two storey extension, the addition of a first floor canted bay window on the garden elevation, a plain tile pitched roof canopy on timber brackets over the canted bays facing the garden plus a glazed porch with brick plinth and plain tile pitched roof at the main entrance doorway on the east facing facade. It is possible that part of the original outbuilding adjacent to the boundary wall to Lenton Road survives and is now used as a home office. House restored to single household use c1980 after previously being converted into flats c1946. Original garden area. Simple red brick facades with corbelled and bull nose brick detail at eaves. Blue brick eaves string course enclosing decorative band of inset buff brick or stone panels. Blue brick plinth. Diaper brick pattern on facade. Moulded stone surrounds to original window openings plus bull nosed corner detail at eaves. Stonework now painted. Slate hipped roof with clay hip tiles. A number of original cast iron rainwater fittings have been replaced. Red brick chimneystacks with plinths, string course and corbelled cap. Buff ‘crown’ pots. One stack remains unaltered. Sash windows to original openings, some with glazing bars. Probable changes to canted bays and window on garden facing facade during alterations c1898. Later windows include sash and casements, some with leaded lights. Original garden area enclosed by original brick garden walls. Part of original boundary wall to Lenton Road survives but there have been later alterations and additions including garage door openings and gate piers.
history: Early detached house c1865, possibly by architect Hine & Evans, together with a number of other houses along the southern side of Lenton Road.

desc: Two storeys plus attic and basement. Much altered and extended in the early part of the 20c and later split into two households (13 and 13a) c1975. Plans by Evans & Son Architects c1910 for two storey front and side extensions for Arthur Forman with alterations and further additions by Evans & Son c1919-20 including a bay window and study, also for Arthur Forman. Recent single storey kitchen extension by Boden & Lloyd Architects added c1998. In single household use. Part of original garden area lost to separate residential development c1975-80 with residual garden area divided in half following conversion of house into two separate dwellings c1975. Red brick facades to original house and extensions with unpainted stone dressings. Original two storey brick canted bay window on garden facade with later two storey brick bow window bay facing Lenton Road. Original main entrance doorway was located on the side elevation of the house but has been repositioned following later building extensions. Distinctive frieze-like arrangement of small, square, stone or buff brick inset panels at eaves level. Diaper brickwork pattern on facade. Unpainted stone dressings on original house and later extensions include door and window surrounds, bay cornices, string courses and copings. Hood moulds over stone semicircular arched window openings at attic level. Hipped and gabled roof with projecting eaves, ornamental eaves brackets and bargeboards facing Lenton Road. Slate roof covering with clay ridge and hip tiles. Brick chimney stacks with plinths and corbelled caps. Some of the original buff ‘crown’ chimney pots are missing or have been replaced. Original cast iron rainwater fittings have been replaced and further downpipes added, plus unsightly boiler flue adjacent to bay window facing the garden. Mainly sash windows to original house with a variety of wood and metal casements to later building extensions or alterations. Some casements fitted with leaded lights. Roof windows added. Later door to garden bay. Original garden wall adjacent to No.11 Lenton Road survives and brick boundary walladjacent to Lenton Road may have been altered or rebuilt during extensive building works to house in early part of 20c.
history: Early house c1856-60 also known as ‘Fairholme’, probably by architect Hine & Evans.

desc: Much altered and extended in early part of 20c and split into two dwellings (13 and 13a) c 1975. Plans by Evans and Son Architects c1910 for two storey front and side extensions for Arthur Forman with alterations and further additions also by Evans & Son c1919-20 including bay window and study. In single household use. Part of original garden area lost to separate residential development c1975-80 and residual garden area divided in half following conversion of house into two separate dwellings c1975. Three storeys including attics. Red brick facades to original house and extensions with unpainted stone dressings. Considerable alterations to road front including rectangular projections, oriel bays on brackets at first floor level and new entrance. Later two storey flat roofed side extensions added. Band of small inset buff brick or stone panels at eaves level. Stone door and window surrounds, brackets, string courses, copings and other minor details. Hoodmoulds over stone semicircular arched window opening at attic level. Hipped and gabled roof on original house with projecting eaves and eaves brackets. Bargeboards to gable and dormer. Slate roof with clay ridge and hip tiles. Red brick chimney stacks with stone dressings and corbelled caps. Alterations to one stack and missing buff ‘crown’ chimney pots. Mainly sash windows to original house plus variety of types including casements to later extensions or alterations. Leaded lights to some later windows including oriel bays on the front facade. Original brick boundary wall adjacent to Lenton Road with blue brick patterning and stone copings, now partly altered plus new gate opening with piers added probably when house was split into two dwellings.
history: Early house c1856-60 also previously known as ‘Fairholme’. Probably by Hine & Evans Architects.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic. Red brick with ashlar dressings and hipped slate roof. House, with No.17 Lenton Road, built on site of old Park Bowling Green, established c1807. Main entrance bay on side of house with double leaf panelled main entrance door flanked by single windows with fixed leaded lights. Twin rectangular window bays on road front with gabled side wing to the left. Garage building, probably added by Thomas Barker & Son for J. Farrer Kirk c1924. Single storey canted window bay on garden front with single window opening above. To right, altered openings to create French window at ground floor level with enlarged window opening above. To right again, single storey early 20c extension with hipped roof and large canted bay window. Decorative brickwork details include buff brick banding and square inset panels to bays and eaves. Stone surrounds to original door and window openings with rounded corners or round arched heads. Also, stone plinths, moulded string courses and copings. Hipped slate main roof with hipped bays and extension plus gabled side wing. Overhanging wooden eaves with moulded details. Clay ridge and hip tiles with lead flashings to bay roofs. Red brick chimney stacks with stone plinth course, brick string course plus corbelled brick detail at moulded stone coping. A number of original pots are missing or replaced. Roof window added. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further pipework added. Glazing bar sash windows generally with later additions and alterations. Part of original brick boundary wall on road front replaced by later garage building and part of remaining section now rendered and painted. Unrendered section survives adjacent to location of original outbuildings. Brick and stone gate piers adjacent to garage. Original brick garden wall adjacent to No.13. Although a substantial part of garden area, previously part of bowling green, was used communally with garden of No.17 a fence now seperates the gardens.
history: Detached house c1859, possibly by Hine and Evans architects. One of a group of early houses at southern end of The Park. In single household use.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic storey. Single storey later brick and plain tile lean-to extension abuts west facing facade. Additions and alterations for Walter Sisson c1946 possibly also include single storey flat roofed and rendered outbuildings abutting boundary wall to Lenton Road. Records indicate a plan for a garage for Bernard Wright c1956. Coach house and small outbuilding, now semi-derelict, added in garden area adjacent to Clifton Terrace c1900-1914. Red brick facades with unpainted stone dressings including door and window surrounds plus quoins, plinth caps and projecting string course with gargoyle-like carved grotesque heads at corner positions. Pair of columns with foliated capitals form part of stone portico with moulded segmental arched heads at main entrance doorway. Brick canted window bays to principal rooms on garden facing facade. Gabled roof with projecting wood bargeboards and carved finials. Slate roof covering with clay ridge tiles. Original brick chimney stacks with stone quoined plinths, brick string courses, corbelled caps and buff ‘crown’ pots. Cast iron dated hopper heads and shaped ornamental spouts. A number of original rainwater fittings have been replaced. Mainly wood or metal framed windows, some with leaded lights. A number of alterations or replacements to external doors and windows. Large tree lined garden, originally forming part of shared garden with No.15 Lenton Road (the site of the old Park Bowling Green). Original brick boundary wall to Lenton Road has been rendered. Original house and garden remain mainly intact with some alterations and small extensions.
history: Early houses c1856, also known as ‘Oakhurst’ or Oakhyrst’. House located on the site of the Park Bowling Green established c1807, adjacent to a group of garden allotments known as Fish Pond Gardens, dating from 1792-4. In single household use.

desc:
Now converted into five flats. Mainly three storeys with two storey original side extension. Predominantly red brick facades with stone dressings and inset buff brick panel frieze below eaves level. Unfortunately part of the facade facing Lenton Road has been rendered with the loss of original openings and the addition of an unsightly porch. Two shallow rectangular three storey window bays facing Lenton Road, canted two storey window bay on garden facing facade plus prominent octagonal corner bay with gabled dormers overlooking Park Ravine. Stone lintels, sills and surrounds generally to original door and window openings, predominantly plain apart from a number of semicircular arched openings with hood moulds over some windows and the original main entrance door, sadly now
partially infilled to form a window. Bulls-eye window opening on Lenton Road facade. Moulded stone cornices and string courses at storey height and eaves level with raking cornices to open-bed pediments at gables on Lenton Road facade and to gabled dormers on octagonal bay. Original moulded stone caps to a number of unaltered chimney stacks. Much of the stonework remains unpainted except for cornices and string courses, which have also been damaged or partly removed in some areas. Corbelled brick chimney stacks, partly altered, with a number of original chimney pots. Inset buff brick decorative panels on stacks plus brick/stone string courses. Slate hipped and gabled roof with flat roofed dormers added later. Original rainwater fittings and soil pipes have been replaced with plastic. Although a number of original sash windows survive there have been some unsympathetic replacements and a number of new openings formed. Original external doors have also been removed or replaced. No significant extensions to the original building. The setting of the house is enhanced by its corner location adjacent to the excavated road down Park Ravine. Prominent Bulwell stone retaining wall forms the Park Ravine boundary, topped by original brick garden wall and stone balustrading. Stone bellied balusters have now been painted over. Only small sections of the original brick boundary wall to Lenton Road remain, now rendered and painted. A row of garages has replaced the original stone retaining wall facing Clifton Terrace. Original brick boundary wall adjacent to the garden of No.17 Lenton Road survives. Original garden area has recently been subdivided by a low wooden fence.
history: An imposing house on a prominent corner plot, probably by Hine & Evans Architects c1850-1860.

desc: Three storeys including attic storey plus basement. In single household use. Single storey lean-to side extension added c1998. Former coach house / stables building at bottom of garden facing Clifton Terrace now separately owned and converted for residential use. Red brick facades with distinctive Hine detail of small inset buff brick / stone panels arranged as decorative band at first floor and eaves levels. Twin flat roofed, canted window bays on garden facade either side of central doorway. Main entrance doorway on side elevation with moulded stone surround forming part of shallow, bay terminating at roof level in open-bed moulded stone pediment. Diaper brick patterns on Park Ravine facade. Moulded stone surrounds to door and windows generally plus eaves and string courses at storey height levels. Stone capping to brick plinth at base of facades. Stonework now painted. Hipped slate roof with clay ridges and lead roll hips. Prominent pair of original and later dormer windows on each of three main elevations with half round heads to casement windows, pediments and shaped ornamental side brackets. Dormers facing garden are a later addition. Brick chimney stacks with stone plinth heads, moulded caps and brick string course. A number of original crown pots remain. Original c.i. rainwater fittings replaced. Sash windows generally, some with glazing bars. Original half-glazed panelled main entrance door. Garden door replaced. Garden area originally extended over former coach house / stables building. Original gate opening in Lenton Road boundary wall widened c1910 and brick boundary walls rebuilt, reinstating stone balustrading on Park Ravine.
history: Probably by archiect Hine & Evans c 1862 with builder John Barker: Letter, July 1905, confirms assignment of lease from Robert Evans to his son Robert Evans Jnr. who was occupying the premises at that time. House together with houses on Castle Grove and others on Lenton Road are the earliest in the southern part of The Park.

desc: There is some indication of a possible single storey ground floor original extension in the central bay of the Lenton Road facade but this has now gone. The building is again in single household use after being previously converted into three flats. Three storeys above ground level plus basement. Predominantly red brick facades with plinths plus string courses and corbelling at eaves level. Prominent blue brick decorative patterns on chimney stack brickwork. Part of brick facade at ground level facing Lenton Road has been rendered and painted, suggesting alterations in this area. Pair of single storey canted stone window bays on garden front plus a variety of stone dressings including door and window surrounds, string courses, brackets, kneelers, gable copings, finials and plinths. Most of the stonework remains unpainted. Gabled slate roof with clay ridge tiles. Gable dormers on garden elevation. The upper parts of all original chimney stacks have been taken down and rebuilt in a different style. Original rainwater fittings have been replaced. Prominent original main entrance doorway on west facing facade in classical style with stone entablature plus rectangular pilasters with Doric capitals flanking semicircular arched opening over door and fanlight. Original doors and windows to stone canted bays overlooking the garden have been replaced and parts of the bay openings have been altered. There have also been alterations and additions to the windows. A number of original sash windows set within a combination of rectangular or semicircular arched stone openings survive. Other windows including gable dormers on garden elevation and Venetian window style openings on Lenton Road facade appear to have been added later. The house retains its original garden area together with the original brick garden boundary walls adjacent to the neighbouring houses Nos.21 and 25 Lenton Road. Brick boundary wall adjacent to Lenton Road has been rebuilt with gated opening for car access.
history: One of the early large houses in The Park c1858-60. The ‘as built’ form of the original building remains mainly intact with no extensions.

desc: Although outbuildings have been replaced by a separate single storey dwelling (No.25a) c1960-70, the main building remains relatively intact and in single household use. An original single storey extension to the west facing facade was also demolished some time ago and partly replaced by a small lean-to building together with the refacing of part of the same facade with an additional brick skin involving alterations to openings. Original openings were also infilled during the construction of No. 25a. The main house is two storeys plus basement and attic storey. Red brick facades with moulded stone door and window surrounds, string courses plus cornice detail at eaves level. All the stone dressings below eaves level have now been painted. Render has been added to the brick plinth on the garden elevation. Pair of shallow rectangular full height window bays topped by shaped gable dormers on garden elevation, flanking a central two storey canted door and window bay with flat roof. Two storey projecting wing on Lenton Road elevation. Hipped slate roof with gable dormers. Lead flashings to hips, ridges and valleys. Plain brick chimney stacks with stone dressings and original pots with some replacements. Cast iron rainwater fittings and soil downpipes with some plastic replacements. Prominent moulded semicircular stone arched head to main entrance doorway flanked by shaft pilasters and stone dressings, now partly obscured by later development of No.25a. Plain sash windows generally, some with glazing bars, plus later window openings. Leaded light lattice glazing to some feature windows. Plans for billiard room and garage c1921 by Swann & Wright Architects, probably replaced as part of development of No.25a. A covered passage was added to the building between the main entrance doorway and the boundary wall on Lenton Road c1870, but this has now been demolished together with the boundary wall. Part of the original garden area has been used for garaging (Nos.25 and 25a) plus a drive and these have been separated from the residual garden area by a substantial brick screen wall. An area of land, now isolated from the house by the garages and drive, is also part of the property. The land originally formed part of a group of garden allotments known as Fishpond gardens but is now overgrown.
history: One of the early houses in The Park c1858, possibly by Hine & Evans Architects.

desc: Three storeys including attics plus basement. Red brick facades with band of small inset buff coloured brick or stone panels and buff string courses at storey height level and eaves. Stone dressings to door and window openings. Pair of two storey canted window bays on garden facing facade. Brick plinth to main facades. Moulded stone door and window surrounds including prominent semicircular window heads with shafts to first floor windows on one canted bay. Also semicircular arched head to larger first floor window on Lenton Road facade. Segmental arched head to front facade door opening. Moulded stone detail at eaves with stone caps to facade plinths plus other smaller stone details elsewhere. Apart from the eaves most of the stonework remains unpainted or paint has been recently removed. Facade brickwork recently cleaned. A number of later window openings. Hipped slate roof with lead flashings. A number of dormer windows added. Red brick chimney stacks, now altered. Original rainwater fittings replaced and numerous waste and downpipes added. Original sash windows plus a number of alterations. Casement windows generally to later window openings. Original main entrance to house was on east facing facade. Part of original rear garden area lost to block of garages with drive access. Section of original buff and red brick and stone patterned boundary wall adjacent to Lenton Road survives, together with pair of stone capped gate piers. Later openings formed and gate piers added.
history: Early houses c1858, probably by T.C. Hine, architect. Now converted into flats with a number of later single storey extensions and other alterations.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic storey. Red brick with ashlar dressings and hipped slate roof. First occupant of house was William Wright, lace manufacturer. Drive formed at side of garden to create access to 2no. separate dwellings, built c1970 in area once used as allotment gardens. 20c additions include flat roofed garage building adjacent to Lenton Road and two storey hipped side extension behind garage. Panelled main entrance door with overlight at centre of road front facade, flanked by transomed side lights all within moulded stone surround with trefoil arched heads and broken arch pediment. To left, single storey canted stone window bay with balustrade. Above, tripartite sash window at first floor plus brick gabled dormer window at attic level with altered window opening. To right of main entrance, three storey square tower with pyramidal slate roof. To right again, two storey projecting wing with hipped slate roof and gabled dormer. Single storey canted stone window bay with balustrade on garden front. To left, stone framed door opening with overlight and side lights. Tripartite sash window openings at first floor level either side of single window opening with trefoil arched head. Twin gabled dormer windows on garden front with altered window openings. Later flat roofed dormer window added between gabled dormers. Single storey square window bay with balustrade across left corner of garden front. Ashlar surrounds to doors and windows. Also, stone plinths, moulded copings and brackets plus decorative square inset panel band with moulded string course and cornice at eaves. Stonework now painted. Steeply pitched slate hipped roof with red brick side wall stacks, now altered. Mainly glazing bar sash windows with a number of alterations and additions. Coursed rubble stone plinth to boundary wall on road front with ashlar copings and added railings. Small part of garden area subdivided to provide separate outdoor amenity space for one flat.
history: Early detached house c1859-60, probably by architects Hine & Evans. House now converted into three flats.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attics. Red brick with stone dressings and hipped slate roof. Later single storey side extensions plus mid 20c flat roofed garage adjacent to Lenton Road. Garages also added adjacent to stone retaining wall boundary at bottom of garden. Central doorway with segment arched overlight on Lenton Road front with glazing bar sash window to left. To right of doorway, original segment arched window opening replaced by flat roofed, early 20c bow window bay with leaded glazing. Three segment arched glazing bar sashes above. Main entrance doorway on Hermitage Walk front flanked by small sash windows and chimney stacks. Three windows at first floor level above, all with segment arched heads, stone surrounds and plain or glazing bar sashes. Pair of two storey canted window bays on garden front with three segment arched windows at each level. Moulded stone surrounds to all original openings. Sashes with leaded glazing to original first floor opening on east facing facade. Decorative banding at first floor and eaves levels with square inset brick or stone tile panels between parallel buff brick courses. Stone cornice and sill bands to canted bays plus moulded eaves cornice. Most of stonework now painted. Hipped slate roof with clay ridge and hip tiles and overhanging eaves. 4no. red brick side wall chimney stacks with chamfered stone plinth band, separated flues, buff brick string courses and corbelled brick and stone caps. One stack has been altered. Dormer windows with round arched window openings and segmental pediments. Roof windows added. Prominent red brick boundary wall adjacent to Hermitage Walk with buff brick and stone plinth, decorative banding, chamfered stone coping plus balustrading adjacent to house. Red and buff brick square piers with chamfered stone plinth band and moulded stone caps. Later section of red brick boundary wall adjacent to Lenton Road with blue brick band and chamfered stone coping.
history: Detached house c1856 by architects Hine & Evans. Formerly known as Leslie Villa. Now converted into flats. One of a group of early houses on Lenton Road by Hine & Evans. At the time the house was built, it was envisaged that Clifton Terrace would be extended to link Park Ravine with Hermitage Walk and run adjacent to boundary at bottom of garden. However, this plan was never implemented.

name: Cliff House:
desc: Three storeys including attic. Alterations and additions include two large side extensions c1885-95, single storey canted window bay and conservatory at rear, various smaller and more recent extensions plus flat roofed double garage. Red brick facades, buff brick string courses and distinctive Hine decorative features including band of small inset buff brick or stone panels at storey height levels, Italianate splayed corner windows with stone colonnette on main front facade and eight pointed star vent motif. Large west facing later extension, with bow window, similar in style to original house. Prominent east facing extension with relocated main entrance to enlarged house through imposing classical style doorway with stone columns, pilasters and entablature. Brick segmental pediments to first floor windows over entrance and half round arched head with brick voussoirs to dormer window at attic level. Moulded stone door and window surrounds, copings and chimney caps. Most of the stonework now painted. Hipped slate roof with prominent gabled dormer on front facade. Mainly lead flashings to ridges and hips on original house with plain and decorative clay ridges and hips on large extensions. Red brick chimney stacks with buff brick string courses and stone caps plus many original buff crown chimney pots. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Sash window generally with glazing bars and small panes. No glazing bars to sash windows on large extensions. A number of doors and window alterations and additions including roof windows. Original brick and stone boundary wall and piers partly replaced by railings, extensions and garage building. Surviving sections of brick boundary wall have been altered by render and paint. Original garden area largely lost to separate residential development c1980 and residual original garden area now subdivided to suit present occupancy.
history: Prominent early house by T.C. Hine architect c1857 subsequently extended and more recently converted into accommodation for three separate households in main building c1959 plus two flats in much altered and extended original coach house.
converted: Three separate households and two flats

desc: Single storey pitched roof extension added adjacent to original main entrance doorway facing Lenton Road with large flat roofed extension adding second floor to original two storey side wing. Probably red brick facades with stone dressings originally, now much altered by extensive rendering and painting. Pair of two storey canted window bays on south facing facade. Brick half round arched openings to a number of original door and window openings with cruciform shaped inset brick patterning on facades and bays. Double pitched slate roof to part of original house with gables over the main entrance doorway and above flat roofed canted bays. Original roof over two storey side wing now replaced with flat roofed brick storey height addition. Original rainwater fittings replaced and further downpipes and waste pipes added. Most of the original doors and windows have been replaced. Originally a large house with glasshouses, outbuildings and large stepped south facing garden. Now most of original garden has been lost to separate residential development of four houses c1968 with independent road access (Rock Drive). Block of flat roofed garages also built adjacent to original house on Lenton Road. Little or none of original boundary wall adjacent to Lenton Road remains.
history: Built into sloping ground c1870 with, originally, single storey facade to Lenton Road and two storeys facing rear garden area. Located on land immediately adjacent to old Newcastle Bowling Green. (Now Friars Court) Converted into flats and much altered c1968-70 with variety of flat and pitched roof extensions.

desc: Parts of original terraces, retaining walls and garden steps survive. Lower part of garden has been separately developed for commercial use. Original house now converted into three flats. House occupied in early years of 20c by the Ball family, including Albert Ball the son, a famous First World War ‘air ace’, subsequently killed in action and awarded a posthumous Victoria Cross. Steel fire escape staircase attached to side of building. Conservatory originally abutted side of house adjacent to boundary with No.45. Single storey extensions recently added adjacent to Lenton Road boundary to create access and utility accommodation for ground floor flat. Upper flats use original main entrance doorway on east facing elevation. Red brick facades (recently cleaned) with ashlar dressings. Three storey shallow, projecting gabled wing plus canted window bay overlooking garden and three storey shallow projecting gabled wing facing Lenton Road. Original ground floor window openings to projecting wing facing garden now altered to form door openings onto timber decked terrace. Generally, plain brickwork detailing with decorative features including bracketed detail at eaves and gable verges plus moulded brick string courses on gables and at storey height levels. Brick patching in places. Some parts of original brickwork, particularly adjacent to the main entrance doorway, now rendered. Stone door and window surrounds, mullions and transomes with shaped lintels and splay reveals. Stone copings and kneelers to gable on garden facade. Gabled roof with slates and ornamental clay ridge tiles. Hipped slate roof to canted bay facing garden. Alterations to chimney stacks. Original rainwater fittings replaced. Originally, double-hung sashes to main windows. A number of window alterations. Remnants of original etched glass panes in main entrance hall window (?), other panes replaced. Shaped frames to top lights in entrance hall windows. Original six panelled main entrance door and frame. Original gate piers to brick boundary wall adjacent to Lenton Road now replaced by wider opening for car access. Sections of original brick boundary wall with saddleback copings have been altered to increase height or add extensions.
history: Three storey detached house built onto sloping site c1875. Previously known as Sedgeley House. Terraced garden originally terminated at the banks of old route of River Leen.

desc: Road front is one storey above upper garden terrace level. Four storeys including attic storey and basement. Originally, shared upper rear garden terrace with No.47 and further lower terraces down to former route of the River Leen at bottom of garden. Originally, large glasshouse on middle garden terrace linked with other garden levels by steps, some of which survive. Lower part of the garden now separately developed for commercial use. Original house now converted into flats / bedsits. Steel fire escape staircase added to side of building. Red brick facades with unpainted ashlar dressings. Two storey, shallow rectangular bay on road front with gabled dormer above. Alterations to main entrance doorway. Existing main access via common entrance doorway shared with No.47. Brick facade details include bull nose bricks at eaves and gable verges, decorative brick relief band below eaves plus moulded brick string courses, hoodmoulds and window reveals. Brick voussoirs to a number of window heads with pointed or segmental arches. Sections of the original facade brickwork now partially rebuilt, rendered or painted. Assorted clutter added to facades as a result of multi-occupation including external meter boxes, flues, vents, pipework, cables etc. Stonework details include moulded lintels and sills to windows, extended on garden facing elevation to form band courses across width of facade. Also, stone copings to bay window and gable kneelers. Double pitched plain tile roof with gable dormers. Clay ridge tiles. Chimney stacks removed and original rainwater fittings replaced. Mainly sash windows with a number of alterations and added casements. Alterations to boundary wall adjacent to Lenton Road.
history: One of a pair of houses (with 47 Lenton Road) built on sloping site c1875.

desc: Road front is one storey above upper garden terrace level. Four storeys including attic storey and basement. Originally, shared upper rear garden terrace with No.45 and further terraces and retaining walls down to former route of River Leen at bottom of garden. Originally, large glass house on middle garden terrace linked with other garden levels by steps, some of which survive. Lower part of garden now separately developed for commercial use. Original house now converted into flats / bedsits. Steel fire escape staircase added to side of building. Red brick facades with unpainted ashlar dressings. Two storey, shallow rectangular bay on road front with gabled dormer above. Prominent four storey canted window bay on garden facing facade, with hipped roof. Alterations to main entrance doorway. Existing main access now via common entrance doorway shared with No.45. Brick facade details include bull nose bricks at eaves and gable verges, decorative brick relief band below eaves plus moulded brick string courses, hoodmoulds and window reveals. Brick voussoirs to a number of window heads with pointed or segmental arches. Sections of original facade brickwork have been partially rebuilt, rendered or painted. Assorted clutter added to facades as a result of multi-occupation including external meter boxes, flues, vents, pipework, cables etc. Stonework details include moulded lintels and sills to windows, extended on garden facing elevation to form band courses across width of facade. Also, stone console brackets and kneelers to gabled dormer. Double pitched plain tile roof with hips and gabled dormers. Clay ridge tiles. Flat roofed dormer windows added. Chimney stacks removed and original rainwater fittings replaced. Originally mainly double hung sash windows, now a number of alterations and added casements. Alterations to boundary wall adjacent to Lenton Road.
history: One of a pair of houses (with 45 Lenton Road) built on sloping site c1875.

name: Gladstone House :
desc: Alterations and additions include, small single storey extension and porch, late 19c/early 20c, plus flat roofed double garage building added in former outbuildings yard area. Coach house and stable buildings converted for residential use together with additional residential development c1965 on adjacent land originally forming part of house garden. Gladstone House now converted into two flats. Red brick with stone dressings and slate gabled roof. Two storeys plus attic and basement. Six panelled main entrance door on road front plus side and overlights with moulded and decorated stone surround and segmental pediment. Sash windows on road front in singles and pairs. A number of sashes have been altered. Moulded stone surrounds to windows with shaft mullions on window pairs. Moulded stone cornice plus string course at first floor level on all facades. Stone band course aligned with window heads at first floor level and repeated on rear garden facing facade. Decorative brickwork detail plus string course at gutter level on front and rear facades including dentillation and nailhead mouldings. Same brickwork detail on each gable forming broken pediment. Pair of two storey canted window bays on rear garden facade with moulded stone surrounds to plain sash windows and slate hipped roofs.Six panelled door between bays with moulded stone surround and steps. Above at first floor level, stone framed 6 leaded light window with mullions and transoms. Single storey, flat roofed, brick/stone, square window bay added later adjacent to rear doorway. Venetian window with stone surround to each shaped gable, flanked by pair of chimney stacks. ‘EL’ monogram and ‘1876’ datestone carved onto stonework head of Venetian windows. Stone copings and circular vents to shaped brick gables. A number of original sash windows altered and new windows added. Clay ridge tiles to double pitched slate roof. Pair of side wall brick chimney stacks at each gable with brick relief patterning and buff ‘louvre’ pots of varying size. Roof windows added. Brick and stone walls plus brick piers with stone caps to parts of boundary. Boundary wall enclosing former side yard area now rendered and painted.
history: Detached house built by Edwin Loverseed for himself 1876.

desc: Two storeys plus basement and attic storey. Red brick with ashlar dressings and hipped and gabled roof. Building currently being refurbished/ converted. Original single storey extension with hipped slate roof in former yard area adjacent to north facing facade, now extended. Flat roofed garage building added mid 20c adjacent to extension. Also on Clumber Road West, small altered single storey extension with later lean-to roof. Panelled main entrance door with plain overlight in round arched brick opening facing Maxtoke Road. Two storey canted door/window bay with steps on garden front. Elsewhere, mainly single window openings apart from pair with brick shaft mullion facing Clumber Road West. Later doorway with steps added on garden front plus new window openings with lightwells to basement. Segment arched heads to window openings generally with plain or moulded red brick voussoirs and stone sills, plus brick shaft detail at reveals to windows on entrance and garden fronts. Round arched brick opening with side shafts to original main door opening. Also blue brick banding at plinth level and decorative, lozenge patterned brick banding at storey height level. Moulded brick detail with dentillation at eaves and gable verges. Inset brick panels on lower chimney stacks. To right of main entrance, splayed brick corner with moulded corbel at first floor level. Plain stone sills with moulded brick subsills to window openings. A number of stone sills now painted. Hipped and gabled roof with gabled dormer window plus later dormer facing Clumber Road West. All original red brick chimney stacks taken down above roof level, apart from one surviving base of original side wall stack facing Maxtoke Road. Mainly plain sash windows to original openings with some replacements and additions. Prominent brick and stone boundary wall enclosing garden adjacent to Maxtoke Road and Cavendish Road West with coursed rubble stone plinth, blue brick banding and red brick upper section with saddleback copings. Original brick/stone gate piers to main entrance with stone copings. Part coursed rubble stone, part red brick boundary wall with ashlar copings and later railings adjacent to Clumber Road West.
history: Detached house c1870, possibly by architect T.C. Hine. Previously converted into flats.

desc: Red brick with ashlar dressings and slate hipped roof with small gable and gabled dormer. Single and two storey enlarged side extensions and alterations with hipped roofs. Garage at bottom of garden possibly added for Ernest Goddard c1911. Large polygonal corner bay overlooking garden. Corbelled brick banding with nailhead moulding mainly at eaves and gable verges. Brick dentillation detail added to banding at eaves in some areas. Blue brick patterning including band courses at storey height levels, also over pointed arched openings and to some gable verges. Brick voussoirs to pointed arch window openings and blind windows. Stonework details include shaped and moulded door and window surrounds, sill band on polygonal bay plus decorative vents and small window openings including quatrefoil and eight pointed star motifs. Stone copings to gabled dormer. Stonework now painted apart from copings to gabled dormer. Hipped, slate main roof with small gable on road front and shared gabled dormer overlooking garden. Clay hip and ornamental ridge tiles. Hipped slate roof to polygonal bay with clay hip tiles and iron finial. Red brick side wall and ridge chimney stacks with red and blue brick relief patterning. Mainly red clay roll top chimney pots, some with ventilator caps. Combination of sash windows, generally plain with glazing bar sashes on road front facing Newcastle Circus. A number of door and window alterations and additions. Prominent boundary wall adjacent to Clumber Crescent South, with coursed rubble stone plinth, blue brick stretchers plus red brick garden wall above with blue brick decorative banding and saddleback copings. Original red brick boundary wall to Newcastle Circus with blue brick decorative banding and saddleback copings. Original gated opening enlarged to provide car access.
history: One of a pair of houses (with 5 South Road) possibly by architects Hine & Son c1870-75. In single household use.

desc: Red brick with stone dressings and hipped slate roof. Two storeys plus attic and basement. Coursed rubble stone wall to exposed basement storey in yard area facing Clumber Road East. Substantial part of original garden area developed for separate residential use c1980. Original stable block in rear yard area converted for residential use. Projecting central bay to main entrance on Newcastle Circus front. Elaborate ashlar doorcase with round arched main entrance opening flanked by columns with panelled double doors and inner doors with overlight. Above, tripartite window flanked by panelled stone pilasters and above again, round arched window in shaped gable with finial at attic level. Round headed, glazing bar French windows in moulded ashlar surrounds on either side of main entrance doorway and above, first floor moulded ashlar band plus glazing bar sash windows in moulded ashlar surrounds. Coved plaster eaves. South facing facade has two storey, hipped roof canted window bay with glazing bar French window and sidelights plus 3 glazing bar sashes above. To right, two storey rectangular window bay with pair of round arched and square headed glazing bar sash windows at ground and first floor levels. Centrally, between bays, similar French window with sash window above. Large stone framed, mullioned and transomed stair window on north facing facade with leaded glazing.
